text: A cancer vaccine made just for you.
label: OBJ

text: mRNA is back and it's fighting melanoma
label: SUBJ

text: Connie Franciosi, 80, noticed a suspicious spot on her skin and was diagnosed with melanoma in 2020.
label: OBJ

text: "It was considered a late diagnosis," she says.
label: OBJ

text: The skin cancer can be difficult to treat if it's not detected and treated early, and it can spread to other parts of the body.
label: OBJ

text: There are approximately 112,000 melanomas diagnosed in the U.S. each year and about 8,500 deaths.
label: OBJ

text: After Franciosi had surgery to remove the melanoma, she was told she had a high risk of recurrence and was offered a spot in a clinical trial testing a new messenger RNA (mRNA) vaccine combined with Keytruda, an immunotherapy drug.
label: OBJ

text: At the time, mRNA technology was in the news because of the recently developed Moderna COVID-19 vaccine.
label: OBJ

text: This melanoma trial, which included 157 patients in Australia and the U.S., all of whom had surgery to remove their tumors, was set up to test whether the same mRNA technology could be used to create a personalized cancer vaccine, explains Dr. Janice Mehnert.
label: OBJ

text: Mehnert is a melanoma specialist and researcher at NYU Langone Health and senior author of a new paper published Monday analyzing the five-year results.
label: OBJ

text: "This is an incredibly interesting trial because the approach is just so unique," Mehnert says.
label: OBJ

text: "It is a personalized immunotherapy strategy," tailored to each patient's tumor.
label: OBJ

text: The results are striking.
label: SUBJ

text: After five years of follow-up, 68.8% of patients who received the combination therapy remained cancer-free, she says, compared with 49.1% of patients who received Keytruda alone, which amounts to a 49% reduction in risk.
label: OBJ

text: "That's pretty exciting," Mehnert says.
label: OBJ

text: In addition, 92% of patients who received the combination therapy were alive at the five-year mark, compared with 71% of those who only used Keytruda.
label: OBJ

text: "I think this is strong evidence that this therapy, when used in combination with immunotherapy, can demonstrably reduce the risk of dying from this disease," she says.
label: OBJ

text: The results of the study are being presented at the American Society of Clinical Oncology conference today and are published in the Journal of Clinical Oncology.
label: OBJ

text: Mehnert says that predicting when melanoma will recur is difficult.
label: OBJ

text: "Sometimes recurrence is easily treated with surgery or radiation, but sometimes it happens in the lungs, the liver or the brain," and then it can be challenging to treat, she says.
label: OBJ

text: That's why a preventive approach makes sense.
label: SUBJ

text: "We're trying to harness the power of the immune system early in a patient's disease course to optimize their outcomes," she says.
label: OBJ

text: Dr. Sarah Arron, a dermatologist and skin cancer surgeon in the San Francisco Bay Area who was not involved in the research, says the results are significant and demonstrate the potential of mRNA vaccines.
label: OBJ

text: "I think this is a landmark advance in how we treat these very advanced, high-risk melanomas," she says.
label: OBJ

text: Arron notes that while Americans became familiar with mRNA technology through the COVID-19 vaccine, this application is fundamentally different.
label: OBJ

text: "The COVID vaccine was the same RNA fragment given to everybody.
label: OBJ

text: Whereas in this case, the antigen itself is not one virus, it's [each] patient's tumor," she says.
label: OBJ

text: Since each person's melanoma can differ, each vaccine is tailored.
label: OBJ

text: "By matching this RNA treatment to the patient's melanoma, we can really boost the immunotherapy by driving it toward the patient's tumor."
label: SUBJ

text: How the combination works
label: OBJ

text: Cells in the immune system, known as T cells, are built to find and destroy threats like cancer, but cancer can be sneaky, putting up the equivalent of a "don't attack me" shield that hides them.
label: SUBJ

text: To simplify, Keytruda works by lifting that shield so T cells can see the cancer again.
label: OBJ

text: The personalized mRNA vaccine then amplifies the effect.
label: OBJ

text: Scientists sequence a patient's tumor to identify up to 34 unique molecular fingerprints called neoantigens and encode them into a custom vaccine.
label: OBJ

text: When it's injected, the vaccine trains T cells to recognize and target those specific neoantigens, giving the immune system a blueprint for attacking the cancer.
label: OBJ

text: So, each patient's tumor informs the vaccine that they will receive.
label: OBJ

text: In the trial, side effects were mild, including chills and minor pain at the injection site.
label: OBJ

text: Arron says that given the reservations and politicization of the COVID-19 vaccine, it's important to understand how this technology works.
label: OBJ

text: "This mRNA vaccine is designed towards boosting a tumor response," she says.
label: OBJ

text: And the results are "very encouraging" for the use of mRNA.
label: OBJ

text: Researchers are also studying whether mRNA vaccines can be used to prevent the recurrence of other cancers, including lung cancer. The next steps
label: OBJ

text: The next steps "The results are exciting," says Moderna's chief development officer, Dr. David Berman.
label: OBJ

text: The company developed the vaccine, which is called intismeran, and is collaborating with Merck, the maker of pembrolizumab, which is marketed under the trade name Keytruda.
label: OBJ

text: A Phase 3 trial is now underway, which includes nearly 1,000 patients.
label: OBJ

text: Once results are analyzed in the coming months, the goal is to seek FDA approval, Berman says.
label: OBJ

text: Berman has been working on melanoma for many years, before joining Moderna, so he says this feels like a momentous step forward.
label: OBJ

text: "The degree of benefit was incredible," Berman says, pointing to the significant reduction in risk of recurrence.
label: OBJ

text: For Connie Franciosi, the results speak for themselves. "I am cancer-free."
label: OBJ

text: "I am cancer-free." She says her scans have shown no recurrence. "Life is good."
label: OBJ

text: "Life is good." She recently celebrated her 80th birthday.
label: OBJ

text: Her days are packed with the things she loves, including tending her gardens, staying active with her local library, and gathering with friends and playing golf.
label: OBJ

text: "I have some good shots and some not-so-good shots, but I just enjoy the game," she says.
label: OBJ

text: "I have a very satisfying life."
label: OBJ

text: The UK will not have to pay the Rwandan government millions of pounds over a failed migrant deportation scheme set up by Boris Johnson’s administration, an international court has ruled.
label: OBJ

text: The east African nation had sued the current UK government for more than £100m, claiming it was owed after a breach of an agreement.
label: OBJ

text: But after a three-day hearing at The Hague’s permanent court of arbitration in the Netherlands, judges ruled that the UK was not liable for two years of outstanding costs from the scheme, which was shelved in 2024.
label: OBJ

text: Johnson, then prime minister, sealed a deal in 2022 with Kigali to send to Rwanda all asylum seekers arriving on UK shores after “dangerous or illegal journeys” in small boats or lorries.
label: OBJ

text: But the scheme hit legal and political obstacles from the start, with the UK supreme court eventually ruling it illegal.
label: OBJ

text: When Keir Starmer became British prime minister in July 2024, he declared the plan “dead and buried” on his first full day in office, dismissing it as a “gimmick”.
label: OBJ

text: During the case, lawyers representing the UK argued it was “entirely logical” the plan would be scrapped when Labour came into power after the 2024 general election and “simple common sense” that no further payments would be due.
label: OBJ

text: During the two years before the scheme was scrapped, only four people actually went to Rwanda, all voluntarily, according to the current UK government.
label: OBJ

text: About £290m has been paid to Rwanda, the UK government website says, but Kigali argued in its pre-hearing submissions to the court that two annual payments of £50m were still outstanding.
label: OBJ

text: According to legal papers, Rwanda asked the court to find the UK in breach of the agreement and demanded it pay all outstanding sums, as well as compensation.
label: OBJ

text: Lawyers for the UK denied it had breached parts of the deal and said: “Rwanda is not entitled to any of the forms of relief it seeks” when asking the court to dismiss the claims.
label: OBJ

text: Earlier this year, Downing Street defended the decision to scrap the previous Tory administration’s plan when it emerged Rwanda was taking the UK to court.
label: OBJ

text: Before the election, the Conservative government had already spent £700m on its policy, under which migrants who arrived in the UK by boat from France would be sent to Kigali in an attempt to deter Channel crossings.
label: OBJ

text: Emmanuel Ugirashebuja, Rwanda’s minister of justice and attorney general, previously told the court the country had incurred “significant costs” preparing for the partnership and the UK had “then sought to walk away from its legal obligations”.
label: OBJ

text: He also said the UK “did not do Rwanda a courtesy of informing it in advance” that it was scrapping the deal, and that leaders were “left to read about this development in the media”.
label: OBJ

text: In a document setting out Rwanda’s claim, Ugirashebuja asked the court to rule that the UK had breached the agreement and should pay about £100m in two instalments in 2024 and 2025, plus £6m in compensation and interest.
label: OBJ

text: Instead of compensation, he said Rwanda would accept a formal apology from the UK for failing to honour parts of the deal.
label: OBJ

text: The arbitration court, set up to settle contractual disputes between nations, rejected by majority a £50m claim for one year and unanimously rejected the same amount for the second.
label: OBJ

text: A UK government spokesperson said: “The UK robustly defended its position, and the tribunal has now ruled in favour of the UK on all grounds.”
label: OBJ

text: The two nations are already in dispute after the UK slashed aid to Rwanda, accusing it of supporting M23 rebels in the neighbouring Democratic Republic of the Congo.
label: OBJ

text: Israel Pushes Deeper Into Lebanon, Immigration Bill Stalled, Anti-Weaponization Fund

text: American aircraft fired on a number of Iranian sites over the weekend, according to the U.S. military, including on Qeshm Island in the Strait of Hormuz.
label: OBJ

text: Iran said this morning that it has responded by firing on a U.S. military base.
label: OBJ

text: Despite these volatile conditions, President Trump said on Truth Social this morning that "Iran really wants to make a deal," and told Americans to "just sit back and relax, it will all work out well in the end."

text: Congress returns to Washington with a long to-do list.
label: OBJ

text: Lawmakers left for a Memorial Day break without passing a plan to fund immigration enforcement for the rest of Trump's term.
label: OBJ

text: The funding measure is stuck because of concerns about the Trump administration's push for an anti-weaponization fund.
label: OBJ

text: The administration says the fund would allow the DOJ to pay people who claim they were targeted by the federal government.
label: OBJ

text: Critics are concerned it could potentially provide cash payments to Jan. 6 insurrectionists.
label: OBJ

text: Trump's anti-weaponization fund isn't just getting pushback from Congress, but also from the courts.
label: OBJ

text: The nearly $1.8 billion fund was established as part of a settlement stemming from Trump's lawsuit against the IRS over the leak of his tax returns.
label: OBJ

text: U.S. District Judge Kathleen Williams, who initially dismissed the lawsuit after the settlement, now says she will review the case.
label: OBJ

text: A group of 35 retired federal judges filed a motion last week saying that Trump's lawsuit against the IRS was "itself a fraud on the court."
label: OBJ

text: Because the IRS is an agency the president oversees as the leader of the executive branch, the judges say that Trump was, in a sense, both the plaintiff and the defendant in the case.
label: OBJ

text: Judge Williams has given Trump's lawyers until June 12 to respond to the motion.
label: OBJ

text: The DOJ has been temporarily blocked from establishing the fund following a Virginia judge's ruling.
label: OBJ

text: Graham Platner seemingly emerged out of nowhere to become the presumptive Democratic nominee for the Senate seat in Maine.
label: SUBJ

text: But his campaign has been dogged by controversies, including racist comments and remarks blaming sexual assault on victims in old, deleted Reddit posts.
label: OBJ

text: He has covered a tattoo resembling a Nazi symbol.
label: OBJ

text: And, most recently, The Wall Street Journal reported that Platner exchanged sexually explicit messages with multiple women early in his marriage.
label: OBJ

text: The incidents raise a big question: Does Platner have too much baggage to carry on?
label: SUBJ

text: Or can the anti-establishment political message that has generated much enthusiasm among his Democratic base carry him through?
label: SUBJ

text: In a conversation with Leila Fadel for [OUTLET]'s Newsmakers, Platner addresses some of his controversies, the failures of his own party and his criticism of Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth.
label: OBJ

text: The interview was recorded before news broke of the explicit sexual messages.
label: OBJ

text: Watch or listen to the interview or read the article about their discussion.

text: Kenya's Lake Turkana is the world's largest permanent desert lake.
label: OBJ

text: Its waters have long sustained hundreds of thousands of people in one of the most isolated and neglected parts of the country.
label: SUBJ

text: But the lake now faces multiple threats.
label: OBJ

text: Rising water levels have displaced thousands, damaged infrastructure and disrupted fishing, while persistent drought has pushed many herders into fishing, further straining an already delicate ecosystem.
label: OBJ

text: See photos from villages near the lake and read stories from residents about how life has changed.
label: OBJ

text: MS NOW anchor Ali Velshi cited the country’s “so-called democracy” and its “unresolved racial politics” as reasons for him not fully buying into the country’s 250th birthday celebration.
label: OBJ

text: (Watch the video below.)
label: OBJ

text: “Anniversaries are imperfect records of the thing which is being celebrated,” he said on his show Sunday.
label: OBJ

text: “In America’s case, anniversaries often gloss over the racial dynamics underlying much of America’s history and politics, issues that remain unsolved, because America has never actually fully reckoned with its racist past and its original founding sin of slavery.”
label: SUBJ

text: He continued, “In one month, America will mark the 250th anniversary of its founding.
label: OBJ

text: Like previous anniversaries, there is a deep unease about this.
label: SUBJ

text: I feel a deep unease about the celebrations to which I am invited to mark the 250th anniversary of our so-called democracy.”
label: SUBJ

text: Pointing to fundamental unrest fomented by racism, Velshi added:
label: OBJ

text: “Women and Black Americans have seen their rights taken away.
label: OBJ

text: The Voting Rights Act has effectively been gutted.
label: SUBJ

text: A number of states are continuing to gerrymander their congressional maps ahead of the midterm elections, with the explicit effect of taking away political power from Black Americans.”
label: SUBJ

text: Velshi, who was born in Kenya, raised in Canada, and became a U.S. citizen in 2015, empathized with other Americans who may have been hesitant to fully enjoy the festivities.
label: OBJ

text: “If you’ve got conflicting feelings about America’s upcoming anniversary — like you want to celebrate the ideals that America strives for, which are noble and should be celebrated, but lament the state of the country — you’re not alone.
label: SUBJ

text: At least I’m with you on this.”
label: OBJ

text: The heartfelt words of Velshi were unsurprisingly met by racism online.
label: SUBJ

text: “Devastating” wildfires ripped across the wealthier parts of the world in 2025, a study has found, even as globally, the area ravaged by flames fell.
label: OBJ

text: Catastrophic blazes claimed lives, homes and jobs last year in California, Canada, Europe and South Korea.
label: SUBJ

text: But the 335m hectares burned was the second-lowest since 2002, the review found, largely owing to the expansion of African farms that have fragmented landscapes and hampered the spread of large savannah fires.
label: OBJ

text: The disasters in 2025 included a Scottish “megafire” that torched more than 100,000 hectares – contributing to the UK breaking its record for burned area – and the Palisades and Eaton fires in Los Angeles, which were among the most destructive in US history.
label: OBJ

text: Record-breaking blazes in Spain and Portugal burned more than half a million hectares, while South Korea had its biggest and deadliest wildfire season on record.
label: OBJ

text: Fires accounted for more than 38% of insured losses from weather disasters in 2025, the study found.
label: OBJ

text: “2025 shows that a ‘quiet’ fire year globally can still be devastating,” said Matthew Jones, a climate scientist at the University of East Anglia and lead author of the study.
label: OBJ

text: “We are seeing a growing disconnect between total area burned and real-world impacts.”
label: OBJ

text: Changes in land use mean wildfires burn less of the planet than they have historically done, but global heating is creating conditions allowing them to spread, increasing the danger at what researchers call the wildland-urban interface, where people are most at risk.
label: OBJ

text: Adverse weather, inflamed by carbon pollution, turned some of last year’s fires into explosive infernos.
label: SUBJ

text: In southern California and South Korea, the researchers found, high winds and dry vegetation pushed fires through densely populated areas, causing “exceptional mortality, mass evacuations, and major infrastructure losses”.
label: OBJ

text: In the Mediterranean, meanwhile, drought and extreme heat drove severe blazes, from Portugal to Turkey.
label: OBJ

text: “These conditions do not cause the fires, but in the event of a fire, we have material that is more flammable than usual – because it is drier – and wind conditions that fan the flames,” said David Garcia, an applied mathematician at the University of Alicante, who was not involved in the study.
label: OBJ

text: “This makes large fires more likely to occur.”
label: OBJ

text: An attribution study Garcia co-authored last year found the extreme weather fuelling the flames in Portugal and Spain last year was made 39 times more likely by climate breakdown.
label: OBJ

text: “If we continue to warm the planet, large-scale fires will continue to increase,” he said.
label: OBJ

text: The overall reduction in global burned area led to a drop in carbon dioxide emissions to their third-lowest level on record.
label: OBJ

text: In Canada, though, extreme wildfire emissions were recorded for the third year in a row.
label: OBJ

text: Since 2023, boreal forests in North America have emitted close to 4bn tonnes of CO2, exceeding the total emissions of the preceding 15-year period.
label: OBJ

text: As well as heating the planet, the pollutants in wildfire smoke lead to huge numbers of people dying from breathing dirty air.
label: SUBJ

text: The toxic particles spewed by Canadian wildfires in 2023 killed 82,000 people, according to a study published in September, with smoke even choking cities in the US, Europe and Africa.
label: OBJ

text: Adrián Regos, a landscape ecologist at the Biological Mission of Galicia, Spain, who was not involved in the study, said last year’s events illustrated how a relatively small number of extreme fires could dominate the ecological, social and economic consequences of an entire fire season.
label: OBJ

text: “The broader pattern highlighted by this study is consistent with what we are observing across southern Europe: while total burned area may fluctuate from year to year, climate change is increasing the likelihood of extreme fire-weather conditions, and fuel accumulation associated with rural abandonment is making many landscapes more vulnerable to large, fast-moving fires,” he said.
label: OBJ

text: “The challenge is therefore not only reducing the number of fires, but increasing the resilience of landscapes and communities to extreme events.”
label: OBJ

text: Confirmed Ebola cases in Congo reach 282 as survivors describe their recoveries
label: OBJ

text: BUNIA, Congo — At least 282 confirmed cases of Ebola have now been reported in Congo's ongoing outbreak, the central African nation said late Sunday, as patients who recovered from the disease spoke of their indescribable joy in interviews with The Associated Press.
label: OBJ

text: The outbreak remains focused in Congo's eastern Ituri province, where 264 of the cases have been recorded, Congo's Ministry of Health said.
label: OBJ

text: Congo has reported over 1,000 suspected cases with the Bundibugyo virus, the current species of Ebola, which has no approved treatment or vaccine.
label: OBJ

text: According to the health ministry, the main challenges in containing the outbreak include early detection and rapid isolation of cases, rigorous contact tracing, safe and dignified burials and strengthening infection prevention and control in health facilities.
label: OBJ

text: The contact tracing coverage rate so far is 45% with 220 suspected cases under investigation, the ministry said.
label: OBJ

text: Meanwhile, some of the five people who have so far recovered from the disease spoke of their relief in interviews with The Associated Press.
label: OBJ

text: Baraka Bulambulu, a nurse, said he was overjoyed after the last two Ebola tests on him returned negative.
label: OBJ

text: Bulambulu was among those presented certificates of recovery by the World Health Organization Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus as he opened a new Ebola treatment center in Bunia, the capital of Ituri province, on Sunday.
label: OBJ

text: "The first one came back positive, but the second and third were negative for me," Bulambulu said with a wide grin.
label: OBJ

text: "Coming out of this illness alive is an indescribable joy."
label: OBJ

text: Ezo Étienne, another nurse who recovered from the disease, said he first started feeling dizzy during ward rounds at the hospital as he checked on patients.
label: OBJ

text: "That was how it started," he recalled.
label: OBJ

text: "I called the team and told them, 'Something's wrong here.'
label: OBJ

text: I checked my blood pressure and saw that I was immediately experiencing hypotension (low blood pressure).
label: OBJ

text: I decided to rest for a bit, and a few minutes later I started vomiting."
label: OBJ

text: The WHO has said all five survivors are health workers — four nurses and a laboratory worker — a group most affected in the outbreak.
label: OBJ

text: Treatments so far have mostly targeted patients' symptoms, the organization has said.
label: OBJ

text: "Your courage gives hope and your living story, that this outbreak can be stopped," Tedros told the health workers on Sunday.
label: OBJ

text: Neighboring Uganda has also reported nine cases of Ebola and closed its border with Congo seeking to limit its spread.
label: OBJ

text: Although more than 20 Ebola outbreaks have taken place in Congo and Uganda, the Bundibugyo virus has been rare.
label: OBJ

text: The lack of approved vaccines and treatments, as well as the remote locations and armed violence in hot spots, have made the current response challenging.
label: OBJ

text: Despite the challenges, the recoveries are "a victory worth celebrating," said Dr. Dieudonne Mwamba Kazadi, the director-general of Congo's National Institute of Public Health.
label: OBJ

text: "It's a strong message that it is possible to recover from Ebola when seeking care early in a dedicated health facility," he added.
label: OBJ

text: When you ask Adam Hamawy why he’s running for Congress, he sounds like a lot of other progressives.
label: OBJ

text: He goes over his experience as a doctor, squabbling with insurance companies and seeing the cost of care rise.
label: OBJ

text: He mentions his time in the Army National Guard, serving as a combat trauma surgeon, and his time volunteering in war zones around the world.
label: OBJ

text: “I’ve seen where we’re spending our money,” he told [OUTLET] in an interview.
label: OBJ

text: “We’re told that we can’t afford Medicare For All.
label: OBJ

text: But we always find money for bombs.”
label: SUBJ

text: But it doesn’t take long to get to the experience that helped put him on a more direct path to serving in Congress: His stint as a trauma doctor in Gaza during Israel’s assault on the region, when Israel’s seizure of a nearby border crossing left him and his colleagues trapped for a week at their hospital.
label: OBJ

text: When he returned home, he felt an obligation to speak out about what he felt was a genocide: He traveled to Washington, but found many members of Congress did not want to hear what he had to say.
label: OBJ

text: So when his home district congresswoman, the progressive Rep. Bonnie Watson Coleman (D-N.J.), announced her retirement last year, Hamawy decided he had to run.
label: OBJ

text: “I’ve seen the horrors of war.
label: OBJ

text: When I went to Palestine, that was not a war,” said Hamawy.
label: OBJ

text: ”You can only do so much as an advocate; you can make real change as a congressman.”
label: OBJ

text: Hamawy is now the frontrunner in the Democratic primary to replace the retiring Coleman in the state’s 12th district, a solidly blue seat stretching from urban Trenton to wealthy areas surrounding Princeton University.
label: SUBJ

text: While pro-Palestine progressives have won other Democratic primaries this cycle, Hamawy’s victory would be a landmark – a victory for a candidate whose stance on Gaza was the basis for his campaign.
label: SUBJ

text: “They’re finally being seen,” said Rep. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.) when asked what a potential Hamawy victory would mean for the pro-Palestine movement.
label: OBJ

text: “They’re finally being heard after decades of being ignored.”
label: OBJ

text: His potential triumph, however, has elicited howls of protest from pro-Israel organizations, who have attempted to argue that Hamawy has unacceptable ties to Muslim radicals, with Sen. Tim Sheehy (R-Mont.) going as far as to label Hamawy an “actual terrorist.”
label: OBJ

text: The attacks have two prongs: A Wall Street Journal op-ed by former Attorney General Michael Mukasey laid out his ties to the so-called “Blind Sheikh,” who was convicted of seditious conspiracy and sentenced to life in prison following an investigation of the 1995 World Trade Center bombing; and a Jewish Insider story last week noting Hamawy volunteered with a medical charity in the Balkans that was accused years later of being a front group for Al-Qaeda.
label: OBJ

text: Hamawy has condemned the attacks as smears.
label: OBJ

text: He has disavowed the blind cleric’s views, and the charity Hamawy worked for was widely praised – including by President Bill Clinton’s administration – in the years before their exposure.
label: OBJ

text: “Attacks calling Muslims terrorists is not a new thing, and they’re just falling back to their old playbook,” Hamawy told [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: “So I’m just going to continue talking about everything that I have done for this country in uniform and out of uniform.”
label: OBJ

text: Hamawy’s victory is not guaranteed.
label: SUBJ

text: His stiffest challenger is probably Sue Altman, a former director of the New Jersey Working Families Party who narrowly lost a challenge to Rep. Tom Kean (R-N.J.) last cycle.
label: SUBJ

text: But while many of the major interest groups intervening in Democratic primaries, including AIPAC, looked at spending in the race, the establishment – including the powerful South Jersey-based political machine run by the Norcross family – failed to consolidate around a candidate.
label: OBJ

text: “We know AIPAC, Crypto, and AI wanted to buy this seat and were interviewing candidates, but while they couldn’t choose which corporate shill to back, the left united behind a political outsider with a vision that spoke to the needs and priorities of Jersey voters,” said Usamah Andrabi, the communications director for the progressive group Justice Democrats, which endorsed Hamawy.
label: OBJ

text: Andrabi noted AIPAC’s attempted intervention in an early New Jersey special election similarly ended up backfiring: “AIPAC’s cycle so far in New Jersey is one of the best representations of how much they flounder and fail when faced with an electorate who simply knows who they are and therefore, knows better.”
label: OBJ

text: It’s hard to overstate the central role the pro-Palestine movement has played in lifting Hamawy’s campaign: Small donors from around the country, many of them Muslim, were key to giving him a fundraising lead early in the race.
label: SUBJ

text: And a super PAC dedicated to countering AIPAC’s influence, American Priorities, has spent $2 million on ads boosting his candidacy.
label: OBJ

text: There’s good reason for the movement to rally behind him.
label: SUBJ

text: The broad strokes of his resume – Sen. Tammy Duckworth (D-Ill.) credits him with saving her life after her insurgents shot down her Blackhawk helicopter in Iraq – would make him a star recruit for many movements and organizations.
label: SUBJ

text: “This is the resume of a candidate the DCCC would love to recruit,” said Amira Hassan, the political director of PAL PAC, another pro-Palestine group.
label: OBJ

text: “This is the perfect candidate.”
label: OBJ

text: Containing the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo requires community cooperation and is “everybody’s business”, the World Health Organization has said.
label: OBJ

text: Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, the organisation’s director general, made the plea on Sunday during a visit to eastern Congo where some residents have protested against stringent medical protocols for handling victims’ bodies.
label: OBJ

text: “We can stop this Ebola and anyone who has it can also recover.
label: OBJ

text: But the rule … is this thing is everybody’s business and every citizen should be involved,” Ghebreyesus said at the opening of a treatment centre in Bunia, the capital of Ituri province, which is at the centre of the outbreak.
label: OBJ

text: Protesters have complained restrictions on handling victims’ bodies violate local burial rites, a sentiment that has been linked to at least three attacks against health centres.
label: OBJ

text: There is no vaccine for the Bundibugyo virus, the strain behind the current outbreak, but infected people can recover, according to Ghebreyesus.
label: OBJ

text: He said: “If you come to health facilities when you have symptoms, you can get the support and recover, so the key is to come forward as early as possible and to get the necessary support.”
label: OBJ

text: 'At what point does it make sense to ditch a gas car for an electric vehicle?'
label: SUBJ

text: PHOENIX – Rising gasoline prices have some Americans thinking about buying an electric vehicle.
label: OBJ

text: It's a big financial decision, especially since Republicans ended federal subsidies last year worth up to $7,500.
label: OBJ

text: Guadalupe Higuera, 30, of Phoenix, Ariz., bought his Chevrolet Equinox EV before that incentive ended.
label: OBJ

text: But he still wonders if it was a smart choice.
label: OBJ

text: Higuera responded to [OUTLET]'s request for questions about reducing your climate impact and saving money.
label: OBJ

text: "At what point does it make sense to ditch a gas car for an EV?" he asked.
label: OBJ

text: "Does it make sense to replace it at a certain age or mileage?
label: SUBJ

text: Or do we just drive it until the wheels fall off?"
label: SUBJ

text: Higuera says his question is motivated by both saving money and reducing his contribution to the greenhouse gases that are warming the climate.
label: OBJ

text: After investigating his question, the answer, as far as climate pollution is concerned, is clear: it makes sense to switch to an EV now.
label: SUBJ

text: On saving money, the answer is more complicated.
label: OBJ

text: But Higuera concludes that switching to an EV was a good financial choice, too.
label: OBJ

text: "Drive it until the wheels fall off"
label: OBJ

text: One reason Higuera questioned his decision was that nothing was wrong with his previous car, a 2016 Jeep Wrangler.
label: OBJ

text: And his family has owned an auto repair shop, north of downtown Phoenix, since before he was born.
label: OBJ

text: So, he grew up with the idea that it's wasteful to get rid of a car that still runs fine.
label: OBJ

text: "I remember having that conversation with my parents [and] my older brother, before I got my current car," Higuera says.
label: OBJ

text: "And they're like, 'your car — we can keep fixing it. It's still good.
label: OBJ

text: It's still good. There's nothing wrong with it.'"
label: SUBJ

text: Americans are keeping their vehicles longer.
label: OBJ

text: The average age of cars and light trucks on the road increased to 12.8 years, according to the U.S. Bureau of Transportation Statistics.
label: OBJ

text: Often, a big factor in deciding whether to get a new car is repair costs, according to AAA.
label: OBJ

text: And not everyone has the benefit of getting the family discount for repairs, as Higuera does.
label: SUBJ

text: There are lessons for everyone considering an EV in answering Higuera's question.
label: OBJ

text: We set out to compare the costs of keeping his Jeep with buying his EV.
label: OBJ

text: And we used a tool that calculated typical repair costs (not his family discount), so the comparison can be useful to others.
label: OBJ

text: When an EV saves money
label: OBJ

text: Comparing the cost of a 10-year-old Jeep to a year-old Chevy EV gets complicated and involves more than just the cost of fuel.
label: OBJ

text: For example, while maintenance is cheaper for a newer EV, insurance costs are higher because they include parts, such as batteries, that are expensive to replace.
label: OBJ

text: There are some websites to figure out costs for new cars, such as Consumer Reports and Edmunds.
label: OBJ

text: You can also find helpful calculators with specific information, such as this fuel-savings calculator that Oakland, Calif. resident Andrew Krulewitz developed for a car dealership that sells a lot of EVs.
label: OBJ

text: It allows you to adjust the number of miles driven each year, electricity costs, gas prices and vehicle efficiency.
label: OBJ

text: To compare a more complete list of the costs for Higuera's 2016 Jeep and his 2025 Chevy EV, [OUTLET] Cars and Energy Correspondent Camila Domonoske chose a federal Energy Department calculator.
label: OBJ

text: It includes fuel, tires, maintenance, registration, license, insurance and a loan payment.
label: OBJ

text: Working with Higuera, she started with a $0 cost for his Jeep, since he already owned it.
label: OBJ

text: For the EV, she entered $23,000 ($45,500 purchase price, minus the $7,500 tax credit and $15,000 from selling the Jeep).
label: OBJ

text: The calculator figured his electricity cost based on the state where he lives, Arizona.
label: OBJ

text: Then Higuera and Domonoske entered the gas price in Maricopa County from AAA.
label: OBJ

text: In early May, when they made these calculations, that was $4.95 a gallon.
label: OBJ

text: The price has gone down a little since then.
label: OBJ

text: Higuera says he drove 21,300 miles last year — that's nearly 9,000 miles more than an average driver.
label: OBJ

text: His software developer job at a big bank is about 35 miles away and his girlfriend lives on the other side of the Phoenix metro area.
label: OBJ

text: The tool allows you to estimate what percentage is more efficient, highway driving or city driving — 40% city and 60% highway, in Higuera's case.
label: OBJ

text: With all the information entered, the costs of owning and operating both vehicles pops up on the screen.
label: OBJ

text: "It's saying $10,456 for your cost of ownership on an Equinox in year one, versus $8,000 to have continued to own the Jeep."
label: OBJ

text: Domonoske tells Higuera over a video call.
label: OBJ

text: But after that first year the two lines on the graph narrow.
label: OBJ

text: After five years the Equinox, cumulatively, costs just $1,000 more, she says, "and then from there on, it's cheaper to own the Equinox."
label: OBJ

text: "This makes me feel a little bit happier, financially-wise, because I didn't really expect that the cost would be pretty much exactly the same, and then it gets cheaper down the line," Higuera says.
label: OBJ

text: A few factors make Higuera's calculation work out financially, including his ability to take advantage of the $7,500 tax credit that no longer exists.
label: OBJ

text: Also, he drives so many miles each year, and his Jeep's gas mileage averaged only around 20 miles per gallon.
label: OBJ

text: "This would look really different if your 2016 vehicle had been a Prius," Domonoske tells Higuera.
label: OBJ

text: That Prius averages 52 miles per gallon, so gas costs would be less than half the Jeep's.
label: OBJ

text: One expense this tool doesn't consider is depreciation.
label: OBJ

text: After five years, the Equinox, as a newer vehicle, would drop in value faster than the already 10-year-old Jeep.
label: SUBJ

text: Jeremy Michalek, director of the Carnegie Mellon University Vehicle Electrification Group, performed some calculations that included a scenario in which both cars were sold in five years to factor in depreciation.
label: OBJ

text: He says there are a lot of uncertainties with making such calculations, but Michalek suspects even with the extra depreciation, Higuera saves money with the Equinox.
label: OBJ

text: There are benefits in driving a new vehicle too, like fewer repairs.
label: SUBJ

text: "I'd say the Equinox route is likely the better value for Guadalupe," says Michalek, who is also a professor of engineering and public policy at CMU.
label: OBJ

text: He says that because climate change also figured into Higuera's decision, the Equinox EV makes even more sense for him.
label: OBJ

text: EVs are a climate solution
label: SUBJ

text: The Energy Department tool that Domonoske and Higuera used to determine his cost savings also considers where the electricity his EV uses comes from — and more than half of Arizona's electricity is generated by burning gas and coal.
label: OBJ

text: Even though so much of the electricity comes from burning fossil fuels, the tool shows Higuera is still cutting his carbon dioxide emissions 80% by ditching his Jeep for the Equinox EV.
label: OBJ

text: Part of the reason is that EVs waste less energy.
label: OBJ

text: While internal combustion engine cars put less than 25% of the energy in gasoline to use, EVs use about 90% of the energy in electricity to turn the car's wheels.
label: OBJ

text: But Higuera says he's also heard EVs have more pollution associated with manufacturing.
label: OBJ

text: "It is true that manufacturing electric vehicles involves higher emissions intensity than manufacturing gasoline vehicles," Michalek says.
label: OBJ

text: "But you can earn that back pretty quickly by using electricity instead of gasoline."
label: OBJ

text: So, just as EVs cost more up front and save money in the long run, they emit more pollution up front and much less over time.
label: OBJ

text: How long that takes depends on the EV and the energy mix in the region where you live.
label: OBJ

text: "Because the power grid in some areas of the country, like the northern Midwest, are still quite dirty — very coal heavy.
label: SUBJ

text: And some, like the West Coast, are very clean," Michalek says.
label: OBJ

text: "How long it takes to drive your electric vehicle to make up the manufacturing emissions is pretty short in California and much longer in Wisconsin."
label: OBJ

text: Higuera says he usually charges his EV at night, when his local utility rates are the cheapest.
label: OBJ

text: That raises another concern for him.
label: OBJ

text: "Where I live, we have a lot of solar — we have a lot of sun — but the sun is not up when it's dark out," Higuera says.
label: OBJ

text: He worries that's encouraging his utility to burn more natural gas to generate the electricity that feeds into his EV.
label: OBJ

text: Michalek and his colleagues have researched this and found that charging when rates are cheapest encourages utilities to build more of the cheapest forms of electricity generation, which also happen to be the cleanest.
label: OBJ

text: "You actually create incentives to build a ton of wind and solar, possibly so much wind and solar that the net effect of charging your electric vehicle can be to reduce the total emissions from the power grid," Michalek says.
label: OBJ

text: Ditching gas for electric makes sense
label: SUBJ

text: Sorting out questions like these are complicated because switching to an EV touches on the two largest sources of greenhouse gas emissions: transportation and electricity generation.
label: OBJ

text: That can make it difficult for researchers who dwell in the details, like Michalek, to offer a simple answer to questions.
label: OBJ

text: But after years of research, he's comfortable making a declarative statement about EVs.
label: OBJ

text: "By and large, if you switch to an electric vehicle, there are lower emissions associated with it, even today, and they'll only get cleaner as the grid gets cleaner," Michalek says.
label: OBJ

text: And, he says, EVs are more cost-competitive too.
label: OBJ

text: "That wasn't true when I started working on this, but now you pay more for the vehicle up front, you save money on fuel, and you can often make up the costs," he says — how long that takes depends on the EV you buy and what incentives are available.
label: OBJ

text: A recent study by researchers at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology shows that across most of the U.S., electric vehicles are cost-competitive with their gas counterparts.
label: OBJ

text: And it found that in most locations, EVs also reduce emissions between 40% and 60%.
label: OBJ

text: Our research leaves Higuera believing that it did make sense to ditch his gas Jeep for an EV – both financially and for reducing climate pollution.
label: SUBJ

text: "Even though I'm just one person, I'm able to do something about it, which is as simple as just switching my vehicle," Higuera says.
label: OBJ

text: The far-right lawyer Abelardo de la Espriella and the leftwing senator Iván Cepeda have just under three weeks to compete for the roughly 3.6m votes that did not go to either of them in the first round of Colombia’s presidential election.
label: OBJ

text: That is no insignificant number, given that De la Espriella’s lead over Cepeda amounted to little more than 670,000 votes – 43.7% to 40.9%.
label: SUBJ

text: Although polls had shown the wealthy lawyer gaining ground, they had also consistently indicated a solid lead for the senator, who is backed by the leftwing president, Gustavo Petro.
label: OBJ

text: This made De la Espriella’s first-round victory on Sunday a surprise to most Colombian analysts and politicians.
label: OBJ

text: An admirer of Donald Trump and other far-right leaders in the region, he campaigned amid a string of controversies and with a promise to end, within 90 days, Colombia’s decades-long armed conflict which has claimed nearly half a million lives.
label: SUBJ

text: His lead on Sunday is being interpreted as a sign that the radical right has overtaken Colombia’s traditional conservative forces, reflected in the collapse of the candidacy of the rightwing senator Paloma Valencia.
label: SUBJ

text: A loyal follower of the former president Álvaro Uribe Vélez, who governed from 2002 to 2010, Valencia spent months in second place in the polls but lost momentum in the final weeks and finished with just 6.9% of the vote.
label: OBJ

text: “What really helped De la Espriella was Valencia’s collapse,” said the political scientist Yan Basset.

text: Both analysts described the lawyer’s first-round victory as “surprising” and said they believed the left faced a difficult, though not impossible, task in overturning the result before the runoff on 21 June.

text: Is bovine colostrum really 'liquid gold' for gut health?
label: SUBJ

text: Known as "liquid gold," colostrum is the first milk that mammals produce after giving birth.
label: OBJ

text: And it's chock full of antibodies, proteins, vitamins and minerals that help build up a newborn's immunity and strengthen their gut lining.
label: OBJ

text: But this substance that's uniquely tailored for newborns is also receiving plenty of attention from adults who've taken to chugging bovine colostrum.
label: OBJ

text: Cows make more than their calves need so excess colostrum is collected, pasteurized, dried, and packaged for human consumption.
label: OBJ

text: Wellness influencers and social media personalities are talking up bovine colostrum as a must-have supplement to support gut health among a range of other benefits.
label: OBJ

text: Among the most common assertions about colostrum is its ability to improve a range of gastrointestinal woes.
label: OBJ

text: Influencers also suggest it can help boost the immune system, and help with issues as diverse as skin tone, hair growth and low energy.
label: OBJ

text: In addition to animal research, there are a handful of preliminary studies in humans suggesting colostrum may improve certain forms of inflammatory bowel syndrome, gastroenteritis, and upper respiratory tract infections, as well as stunting in children.
label: OBJ

text: But some caution that it's too early to say definitely that it's effective.
label: OBJ

text: "I just don't think that we know enough yet," says Elyce Shapiro, a Chicago-based licensed dietician nutritionist who runs a virtual private practice.
label: OBJ

text: "From a safety profile, I don't have a problem with it," she notes.
label: OBJ

text: "I think it has one of the lower risks of some of the other products out there."
label: OBJ

text: Shapiro has seen the studies showing that colostrum can help treat ailments of the gut, but she says that they've tended to focus on particular populations in the short-term, including elite athletes or those contending with a specific medical condition.
label: OBJ

text: So it's unclear how broadly effective colostrum may be for everyday gut troubles, like bloating or constipation.
label: OBJ

text: Patients are "looking for solutions," she says, "but typically your solution isn't just sitting in a supplement."
label: OBJ

text: Still, Raymond Playford, a gastroenterologist at the University of West London and medical director of PanTheryx, a company that produces and distributes colostrum, describes the research as promising.
label: OBJ

text: Playford has been studying the supplement and its potential health benefits for more than 30 years.
label: OBJ

text: He sees a possible mechanism for it to help the mucous lining of the intestines, which can become overly permeable.
label: OBJ

text: "It helps stabilize the gut, strengthen it, stop it being leaky going forward, sealing it," he says.
label: OBJ

text: Playford coauthored a few papers showing benefit from the supplement, including a small pilot study reporting that colostrum reduces some of the GI side effects of GLP-1 drugs like Ozempic, such as acid reflux and bloating.
label: OBJ

text: "In humans," he argues, "we can definitely say that it does have an effect on the human gut."
label: OBJ

text: While he agrees the early data show promise, Rick Bloomer, a nutrition scientist and dean of the College of Health Sciences at the University of Memphis, says a lot of the marketing is "overblown."
label: OBJ

text: He also notes that it may be hard for consumers to discern how best to apply the findings of colostrum studies to themselves.
label: OBJ

text: For instance, the quantity administered by researchers can be higher than what's recommended on a typical supplement bottle.
label: OBJ

text: "The consumer doesn't know what the dosing needs to be," he says.
label: OBJ

text: When it comes to tending to gut health, Shapiro and Bloomer recommend focusing on improving one's overall lifestyle first.
label: OBJ

text: Bloomer emphasizes the importance of regular physical activity, a wholesome diet, and sufficient sleep.
label: OBJ

text: "Those things we know are most important when it comes to gut health, immune function, et cetera," he says.
label: OBJ

text: "To think that I can use a supplement but ignore those other factors is a big mistake."
label: OBJ

text: Shapiro notes that lifestyle factors also include managing stress levels.
label: OBJ

text: "Stress, it's like a battering ram to your gut barrier lining," Shapiro explains.
label: OBJ

text: "So if you have high stress levels, what do we do to help address that stress?
label: OBJ

text: We can supplement all we want, but that's not going to get to the underlying root cause of what's going on here."
label: SUBJ

text: If lifestyle improvements don't work, Bloomer says it might be worth introducing a colostrum supplement.
label: OBJ

text: But it should be done consistently over a period of two months without changing anything else, "just like a scientific experiment in a lab," he says.
label: OBJ

text: "Otherwise, we really don't know — is it the intervention or is it something else they're doing that's leading to that outcome?"
label: SUBJ

text: Bloomer says that if the colostrum does work, great.
label: OBJ

text: But he generally advises that the money one spends on supplements — which varies but can be as high as $1-$2 per daily dose for colostrum — may be better spent on healthy foods.
label: OBJ

text: Incorporating fruits, vegetables and whole grains often have a broader positive impact on the body.
label: OBJ

text: "I think [it's] really important for people to consider the cost-benefit ratio," he says.
label: OBJ

text: One other caution: though there's little evidence of serious side effects from colostrum, Playford warns those who are lactose intolerant to steer clear.
label: OBJ

text: "If you suffer from a milk allergy," he says, "then you shouldn't do it.
label: OBJ

text: But if you can take milk, you can take colostrum."
label: OBJ

text: The research on colostrum points to potential benefits for gut health, but the research is still in its early stages.
label: OBJ

text: It shouldn't be regarded as a quick cure all, and doctors say lifestyle changes are a better first stop for trying to improve gut troubles.
label: OBJ

text: Colombian Voters Are Weighing Peace Deals Or A Crackdown
label: OBJ

text: Voters across Latin America are increasingly ditching leaders that pitched progressive policies aimed at addressing the root issues of conflict, such as lack of opportunities for young people and corruption.
label: SUBJ

text: Instead, voters have increasingly turned to candidates promising heavy-handed security crackdowns.
label: OBJ

text: The polarized vote comes as the Trump administration is playing a more aggressive role in Latin America than any U.S. government in decades, placing mounting pressure on countries like Colombia, Mexico, and Ecuador to crack down on crime.
label: SUBJ

text: The election has also underscored two sharply diverging visions for the future of peace in a country marked by years of conflict.
label: SUBJ

text: On one side, Cepeda has promised to continue Petro’s progressive agenda and a largely failed effort to negotiate peace pacts with armed groups, following a plan that’s likely to sharply contrast with Trump’s vision for Latin America.
label: SUBJ

text: On the other side, de la Espriella has promised to fiercely crack down on criminal groups and build 10 mega-prisons, echoing the war on gangs policy of El Salvador’s President Nayib Bukele, which has driven down homicide rates but fueled accusations of human rights abuses.
label: OBJ

text: “Today’s election isn’t just important for us, it’s important for all of Latin America,” said Juan Acevedo, a 62-year-old sociologist walking out of a voting station in Colombia’s capital on Sunday morning.
label: OBJ

text: “Whoever wins here will suggest to the region if progressive policies will continue or if things are going to return to the right.”
label: OBJ

text: Vote Is Seen As A Referendum On Petro
label: OBJ

text: The election — 10 years after Colombia signed an historic peace pact with guerrillas of the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia, or FARC — as seen as a referendum on Petro’s policies.
label: OBJ

text: The deal a decade ago had offered hope to break the nation’s vicious cycle of fighting between rebel groups and the government.
label: SUBJ

text: But violence has since roared back, in part because armed groups have taken advantage of peace negotiations with Petro’s government to make territorial gains.
label: SUBJ

text: That came to a head in the lead-up to the election.
label: OBJ

text: Criminal groups have increasingly launched drone strikes, armed attacks have plagued the race and last June, 39-year-old politician and presidential hopeful Miguel Uribe Turbay was fatally shot at a political rally.
label: OBJ

text: Still, Cepeda and Petro have maintained strong support among many because of progressive policies pushed forward under Petro, such as boosting the minimum wage.
label: OBJ

text: Both de la Espriella and Valencia have touted their affinity for Trump, though Valencia’s electoral loss dealt another blow to a once powerful political current known as Uribismo.

text: Colombians Are Divided On The Way Forward
label: OBJ

text: Maria Eugenia, a 57-year-old seamstress on Friday in downtown Bogotá, Colombia’s capital, said she welcomed an all-out offensive on an expanding slate of criminal groups, regardless of the human cost.
label: OBJ

text: While she approved of Petro’s pushes to improve the country’s medical infrastructure, she said she was voting for de la Espriella because violence in rural areas of the country has gotten out of hand.
label: OBJ

text: She said negotiating peace pacts was effectively rewarding armed groups.
label: OBJ

text: “Of course, whenever you come down with a heavy hand, there’s always going to be debate,” she said.
label: OBJ

text: “But some people are going to have to fall to clean up what needs to be cleaned up.”
label: OBJ

text: Others, like Acevedo, the sociologist, said a security crackdown such as the one promoted by de la Espriella meant a return to past military campaigns that he said only reinforced Colombia’s cycle of violence.
label: OBJ

text: He said he supports Cepeda, adding that while the government hasn’t done a perfect job — failing to pass ambitious reforms and follow through on promises to reduce violence — it was better to continue pushing forward with their political coalition’s efforts to take a different approach in addressing the country’s violence.
label: OBJ

text: He added that his main critique of Petro’s administration was the power grabs made by criminal groups as they negotiated with the government.
label: OBJ

text: He said he hoped that if Cepeda won, he would strike a better balance between negotiating peace and maintaining control over those groups.
label: OBJ

text: “We’re a country that has lived through 60 years of conflict,” Acevedo said.
label: OBJ

text: “The danger here is that we return to the times where everyone is saying that the only way to solve our problems is with bullets and more war.”
label: OBJ

text: President Donald Trump offered some advice to the nation early Monday morning as the United States and Iran renewed hostilities amid a fragile ceasefire.
label: OBJ

text: “Just sit back and relax, it will all work out well in the end,” the president wrote on Truth Social at 1:02 a.m. “It always does!”
label: OBJ

text: “It always does!” Trump insisted that Iran “really wants to make a deal” to end the three-month-old war, then attacked his critics:
label: OBJ

text: “But don’t the Dumocrats, and various seemingly unpatriotic Republicans, understand that it is MUCH tougher for me to properly do my job and negotiate, when political hacks keep negatively ‘chirping,’ at levels never seen before, over and over again, that I should move faster, or move slower, or go to war, or not go to war, or whatever.”
label: SUBJ

text: Trump initially anticipated the war would last “four to five weeks,” and insisted in early March that the effort was “substantially ahead of our time projections.”
label: OBJ

text: He has also repeatedly declared victory.
label: OBJ

text: “We’ve won,” he said on March 11.
label: OBJ

text: “Let me tell you, we’ve won.
label: SUBJ

text: You know, you never like to say too early you won. We won.
label: OBJ

text: We won. In the first hour it was over. But we won.”
label: OBJ

text: But we won.” “We’ve won this,” he insisted about two weeks later.
label: OBJ

text: “This war has been won.”
label: OBJ

text: Trump has also insisted that Iran would end its blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, a vital passageway for about 20% of the world’s oil that has effectively come to a standstill.
label: OBJ

text: As a result, the price of crude oil is about 50% higher than it was before the war, which in turn has led to higher fuel prices for U.S. motorists as well as a spike in inflation.
label: OBJ

text: In May, Trump said none of that’s a factor in his negotiations with Iran.
label: OBJ

text: “I don’t think about Americans’ financial situation.
label: OBJ

text: I don’t think about anybody,” he said.
label: OBJ

text: “I think about one thing: We cannot let Iran have a nuclear weapon.”

text: Colombians are casting ballots in the first round of the South American nation’s presidential election, choosing between candidates with radically diverging visions for the future of peace in a country haunted by decades of armed conflict.
label: SUBJ

text: The vote on Sunday, seen as a referendum on outgoing President Gustavo Petro’s policies, comes 10 years after Colombia signed a historic peace pact with guerrillas of the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (Farc).
label: OBJ

text: That agreement offered hope to break the nation out of a vicious cycle of fighting between rebel groups and the government but violence has roared back since then, coming to a head in the lead-up to the presidential vote.
label: SUBJ

text: Criminal groups have increasingly launched drone strikes, armed attacks have plagued the race and last June, the presidential hopeful Miguel Uribe Turbay, 39, was fatally shot at a political rally.
label: OBJ

text: In a country where the fight for peace has long been a part of the political ethos, the question of how to address the conflict is once again dividing the country.
label: OBJ

text: There are 14 candidates on the ballot, but the election has basically turned into a three-horse race.
label: SUBJ

text: The senator and peace-builder Iván Cepeda – a Petro ally – has led the polls and promises to carry on with Petro’s “total peace” initiative to negotiate with the country’s remaining rebel groups and sign peace agreements with them in an effort to resolve the persistent crisis.
label: OBJ

text: While the peace plan has largely failed as criminals have taken advantage of ceasefires with the government, Cepeda and Petro have maintained strong support among many because of progressive policies pushed forward under Petro, such as boosting the minimum wage.
label: SUBJ

text: Running against Cepeda are Abelardo de la Espriella and Paloma Valencia, who have vowed to come down on armed groups with a heavier hand.
label: OBJ

text: De la Espriella – a bombastic lawyer known as “the Tiger” – has gained traction among voters in recent weeks for pitching himself as an outsider keen on emulating the heavy-handed tactics used in El Salvador’s war on gangs, which sharply reduced gang violence but fuellled accusations of human rights abuses.
label: SUBJ

text: Valencia is considered the political protege of Colombia’s former president and strongman Álvaro Uribe, who governed from 2002 to 2010 with strong support from the US and whose government beat back Farc rebels in an offensive that took a massive civilian toll.
label: OBJ

text: De la Espriella and Valencia have both touted their affinity with Donald Trump even as he has taken a more aggressive stance toward Latin America than any US president in decades and has put pressure on nations such as Colombia, Ecuador and Mexico to more forcefully crack down on criminal groups.
label: OBJ

text: If no candidate wins at least 50% of the vote – something extremely rare in Colombia – the two top vote-getters will face a runoff in June.
label: OBJ

text: Maria Eugenia, 57, a seamstress in downtown Bogotá, Colombia’s capital, said she welcomed an all-out offensive on an expanding slate of criminal groups, regardless of the human cost.
label: OBJ

text: While she approved of Petro’s pushes to improve the country’s medical infrastructure, she said she was voting for de la Espriella because violence in rural areas of the country had got out of hand.
label: OBJ

text: “Of course, whenever you come down with a heavy hand, there’s always going to be debate,” she said.
label: OBJ

text: “But some people are going to have to fall to clean up what needs to be cleaned.”
label: OBJ

text: Others, like Cristian Morales, 26, who strolled outside Eugenia’s shop, disagree.
label: OBJ

text: While Petro’s peace plan had failed on many fronts, he said, making changes to a plan seeking to break the country out of cycles of violence was far better than swinging to another extreme.
label: SUBJ

text: He said he planned to vote for Cepeda, prioritising the candidate’s push to protect Colombia’s biodiversity and expand access to education over bold promises to unravel the country’s deeply entrenched conflict.
label: OBJ

text: That would be something Morales said he thought was impossible to do in just four years of a president’s term.
label: OBJ

text: “The solution to this conflict isn’t aggressive confrontations.
label: SUBJ

text: It will only end in more bloodshed,” he said.
label: OBJ

text: “It’s so difficult because it’s either dialogue or arms, and an internal conflict isn’t good for anyone.”
label: OBJ

text: NewsNation’s Batya Ungar-Sargon — a self-described “MAGA Lefty” who has previously hailed Donald Trump as “the most effective president of my lifetime” — pleaded with the president to help his supporters, as many struggle financially.
label: OBJ

text: “Mr. President, your supporters are hurting,” Ungar-Sargon bluntly said on the air Saturday.
label: OBJ

text: “People who voted for you three times, who would walk over Legos covered in hot coals to vote for you, are broke,” she continued.
label: OBJ

text: “Some are skipping meals, others are buying canned chicken in bulk, because that’s all they can afford.
label: OBJ

text: The cost of food and gas is just so high.”
label: SUBJ

text: Trump voters who support his Iran war had been “willing to pay a little more to make sure our grandkids don’t have to live with the threat of a nuclear-armed terror state” have now “paid all they can,” she said.
label: OBJ

text: Ungar-Sargon pointed to growing frustration among some MAGA supporters, saying savings are at “an all-time low” while credit card delinquency rates continue to climb.
label: OBJ

text: The vast majority of people are “broke,” she said.
label: OBJ

text: “Your supporters need help, Mr. President, and they need it from you.
label: SUBJ

text: We brought in $200 billion in tariff revenue.
label: OBJ

text: Couldn’t some of that be shared with Americans struggling to put food on the table?” Ungar-Sargon asked.
label: SUBJ

text: Mateo Pérez Rueda was one internship away from completing a degree in political science.
label: OBJ

text: The 24-year-old also worked as a bicycle delivery rider and sold fruit salads and juice to finance his passion: the Colombian independent digital magazine El Confidente.
label: OBJ

text: On 4 May he travelled to Briceño, in the western province of Antioquia, to report on the long-running conflict between the army, paramilitaries and dissidents of the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (Farc).
label: OBJ

text: The next day, he stopped responding to his parents.
label: OBJ

text: Three long days of agony followed, with relatives and friends pressing the authorities for information, until a humanitarian mission confirmed what many had feared: Rueda had been kidnapped, tortured and killed by one of the Farc dissident groups, known as the 36th Front.
label: OBJ

text: His case became yet another symbol of the surging political violence that has reached its highest levels in a decade – and that has made the decades-long internal armed conflict central to this Sunday’s presidential election.
label: SUBJ

text: The vote will be a contest between left and right – and two entirely contradictory proposals for dealing with the war that claimed nearly half a million lives.
label: SUBJ

text: Colombia’s president, Gustavo Petro, who under the constitution cannot seek re-election, has backed the leftwing senator Iván Cepeda, 63, who is leading in the polls and is regarded as the architect of the government’s “total peace” effort to sign disarmament deals with all criminal groups.
label: OBJ

text: Many security experts consider the plan to have failed, noting that armed factions have taken advantage of temporary ceasefires to continue expanding, but Cepeda remains committed to the plan.
label: OBJ

text: The two main challengers, the far-right lawyer and “outsider” Abelardo de la Espriella, 47, and the rightwing senator Paloma Valencia, 48, promise a return to all-out war as soon as they take office.
label: OBJ

text: During the election period, there has been a surge in guerrilla attacks, homicides, kidnappings, forced displacement and massacres; and last year, the rightwing senator and presidential hopeful Miguel Uribe Turbay was shot during a campaign event and later died.
label: OBJ

text: The violence is widely seen as a reminder that although the landmark 2016 peace deal between the government and most of Farc dramatically reduced violence for years, it did not end it for good.
label: OBJ

text: Subsequent administrations slow-walked the implementation of the settlement, while some Farc factions and other rebel groups refused to sign any agreement, instead growing in strength and size.
label: OBJ

text: “Here in Antioquia, the war never ended,” said Jorge Rueda, Mateo’s cousin and godfather, who lived a few blocks away from the journalist in Yarumal, only 33 miles (53km) from where he was killed.
label: OBJ

text: Although the various rebel factions claim a political agenda, most of the violence is driven by competition over drug production, retail and smuggling (Colombia remains the world’s biggest producer of cocaine), illegal goldmining, logging and local corruption.
label: OBJ

text: “Here, the war is over micro-trafficking and another over the goldmines,” added Rueda.
label: OBJ

text: On Monday, more than 50 people were killed in clashes between two Farc dissident groups on the opposite side of the country, in the southern department of Guaviare.
label: OBJ

text: Many of them were children and teenagers forcibly recruited by the crime factions.
label: OBJ

text: Alejandro Chala, a researcher at the Fundación Paz y Reconciliación, argued that although the figures were high, the current moment was not comparable to the period before the peace agreement, when the homicide rate peaked at about 80 per 100,000 inhabitants; it now stands at about 26 per 100,000.
label: OBJ

text: “The violence now is much more territorially concentrated, largely entrenched in the main areas where illegal economic routes operate … It clearly generates a lot of media noise, but it does not have the national reach it had in the past,” he said.
label: OBJ

text: Even so, Espriella has argued that it is necessary to “save Colombia” from crime, while Valencia says that instead of “total peace”, the country needs “total security”.
label: OBJ

text: Until recently Cepeda remained firmly at the top of the polls, with Valencia in second place; but in the past two weeks she has been overtaken by Espriella.
label: OBJ

text: With a large share of voters still undecided, the outcome is uncertain: if no candidate wins more than half of the vote, a runoff will be held on 21 June.
label: OBJ

text: Valencia has been a senator since 2014, and is the granddaughter of the former president Guillermo León Valencia, and a loyal follower of the ex-president Álvaro Uribe Vélez, whose two terms between 2002 and 2010 were marked by an aggressive military confrontation with armed groups that produced limited results and became tainted by the “false positives” scandal, when innocent people were extrajudicially killed by the army and falsely labelled as enemy combatants.
label: OBJ

text: Espriella is a criminal lawyer and millionaire businessman with a lavish lifestyle who owns wine and rum brands and investments in cattle ranching and real estate, but has never held public office.
label: OBJ

text: He is an admirer of Donald Trump, and has courted controversy during the campaign, telling a radio host that he is winning female voters because of the size of his genitals.
label: OBJ

text: With his neatly trimmed beard, he has also modelled himself on El Salvador’s populist autocrat Nayib Bukele, who has imprisoned at least 2% of his country’s adult population as part of a controversial crackdown on gangs.
label: SUBJ

text: Espriella promises to follow the Salvadoran mano dura (iron-fist) approach and build 10 maximum-security “mega-prisons”.
label: OBJ

text: Sandra Borda Guzmán, an associate professor of political science at Universidad de los Andes, said: “He also carries some elements of this new counterculture against political correctness, in the style of [Argentina’s president] Javier Milei and Donald Trump.”
label: OBJ

text: Despite Espriella’s openly declared admiration for Trump, the US president has so far refrained from endorsing either him or Valencia, unlike in other recent elections involving far-right candidates, such as in Hungary, Honduras and Argentina.
label: OBJ

text: Guzmán believes one reason may lie in Espriella’s recent past: he spent years as the lawyer for figures such as the Colombian businessman Álex Saab, widely regarded as the main financial frontman for Nicolás Maduro’s regime in Venezuela, who was recently deported to the US by the acting president, Delcy Rodríguez.
label: OBJ

text: “As a criminal lawyer, Espriella has long been linked to people prosecuted or extradited by the US and those close ties remain concerning for Washington.
label: SUBJ

text: So he’s not really the ideal candidate for the White House,” said Guzmán.
label: OBJ

text: She believes another reason why the US has not openly tried to interfere in the election is that “they eventually realised those interventions produce the opposite effect to what they want”, noting that Trump’s attacks on Petro – calling him a “sick man” and “drug-trafficking leader” – ended up boosting the Colombian president’s popularity.
label: OBJ

text: Petro, a former member of a smaller rebel faction that signed a peace deal years before the Farc agreement, is Colombia’s first leftwing president.
label: OBJ

text: His approval ratings are widely seen as high for a president nearing the end of his term, something many analysts attribute to the expansion of the government’s social programmes and increases in the minimum wage, alongside falling poverty rates.
label: OBJ

text: Despite losing his godson Mateo to the internal armed conflict, Jorge Rueda believes Colombia is doing better.
label: OBJ

text: “I could say something different out of anger, but from the heart I believe Colombia has improved enormously in recent years … such as giving young people better opportunities so they don’t join the armed conflict.
label: OBJ

text: However, there are some regions that never improved,” he said.
label: OBJ

text: Right-wing candidate pulls ahead in first round of Colombia's presidential vote
label: OBJ

text: BOGOTÁ, Colombia — Tough-on-crime outsider Aberaldo de la Espriella took the lead in Colombia's presidential race in the first round of voting Sunday night, setting up a runoff with Iván Cepeda, an ally of Colombia's outgoing President Gustavo Petro who questioned the results of the election.
label: OBJ

text: With no candidate taking an outright majority of the vote, the election will head to a second round in June.
label: OBJ

text: But Cepeda and Petro sowed doubt in the results of the first round, claiming without evidence that hundreds of thousands of votes were manipulated and that foreign actors manipulated the results of the election.
label: OBJ

text: Cepeda said he was waiting for electoral authorities to scrutinize the results before accepting the election.
label: OBJ

text: "Only when the vote-counting commissions have fully clarified what happened will we comment on tonight's results," Cepeda said, though he acknowledged the vote was likely going to a second round.
label: OBJ

text: Cepeda won 41% of the vote, while de la Espriella won 44% of the votes, with 99.98% of the results counted by electoral authorities.
label: OBJ

text: Cepeda is a progressive senator who has promised to carry on a fraught plan to achieve "total peace" by negotiating peace pacts with guerrillas and criminal gangs.
label: OBJ

text: He was consistently leading polls in the run up to the Sunday vote, but in the weeks leading up to the election de la Espriella rapidly gained support with a promise that he would crack down on armed groups.
label: OBJ

text: The neck-and-neck results likely spell trouble for Cepeda in the run-off election, as de la Espriella is expected to scoop up support from voters who threw their support behind another conservative candidate in the first round.

text: Following last week’s rollout of the Aliens.gov webpage targeting immigrants — which critics condemned as “dehumanizing” — the White House shared a video of a “Space Invaders”-inspired shoot ’em up game on X.

label: OBJ

text: U.S. bombs Iranian military sites and Kuwait is hit by drone and missile fire
label: OBJ

text: DUBAI, United Arab Emirates — The United States bombed Iranian radar and drone control sites in Iran after Tehran shot down an American MQ-1 Predator drone this weekend, the American military said Monday.
label: OBJ

text: Iran acknowledged launching a retaliatory strike, while Kuwait said it was intercepting incoming drone and missile fire.
label: OBJ

text: The dueling attacks reflect the fragility of a weekslong ceasefire in the Iran war, which has seen repeated attacks even as American and Iranian officials try to negotiate a deal to extend it.
label: OBJ

text: Iran has maintained its chokehold on the Strait of Hormuz in the meantime, disrupting global energy supplies as a fifth of all oil and natural gas traded once passed through the narrow mouth of the Persian Gulf.
label: SUBJ

text: Meanwhile, fighting continues to escalate as Israel extends its occupation of Lebanon beyond the Litani River and as the militant group Hezbollah continues to launch drones into Israel.
