text: US military attacks Iran
label: OBJ

text: The U.S. military's Central Command said it carried out the strikes in Iran on Saturday and Sunday around the city of Geruk and on Qeshm Island.
label: OBJ

text: "The measured and deliberate strikes occurred ... in response to aggressive Iranian actions that included the shootdown of a U.S.
label: OBJ

text: MQ-1 drone that was operating over international waters," Central Command said.
label: OBJ

text: "U.S. fighter aircraft swiftly responded by eliminating Iranian air defenses, a ground control station, and two one-way attack drones that posed clear threats to ships transiting regional waters."
label: SUBJ

text: The Predator has been phased out of service by the U.S. Air Force, which now flies the MQ-9 Reaper, though the U.S. Army still flies the Predator.
label: OBJ

text: The U.S. military said no American troops were hurt in the attacks.
label: OBJ

text: Kuwait reports incoming fire
label: OBJ

text: Kuwait meanwhile said its air defenses had opened fire early Monday morning to intercept incoming drone and missile fire.
label: OBJ

text: Iran's paramilitary Revolutionary Guard in a statement carried by the state-run IRNA news agency that U.S. forces had targeted a telecommunications tower.
label: OBJ

text: The Guard said it responded with an attack without saying where, likely referring to the attack on Kuwait.
label: OBJ

text: The country is home to U.S. Army Central, the Mideast forward command for the Army.
label: OBJ

text: Iranian state television later shared footage of the ballistic missile launch, including a close-up showing a sticker on its body depicting a bruised U.S. President Donald Trump overlaid on a "closed" Strait of Hormuz with the caption: "Until the last American soldier leaves the region."
label: OBJ

text: Attacks rattle ceasefire talks
label: OBJ

text: The attacks represent the latest escalation between the U.S. and Iran, even as both have insisted they are continuing to negotiate, particularly over Iran's stockpile of highly enriched uranium.
label: OBJ

text: Over the weekend, the U.S. fired a missile into the engine room of a Gambia-flagged cargo ship trying to break its blockade of Iranian ports.
label: OBJ

text: A trickle of ships have made it out of the strait, but pressure continues on global energy supplies, as well as chemical fertilizer which has led to fears of food shortages.
label: SUBJ

text: The Gulf region produces 30% of globally traded chemical fertilizers.
label: OBJ

text: Trump met with advisers on Friday but has yet to decide on whether to move ahead with a deal to extend the ceasefire and reopen the strait.
label: OBJ

text: Iran has said the deal had not been finalized.
label: OBJ

text: Trump expressed optimism about the talks in a post on his Truth Social platform early Monday in Washington, mocking critics without addressing the ongoing crossfire.
label: OBJ

text: "Iran really wants to make a deal, and it will be a good one for the U.S.A. and those that are with us," he wrote.
label: OBJ

text: "Just sit back and relax, it will all work out well in the end — It always does!"
label: SUBJ

text: A powerful tropical storm is forecast to track near Okinawa, Japan, on Monday before moving towards the south-east of the country.
label: OBJ

text: Typhoon Jangmi (also known as Typhoon No 6) has formed within the monsoonal gyre over the Philippine Sea.
label: OBJ

text: A monsoonal gyre is a large, slow-rotating weather system that spawns typhoons through smaller vortices formed within it.
label: OBJ

text: This flow can intensify storms.
label: OBJ

text: Such typhoons are typically characterised by broad areas of low pressure and extensive wind fields, often without a distinct eye.
label: OBJ

text: Residents have been advised to take precautions, with strong winds and heavy rain expected early this week raising the risk of flooding, storm surges and landslides.
label: OBJ

text: By early Monday morning, the typhoon was moving at 10mph (16km/h), with sustained winds of 67mph, gusts of up to 100mph and a central pressure of 975hPa.
label: OBJ

text: The Japan Meteorological Agency forecasts the pressure could fall to about 965hPa, with maximum winds increasing to 70mph and gusts reaching 110mph on Monday.
label: OBJ

text: Similar conditions are expected across parts of southern Australia early this week as a 980hPa low-pressure system sweeps eastwards .
label: OBJ

text: The weather system, which brought severe storms to Western Australia last week, is forecast to hit densely populated parts of Victoria on Monday.
label: OBJ

text: Gusts reaching 80mph have lashed the western coast and similar wind speeds are expected midweek in coastal areas of Victoria.
label: OBJ

text: Snow is also possible in Kosciuszko national park on Monday afternoon.
label: OBJ

text: In contrast, the heat affecting southern Iberia is expected to persist as high pressure lingers over southern Europe.
label: OBJ

text: Although meteorological summer has only just begun, temperatures are likely to climb to the high 30Cs across a large swathe of southern Spain, with some regions soaring to 40C.
label: OBJ

text: While such temperatures may not seem unusual for early June in Iberia, they are 5-10C above the long-term average.
label: OBJ

text: In a case from Mississippi, Republican Party officials are seeking to strike down state laws that allow late-arriving mail ballots to be counted as long as they are postmarked by Election Day.
label: OBJ

text: Trump has sought to cast doubt on the security of mail-in ballots, though evidence of voter fraud is rare, and Democratic voters tend to use this mode of voting more than Republicans.
label: OBJ

text: In a separate case involving Trump’s Vice President JD Vance, Republicans are seeking to further chip away at legal limits on money in political campaigns - specifically involving spending coordinated between party organizations and candidates.
label: OBJ

text: They argue such curbs violate the U.S. Constitution’s First Amendment protections against government abridgment of freedom of speech.
label: OBJ

text: The court has proven receptive to such an argument, including in its landmark 2010 decision in the case Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission.
label: OBJ

text: Rulings in both cases are expected by around the end of June.
label: OBJ

text: Republicans are defending slim majorities in both the House of Representatives and Senate in the November 3 midterms.
label: OBJ

text: If Democrats win control of either chamber, they could impede Trump’s legislative agenda and mount investigations of him and his administration.
label: OBJ

text: VOTING RIGHTS CASE RULING
label: OBJ

text: The Supreme Court has a 6-3 conservative majority.
label: OBJ

text: In an April decision powered by the conservative justices in a case from Louisiana, it gutted a key provision of the Voting Rights Act, making it harder to challenge electoral maps as racially discriminatory under the landmark civil rights law.
label: SUBJ

text: That decision provided an immediate advantage to Trump’s party ahead of the midterms, though legal experts said the impact of the two forthcoming rulings is harder to gauge.
label: OBJ

text: The Voting Rights Act ruling opened the door for Republican state legislators to dismantle Democratic-held U.S. House districts with large Black or Latino populations across the South, potentially giving Republicans an electoral advantage for years to come.
label: SUBJ

text: Black and Hispanic voters tend to vote for Democratic candidates.
label: OBJ

text: That decision has been a “boon for Republicans,” said Travis Crum, a Washington University in St. Louis School of Law professor.
label: OBJ

text: Thanks in part to that ruling, Republicans are positioned in November to gain up to a dozen U.S. House seats currently held by Democrats through the process of redistricting - redrawing the boundaries of voting districts.
label: OBJ

text: Working against Republicans in November are Trump’s sagging approval ratings, as measured by public opinion polls, amid the unpopular Iran war and the higher gasoline prices it caused, and the historical trend of a president’s party losing congressional seats in midterms. CAMPAIGN FINANCE CASE
label: OBJ

text: CAMPAIGN FINANCE CASE Vance and other Republicans appealed a lower court’s ruling that upheld restrictions on the amount of money parties can spend on campaigns with input from candidates they support - called coordinated party expenditures.
label: OBJ

text: Vance was running for the U.S. Senate in Ohio in 2022 when he and other challengers sued.
label: OBJ

text: Under the bedrock 1971 law regulating fundraising in U.S. federal elections, spending by a political party to advocate for or against a candidate that is not coordinated with a candidate’s campaign is considered an independent expenditure not subject to amount limits.
label: OBJ

text: But it does restrict contributions coordinated between a party and a campaign.
label: OBJ

text: Supporters of these restrictions have said they help prevent corruption.
label: OBJ

text: Without them, they said, wealthy donors could curry favor with candidates by routing massive sums to them through political parties, evading limits on how much any individual can donate to a candidate per election cycle.
label: OBJ

text: Conservative election law attorney Dan Backer said striking down limits on coordinated spending would strengthen political parties, which “have a generally moderating impact” on U.S. politics compared to special interest groups.
label: OBJ

text: “The overall political system is benefited by very strong parties,” said Backer, who has represented Republican candidates and right-leaning organizations.
label: OBJ

text: During December arguments in the case, conservative justices appeared sympathetic to the First Amendment argument against these limits.
label: SUBJ

text: University of Minnesota political science professor Timothy Johnson said a ruling in favor of the Republicans seems likely, and it could let them capitalize on their fundraising advantage over the Democrats.
label: OBJ

text: Three major Republican committees - the Republican National Committee, the National Republican Congressional Committee and the National Republican Senatorial Committee - ended April with $251 million in cash and no debt.
label: OBJ

text: That was about double the roughly $125 million held by their Democratic counterparts, who also carried more than $17 million in debt.
label: OBJ

text: “There certainly is an advantage, monetarily, on the Republican side in terms of the party committees,” Johnson said.
label: OBJ

text: “Once that ruling comes down, there could be coordination between those committees and candidates pretty instantaneously.”
label: OBJ

text: Johnson added that some individual Democratic candidates in high-profile races have impressive fundraising hauls that could blunt the impact of a ruling in favor of the Republicans.
label: OBJ

text: The ruling may lead party committees to seek the same discounted rates for television and radio advertisements that candidates have long received, though election law specialists said this would raise an untested legal question.
label: OBJ

text: In the Citizens United ruling, the court cited the First Amendment, invalidating campaign finance limits and letting corporations and other outside groups such as labor unions spend unlimited money on elections. MAIL-IN BALLOTS CASE
label: OBJ

text: MAIL-IN BALLOTS CASE The Supreme Court’s ruling in the mail-in ballots “grace period” case could lead to stricter voting rules around the country.
label: SUBJ

text: Casting ballots by mail has been common among some Republican voters, particularly among rural and older ones.
label: OBJ

text: But Trump’s false claims about widespread voter fraud, including allegations involving mailed ballots, after he lost the 2020 election to Democrat Joe Biden, have made the practice less popular among Republicans.
label: SUBJ

text: Trump in March signed an executive order aiming to tighten mail-in voting rules, drawing legal challenges involving whether his directive infringed on the constitutional rights of individual states to regulate elections.
label: OBJ

text: In the 2024 U.S. election, 37% of Democratic voters reported casting ballots by mail, compared to 24% of Republicans, according to the MIT Election Lab.
label: OBJ

text: In the 2020 election conducted during the COVID pandemic, 60% of Democratic voters and 32% of Republican voters cast mail-in ballots.
label: OBJ

text: The Supreme Court heard arguments in March in Mississippi’s appeal of a lower court’s ruling that deemed its mail-in ballot law illegal in a challenge by the state’s Republican Party.
label: OBJ

text: The law permits mail-in ballots to be counted if they are postmarked by, and then received within five business days of, Election Day.
label: OBJ

text: The dispute involved whether federal laws setting the dates for federal elections preempt any state laws that allow ballots to be received after Election Day.
label: OBJ

text: During March arguments in the case, a majority of the justices appeared ready to invalidate Mississippi’s law.
label: SUBJ

text: According to the National Conference of State Legislatures, 14 states, plus Guam, Puerto Rico, the Virgin Islands and Washington, D.C., accept and count mailed ballots if they are received after Election Day but postmarked on or before - sometimes only before - Election Day.
label: OBJ

text: Crum said the justices could strike down Mississippi’s law but let it remain in effect for the midterms under a legal concept called the Purcell principle under which courts should strive to avoid changing voting rules too close to an election to avoid voter confusion.
label: OBJ

text: The Democratic National Committee filed a legal brief warning of “disastrous consequences” if the court backs the Republicans in the case.
label: OBJ

text: Striking down Mississippi’s law, it said, and imposing on states an inflexible Election Day deadline for receiving mail-in ballots could disenfranchise millions of voters, “including military voters stationed away from home, overseas citizens, rural voters, elderly and disabled voters, and voters lacking reliable transportation.”
label: OBJ

text: Chris McIsaac, a researcher at the R Street Institute libertarian think tank, viewed requiring mail ballots to arrive by Election Day as reasonable, but said there could be administrative challenges to implementing new rules just months before an election.
label: OBJ

text: “All of the voter communications and information that election offices publish in advance of elections that give the instructions for when ballots are due - that stuff happens pretty far in advance,” McIsaac said.
label: OBJ

text: “Some of that would need to be reprinted.”
label: OBJ

text: Japan’s defence minister took a veiled swipe at China on Sunday, pledging to keep strengthening the military despite Beijing’s criticism of Tokyo’s increasingly muscular security stance.
label: OBJ

text: Under the prime minister, Sanae Takaichi, Japan has quickened its pivot to a more proactive defence policy, further shaking off – with US encouragement – its pacifist outlook in place since the end of the second world war.
label: SUBJ

text: The change has drawn frequent rebukes from Beijing, which has accused Tokyo of following a reckless policy of “new militarism” that could destabilise the region.
label: OBJ

text: The Japanese defence minister, Shinjiro Koizumi, hit back on Sunday, saying “nothing could be further from the truth”. “Think about it.
label: OBJ

text: “Think about it. There is a country that has a huge arsenal of nuclear weapons and strategic bombers,” Koizumi said at the annual Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore.
label: OBJ

text: “Japan has neither of such weapons.
label: OBJ

text: And yet, Japan is labelled (as) ‘new militarism’. Isn’t it strange?”
label: SUBJ

text: Isn’t it strange?” he said, without mentioning China by name.
label: OBJ

text: China is thought to possess hundreds of nuclear warheads and has been rapidly developing its military in recent years.
label: OBJ

text: A diplomatic spat between the Asian rivals has been rumbling since Takaichi suggested in November that Japan might intervene militarily if China were to attempt to seize Taiwan, the self-ruled island that Beijing claims is part of its territory.
label: OBJ

text: Koizumi said that China was expanding its military capabilities “without sufficient transparency” and that its military activities were “a matter of serious concern for Japan”.
label: OBJ

text: Tokyo would “steadily build up its defence capabilities and make continuous updates with a high degree of transparency”, including in the fields of artificial intelligence, uncrewed systems and cyber and space defence, he said.
label: OBJ

text: “Japan’s past as a peace-loving nation has been valued by the region and by [the] international community.
label: OBJ

text: This fact will not be shaken by false claims, because it is a fact,” he said.
label: OBJ

text: The Shangri-La Dialogue is Asia’s top defence forum, bringing together security officials and experts from about 45 countries.
label: OBJ

text: In contrast to Japan – and its ally the US – China has sent a watered-down delegation that does not include its defence minister, Dong Jun, for the second year running.
label: SUBJ

text: Koizumi said he was “feeling sad that we were unable to have the opportunity to have a meeting this time”.
label: OBJ

text: The defence minister, Richard Marles, has said the “seabed is a battlefield” in a combative speech urging Beijing to be more transparent about its maritime operations, and taking aim at weak international controls over so-called “shadow-fleet” vessels.
label: OBJ

text: The warning came as the US, UK and Australia announced a new Aukus project to develop new underwater drone technology to protect undersea cables.
label: OBJ

text: The same announcement also revealed that Australia would buy three secondhand Virginia-class submarines from the US under Aukus, instead of a mix of old and new, in a move to “simplify supply chain management, operational and maintenance requirements, and maximise cost efficiencies”.
label: OBJ

text: Speaking at the Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore, Marles said undersea internet cables – “the arteries of modern civilisation” – were being cut at an unprecedented rate, with island nations like Australia acutely vulnerable.
label: OBJ

text: “The seabed is becoming a battlefield.
label: SUBJ

text: The shadow fleet is becoming a weapon,” he told Asia’s largest defence summit.
label: OBJ

text: “Over the past 18 months, we have witnessed a series of attacks against subsea critical infrastructure at a scale and frequency that is historically unprecedented.
label: SUBJ

text: “This is not speculation.
label: OBJ

text: This is a documented pattern of behaviour.
label: OBJ

text: And we must reckon with it honestly.”
label: SUBJ

text: Marles cited five cases of cables being cut in the Taiwan Strait in the past 18 months, attributed to China, and three in the Baltic Sea, alleged to have been committed by Russia.
label: OBJ

text: “Now, maybe these were accidents.
label: SUBJ

text: But even if they were, it highlights the vulnerability of this crucial part of the globe’s infrastructure,” Marles said.
label: OBJ

text: “If they were intentional, we are left to wonder: are countries testing our response times, testing our attribution thresholds and testing our political will to respond?”
label: SUBJ

text: The US secretary of defence, Pete Hegseth, announced the first “signature project” for the second pillar of the Aukus treaty, whose first pillar is the nuclear submarine project.
label: OBJ

text: “This signature project will deliver a suite of highly adaptable multi-mission UUV payloads designed to support undersea operations and maintain our collective advantage in the maritime domain,” he told reporters at a briefing in Singapore.
label: OBJ

text: The UK defence secretary, John Healey, said that the planned technology, a “range of cutting edge sensors and weapons systems” for underseas drones, “will rapidly give our forces the very most advanced battlefield technologies”.
label: OBJ

text: The systems will be deployed on uncrewed underwater vessels, Healey added.
label: OBJ

text: About 99% of Australia’s internet traffic flows through just 15 subsea cables, Marles said.
label: OBJ

text: “Our financial systems, our health systems, our communications, our intelligence partnerships, our ability to operate as a modern economy and a functioning state: all of it is critically dependent on infrastructure that is exposed, that cannot move and … can be cut with an anchor in the middle of the night.”
label: OBJ

text: Marles said China had “a real opportunity” to contribute to a more stable Asia-Pacific region.
label: OBJ

text: “A commitment to transparency around its maritime operations would be a meaningful contribution to the regional stability upon which China’s own prosperity depends.
label: SUBJ

text: “Existing patterns of grey zone activity are not consistent with a peaceful and stable regional order.”
label: OBJ

text: Marles said the problem of “shadow-fleet” ships – vessels operating in the grey zone between commercial shipping and instruments of state coercion – extended far beyond the vulnerability of subsea cables.
label: OBJ

text: “These same networks of unregistered, flag-of-convenience vessels are vectors for sanctions evasion, for the transport of energy that sustains Russia’s war in Europe, for illegal fishing, for human and drug trafficking.”
label: OBJ

text: Hegseth said the Trump administration would not let China dominate the Pacific, but did not directly mention Taiwan.
label: OBJ

text: Hegseth poured further pressure on allies in Europe and Asia to spend more on defence, arguing the US wanted “partners not protectorates”.
label: OBJ

text: And he invoked Theodore Roosevelt by declaring America would “speak softly, but carry a big stick”.
label: OBJ

text: Hegseth said the US wanted to work with allies to create a “stable equilibrium” in the Asia-Pacific.
label: OBJ

text: “A Pacific dominated by any hegemon would unravel the regional balance of power and undermine the equilibrium we all seek to preserve,” Hegseth said.
label: OBJ

text: “The Department of War [the unofficial alternate name for the US department of defence] is working with the utmost focus to prevent any such unravelling.”
label: OBJ

text: He warned the Trump administration’s record defence budget request – US$1.5tn (A$2.085tn) – would “unleash America’s arsenal of freedom and expand America’s military dominance for decades to come”.
label: OBJ

text: But he did not directly mention Taiwan, the issue most keenly watched by officials and ministers from other countries, in the wake of an underwhelming summit between Donald Trump and Xi Jinping in Beijing this month, as well as America’s stalled arms sales to Taiwan.
label: SUBJ

text: There is concern in Taiwan that the Trump White House’s commitment to the autonomous island is less robust than that of previous US administrations.
label: OBJ

text: Last year, Hegseth was full-throated in his position, warning against the “real” and potentially “imminent” threat of a Chinese invasion of the island.
label: OBJ

text: He said China’s army was “rehearsing for the real deal”.
label: OBJ

text: Beijing’s “One China” principle regards Taiwan as an inalienable part of its own territory and has vowed “reunification” with the democratic and autonomously governed island, by force if necessary.
label: OBJ

text: Taiwan’s government rejects Beijing’s sovereignty claims, insisting the island’s people must determine their future.
label: OBJ

text: Texas Republican Ken Paxton practically served his Democratic challenger opposition material on a silver platter while defending his colored reputation during a Sunday morning appearance on [OUTLET].
label: SUBJ

text: Paxton, President Donald Trump’s choice for the Lone Star State’s 2026 Senate race, couldn’t have picked a worse comparison when “Sunday Morning Futures” host Maria Bartiromo asked what he was going to do about being called “scandal-plagued” in a recent opinion piece published by the Wall Street Journal.
label: SUBJ

text: Claiming any controversies were concocted up by anti-Trump Republicans, he told voters, “I get why they said that, but the reality is they could say the same thing about Donald Trump” — the White House’s chaos agent-in-chief, a convicted felon and alleged associate of prolific sex criminal Jeffrey Epstein.
label: SUBJ

text: “When you’re fighting the fight, unfortunately, you get attacked, and you have to defend yourself,” Paxton went on.
label: OBJ

text: “And when you do that, and ... they’re not successful, they still accuse you of things.”
label: OBJ

text: “Accusations don’t mean that the thing actually happened.
label: OBJ

text: They have to prove these things in our country.
label: OBJ

text: That they didn’t do with President Trump and that they didn’t do with me, and yet they continue to act like something bad happened when they have no proof of it.”
label: SUBJ

text: Penned by former President George W. Bush advisor Karl Rove, the Wall Street Journal story in question was titled “The GOP’s Five Paxton Problems.”
label: OBJ

text: According to Rove, who Trump once called a “RINO of the highest order,” Paxton has been accused of “corruption, bribery, obstruction, securities fraud, multiple mistresses and incompetent handling of sex-trafficking cases.”
label: OBJ

text: The accusations had enough merit to get the former Texas attorney general impeached by the Republican-run state House in 2023, but he was narrowly acquitted by the Senate.
label: OBJ

text: Rove warned that Paxton’s baggage has the possibility of dragging down the entire GOP ticket come November, claiming, “Every Texas Republican running for Congress, statewide office or the Texas Legislature could be asked about his scandals.”
label: OBJ

text: With the help of a coveted Trump endorsement delivered just seven days before the primaries, Paxton pummeled incumbent Sen. John Cornyn (R-Texas) 64% to 36% last week.
label: OBJ

text: But Rove also pointed to Paxton’s poll numbers prior to the president’s 11th-hour support as a sign he’ll have an uphill battle in the general election against State Senator James Talarico.
label: OBJ

text: According to the political strategist, just 81% of primary voters knew who Paxton was and out of those, only 35% said they would be more likely to vote for him than Coryn.
label: OBJ

text: Four more miners who were trapped in a flooded cave in Laos for 10 days have been freed by divers, but two people are still missing as rescuers continue to crawl through narrow, deluged tunnels and sharp rocks to find them.
label: OBJ

text: The first of the party of seven men was rescued on Friday in a perilous rescue mission which has required teams to drain water from the cave and navigate collapse hazards.
label: OBJ

text: The four men who were extracted on Saturday were found crouched and huddled together on a rocky ledge inside a chamber about 300 metres (980ft) from the cave entrance on Wednesday, but rescuers were unable to immediately evacuate them.
label: OBJ

text: The search for the remaining two missing people is continuing, with international diving teams – including some who worked on the dramatic rescue of a young Thai football team – crawling and twisting through muddy water in narrow passageways and poor visibility in an attempt to locate them.
label: OBJ

text: It is thought the men entered the cave last week to look for valuable minerals such as gold ore before being trapped by flash flooding caused by heavy rain, which carried in sand and gravel, blocking a crucial exit.
label: OBJ

text: Rescuers supplied the party of five they found on Wednesday with water, soft food and foil blankets to keep them warm while they remained inside the cave.
label: OBJ

text: The first man to be rescued was captured on camera emerging from the cave on Friday, covered in mud and walking unsteadily.
label: OBJ

text: As rescuers cheered and helped him to move away from the cave entrance, he cried out in pain and one rescuer could be heard warning others to be careful because his hands were injured.
label: OBJ

text: “The first one is out. Safe and sound!!!”
label: OBJ

text: Safe and sound!!!” wrote Manat Artmongkron, a rescue technician for Saithan Saphanboon Foundation, a Thai rescue group, in a jubilant Facebook post.
label: OBJ

text: On Saturday, a second man was filmed slowly climbing out of a narrow entrance to the cave.
label: OBJ

text: He stood shakily and smiled as his rescuers wrapped him in a foil blanket.
label: OBJ

text: Lao and Thai rescue groups posted photos on social media of the men lying on stretchers, wearing oxygen masks and being wrapped in foil blankets after the successful rescue operation.
label: OBJ

text: Thai rescuer Kengkaj Bongkawong, of the Metta Tham Rescue Kalasin, said rescuers faced “issues with the temperature, narrow areas, control of movement, and managing the panic of the survivor” while they were diving.
label: OBJ

text: He said teams were required to dive through a 25 metre-long narrow tunnel: “Diving in the narrow passage that has no space to make a U-turn, this is really dangerous so we need to evaluate the safety measures, principles, routes and expertise in the cave area.”
label: OBJ

text: Josh Richards, an Australian cave diver who joined the team on Friday, said rescuers were trying to pump out as much water as possible because of the cave’s “particularly unstable” clay and mud walls.
label: OBJ

text: This affects the consistency and visibility of the water, he said: “You’re essentially diving in coffee.
label: OBJ

text: You’re not going to be seeing anything through it.”
label: OBJ

text: Bongkawong said his team were planning to explore an area deeper inside the cave, about 20 to 25 metres beyond where the survivors were found.
label: OBJ

text: However, he warned that the section was heavily flooded.
label: OBJ

text: Somehow, President Donald Trump found a way to be smug about a new report revealing over 10,000 lawyers have abandoned their federal jobs since he was reelected in late 2024.
label: SUBJ

text: Trump celebrated the D.C. brain drain as a victory against the so-called “deep state” in a Sunday afternoon Truth Social post, where he knocked a New York Times deep dive into the flight for being framed as if it was a “bad thing.”
label: SUBJ

text: Claiming the exodus fit perfectly into his “Make America Great Again” agenda, he said, “The people that are leaving are Radical Left Deep State Lunatics, who are destroying our Country, and Weaponizing Government.
label: OBJ

text: Many of them didn’t leave, but were fired!”
label: OBJ

text: Trump continued his contrarian take, writing, “The Failing New York Times writes this, but makes it sound like it’s a terrible thing when actually, it’s just the opposite.”
label: OBJ

text: “We want people that will, MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN, not people that are trying to destroy our Country, that were put in by Obama and Biden and, in many cases, they shouldn’t have been representing the U.S.A. in the first place,” the president went on.
label: OBJ

text: “Let them go on to ‘bigger, better, and brighter’ things in the future - I fully support that, and wish them all well!”
label: OBJ

text: he seemed to snark at the end.
label: SUBJ

text: In an analysis of federal employment data, the Times found about 17% of lawyers who were working for the government at the end of 2024 had left their positions by this March.
label: OBJ

text: Though the story attributed some of the hemorrhage of legal talent to typical turnover reasons like retirement, it also cited deep staffing cuts and people who decided they’d rather resign than help carry out Trump’s policies, both problems paired with hiring levels which were drastically lower than the amount of departures.
label: OBJ

text: The Trump government’s reputation as ethically questionable and notoriously unstable has reportedly had a chilling effect on recruiting early-career lawyers into working for the government, which the Times said was formerly the “the gold standard ” for those seeking roles in public service.
label: OBJ

text: Andrew Mergen, the director of the Emmett Environmental Law and Policy Clinic at Harvard Law School, told the paper he was seeing a real shift in both the prestige and credibility once linked to federal legal work.
label: OBJ

text: “A lot of people I’ve spoken to just in the last few months have said that they would look down on a person if they had a federal job on their résumé that they started during this administration,” he explained.
label: OBJ

text: “And some people have explicitly said they would see a person willing to go to work at this D.O.J. as somebody they couldn’t trust.”
label: OBJ

text: A Chinese dissident who orchestrated an anti-government protest in China after fleeing to the UK has claimed that a “pro-regime” interpreter used by a British police force berated him when he sought help.
label: OBJ

text: Hong Qi, who made headlines last year after using a mobile phone while in the UK to remotely project anti-regime slogans on to a building in his home city, Chongqing, contacted police after discovering that his bank accounts had been frozen.
label: OBJ

text: The Chinese national rang 101, the UK non-emergency number, on 20 December and asked to speak to the closest police force via an interpreter out of concern he would have to sleep rough with his wife and two teenage daughters due to lack of funds.
label: OBJ

text: Instead of receiving advice from Devon and Cornwall police, Qi, 43, claims the interpreter assigned to the call launched a political tirade, asking him why he did not “love China” and taunting him for his lack of money.
label: OBJ

text: Qi, who along with his family had been staying in Exeter, explained, in a call that began at 2.54pm and lasted 20 minutes, that his bank accounts had been frozen by the Chinese authorities, leaving him unable to pay for accommodation for his wife and children.
label: OBJ

text: According to Qi, the interpreter – who spoke with a mainland Chinese accent – interrupted the conversation to challenge him.
label: OBJ

text: “China is so good, why did you come out?”
label: SUBJ

text: the interpreter allegedly asked.
label: OBJ

text: “You came out to claim political asylum?
label: SUBJ

text: You brought your children out here to suffer.”
label: SUBJ

text: When Qi attempted to convey the desperation of his situation, he claims the interpreter refused to pass the message to the police representative on the call.
label: OBJ

text: “I will not translate your emotions,” the interpreter said, according to Qi.
label: OBJ

text: “On what grounds should the British help you?
label: SUBJ

text: If you have money, it is convenient everywhere.”
label: OBJ

text: On 21 January, 22 days after he made a complaint, Devon and Cornwall police informed Qi, who has recently been granted asylum in the UK, that responsibility for the interpreter lay with a contractor that is paid £130,000 a year to provide translation services.
label: OBJ

text: The contractor did not respond to a request for comment.
label: OBJ

text: The force has failed to provide a copy of the recording to Qi despite requests.
label: OBJ

text: The Information Commissioner’s Office has noted the force’s breach and issued a reprimand.
label: OBJ

text: Qi’s allegation will add to fears of the widespread infiltration of the Chinese interpreting community by the United Front Work Department, an organ of the communist regime in China that is said to seek to suppress political dissent and shape opinion abroad.
label: SUBJ

text: A Home Office-sponsored report that was declassified in February pinpointed the dearth of Chinese language skills within the police as a risk given the attempts at infiltration of the interpreting community by the Chinese Communist party and organised crime.
label: OBJ

text: The report’s author, David Wilson, a regional coordinator for the organised immigration crime domestic taskforce at West Midlands police and a former detective inspector, said there was “a lot of compromise” of Mandarin interpreters in the UK.
label: OBJ

text: He said: “The United Workers Department will co-opt everybody.
label: OBJ

text: We have had it that people will absolutely not talk in front of interpreters.
label: SUBJ

text: So we have had compromise.
label: OBJ

text: This is not unusual.
label: OBJ

text: The compromise will come both from the Chinese state and organised crime groups.”
label: OBJ

text: Wilson said a solution was to increase the number of Mandarin speakers within British policing.
label: OBJ

text: His report noted that officers were at times having to rely on Google Translate to do their work.
label: OBJ

text: During the recent trial of two men convicted of spying for China – including a UK Border Force officer – the court heard that eight suspects arrested in May 2024 as part of the alleged see spy ring had to be released after a shortage of interpreters meant the contents of their phones and laptops could not be translated before the 14-day statutory detention limit was reached.
label: OBJ

text: The suspects later left the UK.
label: OBJ

text: Qi said he had been left at his lowest ebb after the conversation with the police interpreter.
label: OBJ

text: “To me, she was clearly pro-regime,” he said.
label: OBJ

text: “The police need to deal with this.”
label: OBJ

text: Qi’s protest last August in Chongqing, a city of 30 million people, was staged on the eve of a major military parade and involved the projection on to a building of slogans such as “Only without the Communist party can there be a new China”.
label: OBJ

text: The police soon after found the source of the projection in a hotel room.
label: OBJ

text: Qi later released video footage of five police officers entering the hotel room, rushing to the window and finding the projector hidden behind a half-closed curtain.
label: OBJ

text: Qi, who was operating the projector and surveillance camera from London where he had arrived four days earlier, had left a handwritten letter on the coffee table addressed to the officers.
label: OBJ

text: “Even if you are a beneficiary of the system today, one day you will inevitably become a victim on this land,” it read.
label: OBJ

text: One of Qi’s posts was watched by 18 million people in four days.
label: OBJ

text: Sam Dunning, director of the research body UK-China Transparency saiid: “After WW2, Britain trained thousands in Russian language skills.
label: OBJ

text: Faced by what successive governments all agree are major challenges and threats from China, nothing remotely on this scale is being done today.”
label: SUBJ

text: A Devon and Cornwall police spokesperson said: “The Devon and Cornwall police professional standards department carefully reviewed the complaint, but as the interpreter was employed by a third party and not the force, no further action was taken.”
label: OBJ

text: President Donald Trump boldly claimed Saturday he answered all the questions correctly on his cognitive test amid ongoing concerns over his mental acuity and sharpness.
label: SUBJ

text: Trump wrote in a Truth Social post.

text: In typical Trump fashion, he fired off at Democrats.

text: “Are the Dumocrats really surprised?
label: SUBJ

text: In fact, this is my fourth such test, all PERFECT or, 120 correct answers out of 120 questions asked!
label: SUBJ

text: It is very rare that anyone gets a Perfect Score, especially when achieved four times in a row.

text: All people running for President and Vice President should be forced to take high difficulty Cognitive Tests.

text: Trump took his latest cognitive test on May 26 as part of his annual medical examination at Walter Reed National Military Medical Center.

text: The Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) that he took during his visit is used by doctors to identify signs of cognitive decline and dementia.
label: OBJ

text: Despite Trump alleging that his score indicates “extreme intelligence,” medical experts have said the MoCA does not test for IQ.
label: OBJ

text: Trump’s visit marked his third scheduled medical appointment at Walter Reed in 13 months, ABC News reports.
label: OBJ

text: On Friday, the White House released a memo recapping the results of Trump’s medical examination, noting that Trump’s “cognitive function” was assessed with the MoCA and found to be “within normal limits” with a perfect score.
label: OBJ

text: However, CNN medical analyst Dr. Jonathan Reiner challenged Trump’s results on Friday, telling CNN’s Abby Phillip that the test is “not really that useful when you take it over and over and over again, because the questions don’t change that much.”
label: OBJ

text: “Once you’ve taken it now four times, it’s probably not that difficult to pretty easily get through it.
label: OBJ

text: It shouldn’t be hard to get through it anyway, because it’s a dementia screening test, it’s not an IQ test,” he added.
label: OBJ

text: Reiner also questioned in a Saturday X post why the president was given another exam so soon when he’s had three in the recent past.

text: Former Liberal senator Linda Reynolds has paid back nearly $8,800 in parliamentary expenses after her partner claimed family reunion allowances to visit his son in Melbourne while en route to meet her in Canberra.
label: OBJ

text: Reynolds accepted the ruling of the Independent Parliamentary Expenses Authority (IPEA) but initially disputed their ruling, saying her partner’s visits to his son – her stepson – were necessary after “high profile (and false) 2021 workplace allegations made against me”, seemingly a reference to her long-running court dispute with former staffer Brittany Higgins.
label: OBJ

text: The IPEA, which administers the use of political spending and travel, last week published a number of recent reviews into parliamentarians’ expenses.
label: OBJ

text: Labor MP Louise Miller-Frost repaid more than $4,100 after travelling to Perth with her husband several days before a committee hearing, while Liberal senator Jacinta Nampijinpa Price repaid $387 after blaming an “oversight” for claiming an extra day of travel allowance.
label: OBJ

text: Reynolds, a former defence minister who left parliament at the May 2025 federal election, was required to repay $8,778.01 after three trips her partner took between Perth, Melbourne and Canberra in May and June 2025.
label: OBJ

text: In a statement to [OUTLET] Australia, Reynolds said she repaid the amount, which included a 25% penalty loading.
label: OBJ

text: “At the time of undertaking the travel I believed it was within the rules.
label: OBJ

text: IPEA took a different view which I accept,” she said.
label: OBJ

text: In a September 2025 email to Reynolds, the IPEA raised concern that on each occasion, her partner “appears to have stayed for two nights in Melbourne without you, before joining you in Canberra”, adding: “there are no provisions in the legislation for personal stopovers when travelling for family reunion purposes”.
label: OBJ

text: The IPEA found, in its review published online, that three trips between Perth and Melbourne were “not to accompany or join Ms Reynolds” but instead “for the predominant purpose, as stated by Ms Reynolds in her correspondence, of spending time with their son.”
label: OBJ

text: The IPEA said family travel entitlements were for “spending time and facilitating family life with the member, not broader family,” ruling all three trips to Melbourne did not meet the guidelines.
label: OBJ

text: It found the subsequent travel from Melbourne to Canberra, however, was consistent with the legislation.
label: OBJ

text: Parliamentarians are entitled to “family reunion” travel benefits, allowing close family members to join them on occasion when travelling away from home – for example, to Canberra for parliament sitting, or other locations for committee hearings.
label: OBJ

text: IPEA advice states a spouse or nominee is entitled to the equivalent of nine business class return airfares from their home city to Canberra, and an additional three business return airfares outside of Canberra.
label: OBJ

text: “Family reunion” travel is permitted only if the family member is travelling to accompany or join the parliamentarian.
label: OBJ

text: In correspondence to the IPEA, also published in the review, Reynolds stated her partner travelled to join her in Canberra “via stopovers in Melbourne to see and stay with his son… my (defacto) stepson”.
label: OBJ

text: “As [redacted] is my partner’s son and an integral member of our family, I believe that [her partner] stopping over in Melbourne on his way to Canberra was clearly to facilitate our family life and therefore meets requirement (c) of the Regulations.
label: SUBJ

text: Given work my commitments I was not always able to take the time to do so on my way to Canberra.
label: OBJ

text: However, we believed it was important for [her partner] to do so,” Reynolds wrote.
label: OBJ

text: “This was particularly important given the high profile (and false) 2021 workplace allegations made against me (and subsequently [redacted]).
label: SUBJ

text: They were highly traumatic for all our family members and [her partner] and I were concerned to ensure that [her stepson] and his sister were ok and that our family unit remained strong.”
label: OBJ

text: [OUTLET] Australia asked Reynolds if this was a reference to her various court proceedings related to former employee Higgins, who was ruled to have defamed her ex-boss when she accused Reynolds of mishandling her rape allegation.
label: OBJ

text: Reynolds did not immediately respond.
label: OBJ

text: The IPEA, in a letter to Reynolds, wrote that her partner was allowed to travel between Perth and Canberra, but that travel rules don’t “make provision for breaks in the journey or ‘stopovers’.”
label: OBJ

text: The senator responded that “it did not cost the taxpayer any extra to make the stopover”, asking for a review and later calling the IPEA’s position “patently ridiculous”.
label: OBJ

text: “[Her partner] travelled to Canberra to be reunited with me and there was no additional cost to the taxpayer.
label: OBJ

text: Even if there had been, surely what would be repayable should be any cost difference with the direct flight from Perth to Canberra (which can be more expensive),” Reynolds wrote.
label: OBJ

text: Miller-Frost repaid $4,183 after travelling to Perth with her husband in January 2023, three days before a committee hearing.
label: OBJ

text: In correspondence to IPEA, the Labor MP said she travelled earlier to “acclimatise” to “the significant time difference between Adelaide and Perth”, saying she was “exhausted” after a busy 2022.
label: OBJ

text: The Labor MP did not claim travel allowance for the extra days before the hearing, but IPEA ruled she should only have travelled on the day of, or before, her parliamentary business began.
label: OBJ

text: She repaid the cost of the airfares, with a 25% penalty loading.
label: OBJ

text: After a Memorial Day break, Congress returns to the same problems they left behind
label: SUBJ

label: OBJ

text: Members of Congress are back this week after their Memorial Day break, but the problems they left behind have not gotten any easier.

label: OBJ

text: He just made this fund.

text: And Republicans don't want to take votes on those amendments - amendments like that - if they can get the president to drop this fund entirely.

text: For decades, Australia’s property market has been defined by relentless price rises, reinforcing the old adage that real estate investment is “as safe as houses”.
label: SUBJ

text: There’s now a wrinkle in that wisdom.
label: SUBJ

text: Data from the three weekends since Labor handed down its budget suggest investors are not competing as vigorously at auctions after being told they won’t be able to negatively gear newly bought established properties beyond mid-next year.
label: SUBJ

text: Auction clearance rates have dropped, home prices in several of Australia’s capital cities have begun to fall, and some analysts now expect price declines of up to 10%.
label: OBJ

text: There are, of course, contributing factors to price movements and forecasts beyond the budget reforms, namely rising interest rates, stretched household budgets and a pessimistic global economic outlook.
label: OBJ

text: It’s a perfect storm, or alignment of stars, depending on whether you want prices to go down or up.
label: SUBJ

text: Any drop in the property market would not, however, show there is a problem with the tax reforms; it would more accurately expose the policy failings of three decades that encouraged unchecked price growth and an affordability crunch.
label: SUBJ

text: ‘Get a fair crack at auctions’
label: OBJ

text: Labor’s capital gains discount and negative gearing changes are already prompting a shift in investor behaviour.
label: OBJ

text: Instead of relying on tax-enhanced speculation, investors are now compelled to evaluate established properties based on financial metrics, such as rental yield and growth prospects.
label: OBJ

text: The changes are reintroducing a level of responsible decision-making to a property market so often marked by speculative bidding.
label: SUBJ

text: Reports from recent auctions suggest this transition is already under way, with observers noting unfamiliar scenes where most of the buyers actually want to live in the home they are bidding on.
label: OBJ

text: Last weekend, clearance rates – the percentage of properties successfully sold – across state capitals fell below 55%, the lowest since April 2020, with Sydney and Brisbane the weakest, according to Cotality data.
label: OBJ

text: This indicates the market is cooling and that there is a standoff between buyers and sellers over what properties are worth.
label: SUBJ

text: Jim Chalmers noted on Monday that while there were a “number of factors at play” in the clearance rates, “if we are making it easier for first home buyers to get a fair crack at auctions, then that’s a good thing”.
label: OBJ

text: The difficulty with reforms is that someone always loses when the status quo is upended.
label: SUBJ

text: Some first homeowners will have recently poured their bank account into a purchase to outbid an investor who wouldn’t have been interested in the property without the favourable tax settings that are now being erased.
label: SUBJ

text: Those homeowners may now be worried about winding up in negative equity, which occurs when the market value of a property falls below the size of the home loan.
label: OBJ

text: If Labor’s reforms weigh on prices already subdued by rising interest rates, homeowners will naturally cut their spending because they feel poorer, with knock-on effects on other parts of the economy, such as hospitality.
label: SUBJ

text: There are also many tax and property consulting businesses that have been built around a negative gearing regime now being unwound.
label: OBJ

text: On the flip side, the pre-budget system was fuelling inequity in Australia, and the longer it went on, the worse it got, with the proportion of housing stock owned by investors relentlessly rising over the last 25 years to the detriment of people who just wanted to live in their own home.
label: SUBJ

text: That trend should start to reverse – and that’s a good thing. ‘That never happens’
label: SUBJ

text: ‘That never happens’ While improved affordability does not necessarily require property prices to fall – they just need to rise at a slower pace than incomes – it will require prices to be lower than they would have been under the pre-budget regime.
label: OBJ

text: The changes should also lead to less housing turnover, or churn, resulting in a more stable market.
label: SUBJ

text: Some will be unhappy about that and fight the tax changes fiercely.
label: SUBJ

text: Michael Fotheringham, the managing director of the Australian Housing and Urban Research Institute, says property market reforms always attract critics who say the sky is falling.
label: OBJ

text: “Every reform does mean some disruption, but before every reform in this sector, we hear it will lead to market collapse, and that we’re ruining the nation,” he says.
label: OBJ

text: “Extraordinarily, that never happens.”
label: SUBJ

text: Indeed, there are at least two reasons that prices in Australia won’t fall too far.
label: SUBJ

text: First, there is still a large housing shortfall, which has been the single biggest price driver of recent decades.
label: OBJ

text: Second, there are various demand-side measures including the government’s expanded first home buyer 5% deposit scheme.
label: OBJ

text: Even if prices were to correct – defined as a drop of at least 10% – home ownership will remain out of reach for many people even if they have a good job, given the severity of affordability issues in Australia.
label: SUBJ

text: The recent market quivers have however raised a question over whether Australia’s “super cycle upswing” over the last 30 years has come to an end.
label: OBJ

text: AMP chief economist Shane Oliver says such predictions may be premature until the supply shortfall comes under control.
label: OBJ

text: In other words, until Australia starts building enough homes, it will remain unaffordable for most, and the real estate market will remain “as safe as houses”, even after the tax reforms.
label: SUBJ

text: How aid cuts are hampering the frontline response to the Ebola crisis
label: SUBJ

text: Porous borders, misinformation and aid cuts pose challenges for fighting Ebola
label: OBJ

text: A large Ebola outbreak in central Africa is spreading, and misinformation about the virus is making matters worse.
label: OBJ

text: Rumors on social media claim that Ebola is not real or that health care workers are out to profit for themselves.
label: OBJ

text: More than 1,000 suspected and confirmed cases have been recorded, with at least 223 deaths suspected of being caused by Ebola, according to the World Health Organization.
label: OBJ

text: Health workers say that's likely a major undercount.
label: OBJ

text: The epicenter of the outbreak is in the Democratic Republic of Congo.
label: OBJ

text: Uganda, which borders the DRC, has so far been spared the worst of the outbreak.
label: OBJ

text: On May 27, the country closed its official border crossings with Congo.
label: OBJ

text: "We still have a number of porous border points … whereby people continue to cross over," said Leonard Musinguzi.
label: OBJ

text: He's a community and surveillance officer for the International Rescue Committee in Uganda.
label: OBJ

text: Musinguzi's job is to track likely cases of Ebola, quarantine refugees, train healthcare workers and prepare his community to battle the disease.
label: OBJ

text: That's an uphill battle, especially because wrong information about Ebola can spread even faster than a virus.
label: SUBJ

text: One of the ways Musinguzi tries to combat that misinformation is public health messaging.
label: OBJ

text: His organization distributes radio spots, posters, and information on hospital televisions meant to educate about the disease.
label: OBJ

text: However, governments like the United States have cut back their support for programs like the IRC's.
label: OBJ

text: That means Musinguzi has less money for the projects he wants to do.
label: OBJ

text: Before, he might have paid to place educational messages during five radio talk shows.
label: OBJ

text: Now, he said, "because of this reduced funding, you only have one."
label: OBJ

text: In a statement to [OUTLET], the State Department said recent federal funding changes did not have any significant effect on U.S. funding levels for global health programs or health security programs in the eastern DRC.
label: OBJ

text: Spokesman Tommy Pigott said, "the United States responded within 24 hours of the first confirmed case, mobilizing a wide range of medical, humanitarian, operational, and consular resources to rapidly respond to the Ebola outbreak."
label: OBJ

text: [OUTLET]'s Adrian Florido spoke with aid workers and a former United States Agency for International Development employee to learn more about the pressures facing the global health system, and how federal government cuts may have contributed.
label: OBJ

text: Mainland Europe has always been a springboard for those in the creative industries, from gaining crucial first credits on a TV, film or theatre production to building a marketable resume and paying the bills while attempting to make it big in the UK or US.

text: Democrats and Republicans think control of Congress runs through Iowa
label: OBJ

text: CLIVE, Iowa — There's very little Democrats and Republicans seem to agree on these days, but Iowa's importance to their political futures is one exception.
label: OBJ

text: Tuesday's primary elections will kick off a sprint to November that includes three competitive House races and a Senate race that will help decide control of each chamber of Congress.
label: OBJ

text: The open governor's race is one of a handful in the country that experts say could change hands this fall.
label: OBJ

text: Iowa is a state where the institutional strengths and weaknesses of Democrats and Republicans alike are on heightened display in a midterm election year.
label: OBJ

text: President Trump faces record-low approval ratings, rising gas prices, an unpopular war in Iran and affordability concerns.
label: OBJ

text: His grip on the Republican Party is stronger than ever, with the few remaining Congressional lawmakers that have notably crossed him ousted in recent primaries.
label: SUBJ

text: The national Democratic Party brand is also historically unpopular, even as Democrats continue to see rising enthusiasm in primary election turnout, overperformances in special elections and a polling advantage five months out from Election Day.

text: So how does the national mood map onto Iowa's elections?

text: "They're coming after Iowa.

text: They want to turn Iowa blue."

text: A look ahead to 2028
label: OBJ

text: Iowa's midterm elections could heavily influence the final two years of Trump's second term in office, and both parties assert that this leads to even more importance in the first wide-open 2028 presidential contest since the 2016 cycle.

text: Immigrant detainees sue over 'horrific' conditions at Texas ICE facility
label: OBJ

text: Four detainees at the largest Immigration and Customs Enforcement detention center in the U.S. filed a federal lawsuit on Saturday alleging human rights abuses, "horrific" conditions and "severe medical neglect" at the facility.
label: OBJ

text: The complaint, filed in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Texas, details "inhumane" treatment inside Camp East Montana on the U.S. Army's Fort Bliss military base in El Paso, Texas.
label: OBJ

text: The suit describes a litany of abuse allegations, including a lack of medical care and physical violence at the hands of guards, and accuses the government of human rights and constitutional violations.
label: OBJ

text: "Detained people are regularly subjected to severe beatings or sexual harassment by guards; squalid living conditions; spoiled and inadequate food; no meaningful programming or recreation; inadequate access to basic hygiene products such as soap, razors or nail clippers, outbreaks of disease; and limited or no access to sunlight," according to the complaint.
label: OBJ

text: It's the first lawsuit against the detention center.
label: OBJ

text: Immigration advocates and former detainees have been calling for the massive facility to be shut down for months.
label: OBJ

text: The plaintiffs filed the suit on behalf of themselves, all detainees of the facility and future people held there.
label: OBJ

text: They're seeking class-action status for the legal challenge.
label: OBJ

text: According to the complaint, the center's guards beat Gerald Akari Angye, one of the named plaintiffs, so severely he had to be hospitalized and placed in a wheelchair.
label: OBJ

text: Angye, who has been at Camp East Montana for just over a month, claims he was then locked in solitary confinement for 15 days.
label: OBJ

text: "No human being should ever have to go through this," Angye said in a statement released by the American Civil Liberties Union, one of the organizations representing the detainees.
label: OBJ

text: "I have already experienced torture in my home country of Cameroon and I never thought I would experience such severely violent treatment by guards here in the United States of America."
label: OBJ

text: Another detainee identified in the complaint only as Navdeep, a former mail handler with no criminal history, says he experienced dirty toilet water flowing into his sleeping area, difficulty accessing cups for drinking water and breathing problems because of excessive dust from the desert.
label: OBJ

text: Navdeep wore the same clothes, including underwear, for three weeks, according to the lawsuit.
label: OBJ

text: "We could die here, and it feels like no one here would care," Navdeep said in the ACLU statement.
label: OBJ

text: People being held at Camp East Montana don't receive timely medications to manage a range of serious medical issues such as HIV, cancer and diabetes, according to the lawsuit.
label: OBJ

text: In February, the detention center was closed temporarily to visitors because of a measles outbreak, according to Marfa Public Radio.
label: OBJ

text: The complaint also describes housing units without windows, crammed spaces, a constant odor of urine and feces, a lack of clean water and reports that detainees receive only two pieces of bread, a piece of ham or bologna, a slice of cheese and a cookie for all three meals.
label: OBJ

text: ICE Director Todd Lyons and Department of Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin are named as defendants in the lawsuit.
label: OBJ

text: In an email to [OUTLET], DHS Acting Assistant Secretary Lauren Bis rebutted the claims in the lawsuit, saying they are "categorically false."
label: OBJ

text: The email read: "ICE is regularly audited and inspected by external agencies to ensure that all ICE facilities comply with performance-based national detention standards.
label: OBJ

text: All detainees are provided with proper meals, quality water, blankets, medical treatment, and have opportunities to communicate with their family members and lawyers.
label: OBJ

text: ICE has higher detention standards than most U.S. prisons that hold actual U.S. citizens."
label: SUBJ

text: Camp East Montana is a sprawling encampment of tents in the Chihuahuan Desert opened in 2025.
label: OBJ

text: It has the capacity to hold up to 5,000 people but usually houses about 3,000.

text: At least three people have died at the center, including Cuban national Gerald Lunas Campos, according to previous [OUTLET] reporting.

text: The El Paso County Medical Examiner's Office ruled Campos' death a homicide and no one has been charged.

text: In February, ICE found 49 violations to detention standards at the center, including inadequate medical care and failure from staff to "accurately document required checks to prevent significant self-harm and suicide."
