text: The following is an installment of “On This Day,” a series celebrating America’s 250th anniversary by following the actions of Gen. George Washington, the Continental Congress, and the men and women whose bravery and sacrifice led up to the signing of the Declaration of Independence. June 1, 1776
label: SUBJ

text: June 1, 1776 After much deliberation, including a week of persuasion and stark reports on army needs from Gen. George Washington, the Second Continental Congress finally decides to send thousands of militia reinforcements from Massachusetts, Connecticut, New Hampshire, and New York to Canada.
label: OBJ

text: Maj. Gen. Lord Stirling writes to Washington in Philadelphia from the Hudson Heights.
label: OBJ

text: Stirling has inspected Fort Montgomery and Fort Constitution and warns Washington that they are undermanned, chaotic, and vulnerable.
label: OBJ

text: Stirling had served as a supply officer during the French and Indian War, where he first met Washington.
label: OBJ

text: He would eventually assume command of the Northern Army during the war.
label: OBJ

text: His most significant military contribution would come in August 1776 when he held off the British troops during the Battle of Long Island long enough to enable Washington to evacuate the remainder of his forces.
label: OBJ

text: As a result of these actions, Stirling was captured by the British and spent several months as a prisoner on parole in New York City.

text: More than 1 in 10 U.S. adults is currently taking a GLP-1 weight-loss drug.
label: OBJ

text: And these medicines are getting cheaper seemingly by the day.
label: SUBJ

text: Since Wegovy launched, its list price has fallen from roughly $1,300 per month to between $300 and $500 per month.
label: OBJ

text: Similarly, Zepbound was once about $1,000 per month, but it now costs about $350.
label: OBJ

text: The cash prices, for patients not paying with insurance, are even lower.
label: OBJ

text: These price drops are no accident.
label: SUBJ

text: They’re the result of intense competition between rival drug companies — and strong patent protections.
label: OBJ

text: OPINION: AMERICA PAYS TOO MUCH FOR MEDICINE. HERE’S
label: SUBJ

text: HERE’S WHO IS DOING SOMETHING ABOUT IT
label: SUBJ

text: That might sound counterintuitive.
label: OBJ

text: After all, critics of the pharmaceutical industry often claim that drug companies game the patent system to prevent competition and keep prices high.
label: OBJ

text: It’s a simple, intuitive narrative.
label: SUBJ

text: But it’s detached from the reality of pharmaceutical innovation.
label: SUBJ

text: In reality, patents don’t give the inventor monopoly pricing power — they encourage market entry.
label: SUBJ

text: Patents give innovators and investors the security they need to pursue expensive, risky, and time-consuming research.
label: SUBJ

text: If inventors make a breakthrough, they know they’ll be able to protect their discoveries, sell them exclusively, and, hopefully, recoup their costs of development and earn a return on investment.
label: OBJ

text: When a company demonstrates that a particular molecular mechanism has the potential to safely and effectively treat a condition, competitors see that a viable market could exist for treatments in that area.
label: OBJ

text: Other companies can then attempt to develop alternative treatments that don’t infringe on existing patents.
label: OBJ

text: Frequently, that means designing different molecules to target the same pathway or related biological mechanism.
label: OBJ

text: As a result, we often see multiple patented drugs compete within therapeutic classes.
label: OBJ

text: And as more competitors enter the field, prices begin to fall.
label: OBJ

text: GLP-1s are hardly the only example of this phenomenon.
label: SUBJ

text: Consider direct-acting antivirals for Hepatitis C. When Sovaldi — the first oral direct-acting antiviral for the fatal liver disease — received Food and Drug Administration approval in 2013, drug manufacturer Gilead charged about $84,000 for a 12-week course.
label: OBJ

text: But other companies saw the opportunity to develop similar treatments, and within a few years, multiple direct-acting antivirals had entered the market.
label: OBJ

text: One study revealed that competition between these products reduced the average cost per treatment course to $55,500 by 2015 and all the way down to $14,400 by 2019.
label: OBJ

text: Prices dropped by 83% over a 5-year period — all while these direct-acting antivirals were under patent protection.
label: OBJ

text: Or consider PCSK9 inhibitors, which help lower cholesterol levels.
label: OBJ

text: In 2015, two PCSK9 inhibitors received FDA approval in the United States.
label: OBJ

text: Praluent launched with a list price of around $14,600 per year; Repatha launched at a list price of around $14,100 per year.
label: OBJ

text: Within just a few years, prices had fallen dramatically.
label: OBJ

text: In 2018, Praluent cost between $4,500 and $8,000 per year.
label: OBJ

text: Meanwhile, Repatha had cost about $5,850 per year — and now, it’s available for about $3,000 per year.
label: OBJ

text: That’s a nearly 80% price reduction.
label: OBJ

text: And once again, it happened while both drugs remained under patent protection. OPINION:
label: SUBJ

text: OPINION: TRUMP’S TWO-FRONT FIGHT TO LOWER DRUG PRICES IS ALREADY WORKING
label: SUBJ

text: In other words, market conditions, of which patents are only one part, determine drug pricing.
label: OBJ

text: Patent protections incentivize innovation, which in turn sparks competition.
label: OBJ

text: And patents expire, after which copies can enter the market and prices can fall off a cliff.
label: SUBJ

text: But if drug companies couldn’t protect their ideas and designs from being copied for a period of time, they’d have no incentive to invest in research to attempt to enter the market in the first place.
label: SUBJ

text: And society wouldn’t have the GLP-1s, direct-acting antivirals, PCSK9 inhibitors, and countless other medicines we enjoy today — many of which have already had their patents expire.
label: SUBJ

label: SUBJ

text: AI, which is short for artificial intelligence, has become the latest next big thing.
label: SUBJ

text: NASA gives a short definition of AI as, “computer systems that can perform complex tasks normally done by human-reasoning, decision making, creating, etc.”

label: OBJ

label: OBJ

text: AI and robotics are busily enhancing every aspect of human life.
label: SUBJ

text: AI systems are helping doctors read scans to detect indications of diseases that might be missed by the human eye.
label: OBJ

text: They are also accelerating scientific discovery, finding cures for diseases, and other products.
label: OBJ

text: AI systems can become the basis for personalized education and customer service.
label: OBJ

text: They can optimize supply chains, reduce business costs, enhance productivity, and reduce fraud.
label: OBJ

text: Robots can take over repetitive and dangerous tasks, whether on the factory floor or even, as the Ukrainians recently proved, on the battlefield.
label: OBJ

text: In short, the advent of AI and robotics constitutes as profound a development as the industrial revolution of the 19th century and the information revolution of the 20th century.
label: SUBJ

text: Is there something to the fears being stoked by Bernie Sanders that the twin technologies of AI and robotics are going to wipe out millions of jobs?
label: SUBJ

text: According to Johns Hopkins, the answer is a little complicated.
label: OBJ

text: AI may well replace some jobs, such as paralegals and customer service reps.
label: SUBJ

text: Robots will not replace all factory workers or rideshare drivers, but they will replace many of them.
label: SUBJ

text: On the other hand, many jobs will be changed and enhanced.
label: OBJ

text: Computer programmers will no longer perform the mundane task of coding software, but they will interact with and guide the AI systems that do.
label: SUBJ

text: AI systems will not replace medical doctors but will greatly enhance their ability to diagnose diseases and treat patients.
label: OBJ

text: They will not replace teachers but will enhance their ability to teach students on a personalized basis.
label: OBJ

text: They will not even replace astronauts but will assist them in space exploration.
label: OBJ

text: Previous technological revolutions have wiped out some jobs but created many new ones.
label: OBJ

text: The Industrial Revolution wiped out a lot of agricultural jobs but created many more factory jobs.
label: OBJ

text: The Information Revolution wiped out many clerical and other jobs but created many more computer jobs.
label: OBJ

text: The AI and Robotics Revolution will have a similar effect.
label: SUBJ

text: Sanders’s idea of standing athwart the path of history yelling, “Stop!” is not going to be productive.
label: SUBJ

text: He is behaving like the early Luddites, breaking knitting machines in the early 19th century.
label: SUBJ

text: A better reaction to the new AI and Robotics Revolution would be to start an education program, both to train people in danger of being displaced and to prepare students for the future that awaits them.
label: SUBJ

text: AI will not replace jobs that require personal service, creativity, and empathy.
label: SUBJ

text: It will be a tool to enhance human productivity, not to replace it.

text: Humans will be in the loop at all times.

text: Movies such as 2001: A Space Odyssey, Colossus: The Forbin Project, and the Terminator films, in which AI systems go berserk, bereft of human guidance, demonstrate why human involvement is necessary.

text: Border Patrol agents said they nabbed 36 illegal immigrants who were driving tractor trailers on roads during a recent operation in Arizona, highlighting what the Trump administration has described as a wave of migrants getting behind the wheel of dangerous vehicles.
label: OBJ

text: Operation Checkmate was led by the Border Patrol’s Yuma sector and ran from May 11-15.
label: OBJ

text: Agents detected a total of 52 illegal immigrants during the operation, with 36 of them nabbed while driving semitrucks.
label: OBJ

text: “Operation Checkmate reflects our commitment to safeguarding communities and roads from unlawfully present drivers who pose significant risks to public safety,” said Dustin W. Caudle, acting chief patrol agent in Yuma.
label: OBJ

text: “My agents are on patrol every day to ensure we stop these individuals and prevent more deadly crashes from occurring on the road across the United States.”
label: OBJ

text: Of the 36 truck drivers, 29 of them actually had commercial driver’s licenses issued by states, even though the Trump administration says they weren’t supposed to.
label: OBJ

text: The government calls them non-domiciled drivers.
label: OBJ

text: Most of them were here on legally iffy Biden immigration leniency programs, such as “humanitarian parole.”
label: SUBJ

text: Despite that lack of firm status, a number of states — most notably California, Washington and New York — were willing to issue them commercial driver’s licenses.
label: OBJ

text: Some of those drivers have been involved in horrific accidents in recent months, drawing attention to the situation.
label: SUBJ

text: Florida had asked the Supreme Court to hear a case challenging Washington And California, citing the case of Harjinder Singh, who authorities said made an illegal U-turn across a median on the Florida Turnpike, crashing into a minivan and killing all three of its passengers.
label: OBJ

text: Mr. Singh, a citizen of India, obtained his license from Washington even though Florida said he couldn’t read English well enough to understand America’s road signs.
label: OBJ

text: The Supreme Court declined to hear Florida’s case.
label: OBJ

text: Indian nationals predominated among the Border Patrol Arizona arrests, accounting for 30 of them.
label: OBJ

text: The other six were from Mexico, El Salvador and Russia.
label: OBJ

text: Customs and Border Protection said most of them did have work permits issued during the Biden administration but which “are no longer valid.”
label: OBJ

text: CBP said all of those caught will be deported.
label: OBJ

text: Part of the problem, according to federal officials, is that the work permits are temporary, and conditional.
label: OBJ

text: When the Trump administration revoked Biden-era parole programs, it meant those permits were also suddenly invalid — though the cards themselves still had validity dates in the future.
label: OBJ

text: And some states issued licenses that stretched beyond the work permit expiration date anyway.
label: OBJ

text: The Transportation Department accused California and New York of doing just that.
label: OBJ

text: Last year, the department announced it was withholding some grant money from California until the state changed its practices and revoked thousands of already-issued licenses.
label: OBJ

text: And in April, the department issued a similar declaration against New York.
label: OBJ

text: Federal officials said a review of 200 non-domiciled drivers in New York found more than half were issued in violation of federal law.
label: OBJ

text: An artificial intelligence-powered software failed to notice that a nurse at a Tennessee hospital was stealing fentanyl for several months, CBS News first reported on Monday.
label: OBJ

text: The nurse later conceded that he had taken and misused leftover fentanyl from surgeries over the course of several months, in some cases daily, according to CBS News.
label: OBJ

text: The hospital relies on Sentri7, a medication-monitoring software that utilizes AI and is supposed to detect missing drugs faster than humans can, the outlet reported.
label: OBJ

text: Sentri7 was apparently unable to detect missing drugs at Erlanger for several months and also failed to notice other “inconsistencies” that “should have been flagged,” per the consent order.
label: OBJ

text: André Rebelo, a spokesperson for Wolters Kluwer — the Dutch technology company that created Sentri7 — declined to answer CBS News’ questions about what occurred at Erlanger but maintained that the company is “confident in our software.”
label: OBJ

text: Wolters Kluwer and Erlanger Baroness each did not respond to the [OUTLET]’s request for comment.
label: OBJ

text: U.S. healthcare facilities are notably not required to disclose whether they use this type of AI software or divulge any errors, CBS News reported.
label: OBJ

text: “The ideal for patients, caregivers, and [hospital] systems would be when an AI is found to be making some type of error, that becomes very transparent and public,” David Rastall, a Johns Hopkins Medicine neurologist and AI researcher, told CBS News.
label: OBJ

text: Johns Hopkins Hospital and Health System Medication Safety and Quality Director Jacob Smith told the outlet that he does not understand how AI-powered software like Sentri7 could miss the drug diversion that occurred at Erlanger.
label: OBJ

text: “I’ve never myself seen these technologies be called out in that specific way,” Smith said.
label: OBJ

text: “It doesn’t make sense to me how you could miss it.”
label: OBJ

text: Drug diversion refers to the illegal distribution or abuse of prescription drugs or their use for purposes not intended by the prescriber, according to a Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services fact sheet.
label: OBJ

text: An estimated 10-15% of all healthcare workers will abuse drugs or alcohol while working in the industry, according to Wolters Kluwer.
label: OBJ

text: About 10% of medical professionals will divert opioids or other substances at some point during their professional career,
label: OBJ

text: Fentanyl is currently the primary driver of drug overdose deaths in the U.S., according to the Government Accountability Office.
label: OBJ

text: The new report comes as the use of AI technology has become more widespread across the U.S. healthcare system in recent years.
label: OBJ

text: Experts previously told the [OUTLET] that AI can be a useful tool for things like cancer detection and analyzing medical data.
label: OBJ

text: Sunday at the Charles Schwab Challenge was a bit sleepy, but then Russell Henley woke up in a rather aggressive mood.
label: SUBJ

text: The 37-year-old flipped a switch down the stretch in Fort Worth to earn the sixth PGA Tour win of his career and pass quite a career milestone in the process.
label: SUBJ

text: Where there is a winner, there are also many losers, and for Eric Cole, he's essentially turned into the face of the latter group since earning his spot on the PGA Tour in 2023.

label: SUBJ

text: On the other side of the planet, Joaquin Niemann found the LIV Golf winner's circle yet again in South Korea.

text: Russell Henley Successfully Storms The Fort
label: SUBJ

text: The theme throughout the final round of the Charles Schwab Challenge on Sunday was that none of the half dozen or so players with a chance to win it wanted to go out and take it.
label: OBJ

text: But then, Russell Henley volunteered to do so when he stepped to the 16th tee while trailing by three shots.
label: OBJ

text: Down three with three to play makes the approach straightforward.
label: OBJ

text: Extremely difficult, but straightforward, and Henley went out and executed by closing his round out with three consecutive birdies to earn a spot in a playoff with Eric Cole.
label: OBJ

text: Henley knocked his tee shot on the par-3 16th hole to 15 feet and drained the birdie putt to officially grab hold of the momentum.
label: SUBJ

text: He hit his approach shot into the par-4 17th to 15 feet and his final approach on the par-4 18th to just outside the same range.
label: OBJ

text: For a player who has earned a bit of a reputation for lacking aggression down the stretch, the former Georgia Bulldog certainly had plenty of it on Sunday afternoon.
label: SUBJ

text: As for the playoff, it didn't exactly come as a surprise that all Henley needed was one hole to finish the job against Cole.
label: SUBJ

text: A driver and a wedge inside of five feet, the job was done, and Henley was hugging his three children and his wife as a PGA Tour winner for the first time in 14 months.
label: OBJ

text: In recent years, Henley has become a player who clearly enjoys difficult tests, and winning at Colonial with a score of just 12-under checks that box.
label: SUBJ

text: With his victory in Fort Worth, Henley passed the career earnings mark of $50 million.
label: OBJ

text: Notable players he's surpassed on the career earnings list are Ernie Els, Bubba Watson, Tommy Fleetwood, Tony Finau and Luke Donald.
label: OBJ

text: He may be the most unsuspecting $50 million winner in PGA Tour history, which is probably exactly the way he wants it.
label: SUBJ

text: Eric Cole's Nightmare Isn't All That Bad
label: SUBJ

text: Eric Cole is a unique player.
label: SUBJ

text: He's 37 years old, is a journeyman in professional golf, has won a dozen times on mini tours, yet remains winless on the PGA Tour despite earning Rookie of the Year honors in 2023.
label: OBJ

text: With that resume as a guy nearing 40, he's earned the reputation of being a perennial loser, and while he's not a winner, the criticism he receives feels a bit harsh.
label: SUBJ

text: Cole is in the middle of just his fourth season on the PGA Tour, and this past week's Charles Schwab Challenge marked his 120th start on Tour.
label: OBJ

text: In that time, he's made 84 cuts, finished runner-up three times, has 41 Top 25 finishes and has earned $13.5 million.
label: OBJ

text: It's safe to assume he'd trade in a couple million bucks to turn one of his second-place finishes into a victory, but it's not as if Cole is living in some sort of endless, unsuccessful hellscape inside the ropes.
label: SUBJ

text: Sunday's playoff loss to Henley was the second time in his young-ish Tour career that he's had to witness his opponent birdie the first extra hole to slam the door shut.
label: SUBJ

text: Chris Kirk gave him the same treatment at the 2023 Honda Classic.
label: OBJ

text: Cole didn't have his best stuff on Sunday, hitting just 10 greens in regulation, but still carded an even-par round of 70 to give himself a chance to earn his first win on Tour.
label: OBJ

text: He's more aware than anyone out there that even-par rounds on Sunday very rarely get the job done, and certainly understands he got beat by a guy who simply got scorching hot down the stretch.
label: SUBJ

text: It won't make the sting hurt less, but that's golf sometimes.
label: OBJ

text: Joaquin Niemann Flips After Yet Another Win On LIV
label: SUBJ

text: LIV Golf, or at least this version of LIV Golf, may not exist in just a few months, and out of all the players on the circuit, Joaquin Niemann may be the most upset about it.
label: SUBJ

text: The Chilean beat Talor Gooch in a playoff to win LIV Korea over the weekend for his eighth individual title since joining the circuit in 2022.
label: OBJ

text: Those eight titles are two more than any other LIV player, and while he's not the first, second, or maybe even fifth player fans think of when it comes to LIV, he certainly should be.
label: SUBJ

text: The loudest LIV critics out there will try claiming that Niemann's eight wins aren't noteworthy, but winning professional golf tournaments, regardless of the fact that they are no-cut events, is not easy.
label: SUBJ

text: Doing so eight times in less than five years is wildly impressive.
label: SUBJ

text: Maybe the scariest, and definitely most alarming fact about Niemann, is that he's still just 27 years old.
label: SUBJ

text: If (when) LIV does officially go belly up, Niemann's next move will be among the most intriguing to follow.
label: SUBJ

text: As a two-time winner on the PGA Tour and former 15th-ranked player on the planet, there is no denying he has the game to make serious noise on the game's biggest stages, but has shockingly failed to do so throughout his entire career.

text: In his 28 major championship starts, he's finished inside the top 10 only once, which was a T-8 at the 2025 PGA Championship.

text: The sample size is large, the results are very poor, but he still has all the time in the world on his side, and what he does with it over the next decade will be an interesting watch.

text: Former Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell on Sunday warned against politicizing the central bank, amid concerns President Donald Trump is undermining the institution’s prized independence.
label: OBJ

text: Powell’s speech while accepting the John F. Kennedy Profile in Courage Award in Boston came after he suggested that the Justice Department’s investigation into concerns he lied or misrepresented the taxpayer-funded, multibillion-dollar renovation of the Fed headquarters building in Washington was politically motivated.
label: OBJ

text: This week, Powell reiterated his call for officials to respect the central bank’s independence, saying that “like many other institutions, the Fed has been undergoing a stress test,” during one of his first major public appearances since leaving office.
label: OBJ

text: “Congress wisely chose to insulate monetary policy decisions from political pressure.
label: SUBJ

text: All other advanced economy nations have done the same,” Powell said during remarks at the John F. Kennedy Presidential Library.
label: OBJ

text: “Democratic institutions take much time, effort, and patience to build but can be torn down all too quickly,” he added.
label: OBJ

text: “It is essential that we preserve what is good about these institutions, even as we strive to improve them.”
label: OBJ

text: Powell’s remarks came after he claimed the DOJ’s inquiry, which the agency ended in April, stemmed from Trump’s public anger that Powell declined to lower interest rates.
label: OBJ

text: “The threat of criminal charges is a consequence of the Federal Reserve setting interest rates based on our best assessment of what will serve the public, rather than following the preferences of the President,” Powell said in a rare statement in January, which referenced the investigation.

text: LOS ANGELES — With less than 24 hours to go before Tuesday’s primary, the top three leading Los Angeles mayoral candidates spent the weekend fanning out across neighborhoods to make their final pitches to voters still weighing their options.
label: OBJ

text: Mayor Karen Bass, a Democrat, who is polling ahead of the other candidates, packed her schedule with campaign stops, appearing alongside union workers and California Attorney General Rob Bonta to energize voters and highlight endorsements in the race’s closing stretch.
label: OBJ

text: The incumbent’s blitz also included stops in Eagle Rock and Van Nuys, underscoring her effort to leverage labor support and her governing record as she seeks another term.
label: SUBJ

text: “We’ve laid a great foundation over three years, and now we need to bring it home,” she said Sunday.
label: OBJ

text: Bass secured a late endorsement from Gov. Gavin Newsom (D-CA), who is term-limited, but he has not campaigned alongside her.
label: OBJ

text: She’s also picked up endorsements from both of California’s U.S. senators, Adam Schiff (D-CA) and Alex Padilla (D-CA), as well as former Vice President Kamala Harris.
label: OBJ

text: There are 14 candidates on the ballot, though only five are viewed as serious contenders.
label: OBJ

text: Of those, Councilwoman Nithya Raman and reality star Spencer Pratt are Bass’s biggest threats.
label: OBJ

text: Under California’s top-two primary system, the two highest vote-getters advance to the general election regardless of party affiliation if no candidate secures more than 50% of the vote.
label: OBJ

text: Bass dismissed her main rivals in the still wide-open race, saying she was the “clear choice.”
label: OBJ

text: “We want somebody that has proven and delivered versus somebody who has a lot of theory and wonderful ideas,” she said.
label: OBJ

text: “The city needs to have the job done … and then there is a guy that functions in fantasy world.”
label: SUBJ

text: The latest polling points to a tight contest with Bass holding a narrow edge over Raman and Pratt.
label: OBJ

text: Bass had 26% support among likely voters, compared with 25% for Raman and 22% for Pratt, according to a UC Berkeley Institute of Governmental Studies/Los Angeles Times poll released Thursday.
label: OBJ

text: Bass’s lead falls within the survey’s 3% margin of error.
label: OBJ

text: For months, the mayor appeared firmly in control of the race, with analysts largely assuming she would cruise into the Nov. 3 runoff against either Raman or Pratt.
label: SUBJ

text: The latest polling of 1,913 registered voters suggests the contest has tightened considerably, leaving all three candidates within striking distance of a runoff.

text: The poll, conducted between May 19 and May 24, is the largest sample of any public poll released ahead of the primary election.
label: OBJ

text: The French Navy, with backing from the U.K., intercepted a sanctioned oil tanker traveling from Russia, French President Emmanuel Macron announced Monday, as Europe looks to clamp down on Moscow’s energy profits.
label: OBJ

text: In a social media post, Mr. Macron said French forces intercepted the Tagor, a ship under international sanctions, 400 miles west of France in international waters of the Atlantic Ocean in “strict compliance with the law of the sea.”
label: OBJ

text: French maritime authorities said the ship originated from the Russian port of Murmansk in the northwestern part of the country.
label: OBJ

text: “It is unacceptable for ships to circumvent international sanctions, violate the law of the sea, and fund the war that Russia has been waging against Ukraine for more than 4 years,” Mr. Macron wrote on X. “These vessels, which fail to adhere to the most basic rules of maritime navigation, also pose a threat to the environment and to everyone’s safety.”
label: OBJ

text: The post was accompanied by a video of French soldiers rappelling from a hovering helicopter onto the deck of the Tagor.
label: OBJ

text: Dmitry Peskov, spokesperson for the Russian government, said Moscow considers the interception to be illegal.
label: OBJ

text: “We consider such actions illegal, as they amount to international piracy,” Mr. Peskov told reporters at a briefing on Monday.
label: OBJ

text: The operation is the latest in a string of incidents in which the French navy has seized Russian-linked vessels in an attempt to curb some of Moscow’s energy profits, which are used to finance its war in Ukraine.
label: OBJ

text: French forces have boarded two tankers this year, both in the Mediterranean Sea, forcing at least one of them to pay a multimillion-euro fee before being released.
label: OBJ

text: Those tankers, including the Tagor, are suspected of being part of Russia’s “shadow fleet,” a network of vessels used by sanctioned countries to transport illicit goods.
label: OBJ

text: Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche has been on the job for less than two months, and he’s already made himself hard to ignore.
label: SUBJ

text: The president’s restless base is paying attention, and his reputation, at least from the outside, is that he is more aggressive than the woman he replaced.
label: SUBJ

text: That read isn’t entirely wrong, insiders tell [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: Blanche’s years of federal experience and his time as number two at DOJ have left him with his hands on a lot of projects and the institutional knowledge to actually move them.
label: SUBJ

text: “He knows how to uncork the bottle quickly and where the levers of power are in DOJ,” a former Justice official tells [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: In less than two months, he’s indicted the Southern Poverty Law Center and former FBI Director James Comey, and signed off on a $1.776 billion “Anti-Weaponization Fund” for victims of political prosecutions.
label: SUBJ

text: The pace has left Trump allies impressed.
label: OBJ

text: But they’re quick to add context: Blanche didn’t build these cases from scratch.
label: OBJ

text: He inherited a loaded weapon and knew exactly how to fire it.
label: SUBJ

text: “Pam Bondi had to take all the slings and arrows to get where we are now.
label: SUBJ

text: And now Todd is taking the baton, and he’s the next man up,” Mike Davis, Trump ally and founder of the Article III project, told [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: Bondi, who was never a federal prosecutor, faced a steep learning curve navigating the inner workings of the DOJ, the former official told [OUTLET].
label: SUBJ

text: It made her transition to AG more complicated.
label: OBJ

text: But her tenure, insiders say, was more spent laying groundwork for the results she wouldn’t get to see through.
label: OBJ

text: Blanche doesn’t have the same problem.
label: OBJ

text: The Acting AG came up as an Assistant U.S. Attorney in the Southern District of New York, one of the country’s most demanding federal districts, and later served as the president’s personal criminal defense attorney before entering the Trump administration as number two at the DOJ.
label: OBJ

text: By the time he stepped into the top job, he already knew which cases were live, which prosecutors to trust, and how to push a grand jury toward an indictment.
label: SUBJ

text: “The advantage of a number two rising to become the acting number one is that you know where all the balls are in the air,” a former DOJ official told [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: “Todd could immediately grab some of those balls and put them into play.”
label: OBJ

text: Nineteen days after Bondi’s firing, Blanche called a press conference alongside FBI Director Kash Patel to announce an indictment against the Southern Poverty Law Center (SPLC).
label: OBJ

text: The left-wing activist group had been covertly directing millions to individuals tied to white supremacist and other extremist organizations, Blanche said.
label: OBJ

text: The announcement was celebrated as a win for conservative organizations, many of whom were targeted by the SPLC and labeled as “hate groups.”
label: OBJ

text: A few days later, Blanche’s DOJ unveiled a second indictment against former FBI Director James Comey.
label: OBJ

text: This one was tied to a since-deleted social media post featuring the numbers “8647,” which some interpreted as a veiled call to kill the president.
label: OBJ

text: A DOJ official told [OUTLET] the SPLC case had been in the works for some time, but that Blanche’s arrival as acting AG was what got it across the finish line.
label: OBJ

text: His familiarity with grand juries, prosecutors, and the mechanics of building a federal case made the difference.
label: OBJ

text: “A lot of that job is knowing the justice system and how to get things across the finish line.
label: OBJ

text: Having that understanding, I think, is where he has found success,” the DOJ official told [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: Still, Bondi’s DOJ played a role in helping build the case, insiders explained.
label: OBJ

text: “I think Blanche is doing a good job, but I don’t think that they’ve just suddenly gotten more aggressive.
label: OBJ

text: I, some of this work, particularly like the SPLC, as a result of work that was clearly in progress when Bondi left,” von Spakosky added. Davis agreed.
label: OBJ

text: Davis agreed. “[Blanche is] taking it to the next level, from a strong foundation behind the scenes, to action in public… you can’t just wave a magic wand and bring indictments or predicate investigations.
label: OBJ

text: That takes a lot of work.
label: OBJ

text: It takes months of work,” he told [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: Whether Blanche gets a permanent nomination remains an open question, and it may stay that way deliberately.
label: SUBJ

text: “Congress takes more recess than kindergarten,” a former DOJ official told [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: “Why would you waste political capital trying to confirm Todd when what happens if he doesn’t get confirmed?”
label: SUBJ

text: Politico has reported there may be a loophole allowing Blanche to remain in the acting role beyond the standard 210-day limit.
label: OBJ

text: The White House, for its part, isn’t signaling any urgency to change course.
label: OBJ

text: “President Trump has a great relationship with acting Attorney General Todd Blanche and is very pleased with the job he’s doing so far,” White House spokeswoman Abigail Jackson told [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: In the meantime, Davis has a prediction for Blanche’s next move.
label: OBJ

text: “I have very publicly, for four years since the Mar-a-Lago raid, called for a federal grand jury in Fort Pierce, Florida to indict Obama for their conspiracy against rights — against President Trump, his top aides, and his allies,” Davis said.
label: OBJ

text: “I fully expect this to happen under Todd Blanche.”
label: OBJ

text: America’s homelessness crisis is routinely framed as a housing crisis. It is not.
label: SUBJ

text: It is not. It is a crisis born from the collapse of accountability at every level of the system.
label: SUBJ

text: Nowhere are the consequences of that collapse more visible than in California — and especially in its capital city, Sacramento.
label: SUBJ

text: In 2016, California became the only state in the nation to formally adopt the federal government’s Housing First mandate as its sole taxpayer-funded approach to homelessness, directing billions in state and federal dollars toward subsidized-for-life apartments with no accountability for sobriety, treatment, or work — ever.
label: SUBJ

text: Sacramento County followed in 2017, embracing the housing-only model, despite repeated warnings from frontline providers that housing alone would never adequately address the addiction, mental illness, trauma or behavioral health challenges that so often accompany homelessness.
label: SUBJ

text: Those warnings proved tragically accurate.
label: SUBJ

text: The problem was never simply homelessness and the solution was never simply apartments.
label: SUBJ

text: Under this mandate, homelessness rose nearly 35% nationally.
label: OBJ

text: In California, it surged 40%.
label: OBJ

text: In Sacramento County, the homeless population more than doubled.
label: OBJ

text: NEWSOM JUST MADE A CATASTROPHIC MISTAKE ON CALIFORNIA’S HOMELESSNESS DISASTER
label: SUBJ

text: But even more alarming than the numbers was what society increasingly learned to tolerate: human deterioration, environmental destruction, exploding encampments and escalating public disorder, all while accountability and expectations were systematically stripped from the system itself.
label: SUBJ

text: That’s because the problem was never simply homelessness, and the solution was never simply apartments.
label: SUBJ

text: Waterways, parks and sidewalks did not become wastelands because of a lack of housing.
label: OBJ

text: They were devastated by a policy framework that systematically removed recovery, restoration and accountability from the center of homelessness policy.
label: SUBJ

text: NEWSOM JUST TOOK A PAGE FROM TRUMP.
label: SUBJ

text: BUT THERE’S MORE TO DO TO KEEP CALIFORNIA A GOLDEN STATE
label: SUBJ

text: What followed was entirely predictable: tens of thousands of needles and shopping carts strewn across rivers, canals and public spaces as encampments became government-sanctioned waiting rooms for permanent housing that often never comes.
label: SUBJ

text: As vulnerable human beings are left idle for years without treatment or purpose, social decay takes root — and what should shock the public conscience becomes normalized.
label: SUBJ

text: Most heartbreaking of all was our time walking Sacramento’s streets, where we saw human beings visibly deteriorate physically, mentally and spiritually while passersby barely looked up — no shock, no outrage, no gasp.
label: SUBJ

text: This is what we saw in Sacramento last week, as a colleague and I spent one day there working with the River City Waterway Alliance — a volunteer group made up largely of retirees who have quietly become one of the last lines of defense protecting Sacramento’s waterways from environmental collapse.
label: SUBJ

text: We spent another day working with the Sacramento County Sheriff’s HOT Team, which removed an estimated 5,000 pounds of waste from a canal that the team had fully cleared just one month earlier.
label: OBJ

text: One conclusion became impossible to escape: America’s homelessness crisis is, at its core, a crisis of zero accountability — and Sacramento is the telltale sign of what happens when accountability collapses, ideology replaces outcomes, and leaders refuse to confront the consequences of their own policies.
label: SUBJ

text: Realities in Sacramento alone paint a staggering picture:
label: SUBJ

text: Over the past three years, Alliance volunteers removed nearly 4 million pounds of waste from Sacramento waterways — including 29,000 needles, 19,000 shopping carts and more than 70,000 batteries.
label: OBJ

text: CALIFORNIA IS BROKE, BUT IT’S NOT TOO LATE FOR THE REST OF US
label: SUBJ

text: Meanwhile, the death rate among the homeless population more than doubled over that same period.
label: OBJ

text: In one year, Sacramento County’s Sheriff’s HOT Team visited nearly 4,600 camps, closed over 1,300 of them, and cleared 3 million pounds of trash from homeless encampments.
label: OBJ

text: Yet as volunteers and law enforcement struggle to contain the fallout, Sacramento’s homeless population grew by another 13% last year alone — an additional 1,000 individuals — further compounding a crisis the region was already failing to keep pace with.
label: SUBJ

text: The deterioration is accelerating far faster than it can be contained, and the sheer scale of the destruction is almost impossible to comprehend unless you witness it firsthand.
label: SUBJ

text: But most heartbreaking of all was our time walking Sacramento’s streets, where we saw human beings visibly deteriorate physically, mentally and spiritually while passersby barely looked up — no shock, no outrage, no gasp.
label: SUBJ

text: In Sacramento, this level of suffering and societal breakdown is now tolerated.
label: SUBJ

text: Yet none of this was inevitable.
label: SUBJ

text: For years, volunteers with the River City Waterway Alliance begged local and state leaders to confront the accelerating environmental destruction unfolding across Sacramento’s waterways.
label: SUBJ

text: At the very same time, Gov. Gavin Newsom vetoed legislation that would have created a sober living housing set-aside — despite pleas from individuals struggling with addiction, frontline providers and mayors across California who warned the state desperately needed more recovery-focused options within its homelessness system.
label: SUBJ

text: To their credit, the city and county are now responding.
label: SUBJ

text: But repeated pleas to the governor, the California Department of Fish and Wildlife, the Wildlife Conservation Board, and even the Sacramento chapter of the Sierra Club — organizations with real power and publicly stated missions tied directly to these issues — were met with silence. Crickets.
label: SUBJ

text: Crickets. At the very same time, the Sheriff’s HOT Team, which is actively containing the destruction, now faces the chopping block as county leaders grapple with a $100 million budget deficit.
label: SUBJ

text: That tells you everything you need to know about the priorities of the current system and the elected officials leading it.
label: SUBJ

text: Today, Sacramento stands as a warning to the rest of the nation about what happens when accountability is stripped away, when leaders refuse to confront the consequences of their own failed policies, and when voters continue returning those same leaders to power even as human suffering, environmental destruction, public disorder and societal collapse accelerate in plain sight.
label: SUBJ

text: That is the real crisis. Not simply homelessness. Not simply housing.
label: SUBJ

text: Not simply homelessness. Not simply housing. It’s the collapse of accountability itself.
label: SUBJ

text: Election interference scandal is uncovered in two major California hubs
label: OBJ

text: An election interference scandal has already rocked California as a voting site was vandalized and burned mail-in ballots were found in a drop box.
label: SUBJ

text: The incidents, which officials described as isolated but serious, have sparked an investigation and renewed warnings that any attempt to interfere with the voting process will carry criminal penalties.
label: OBJ

text: Election workers discovered vandalism Sunday morning at a vote center located at Cesar E. Chavez Park in Long Beach, according to the Los Angeles County Registrar-Recorder/County Clerk’s Office.
label: OBJ

text: Officials said staff responded immediately and voting operations continued without interruption.
label: OBJ

text: In a separate incident, county election workers conducting routine ballot collection identified a small number of Vote by Mail ballots that appeared to have suffered fire-related damage inside a drop box at the Department of Public Social Services-Civic Center in downtown Los Angeles.
label: OBJ

text: Election officials said preliminary information suggests the damage was limited in scope and occurred during a relatively short window between a scheduled ballot pickup and the following morning’s collection.
label: OBJ

text: The Registrar-Recorder/County Clerk’s Office has filed a report with the Los Angeles Police Department and said it will cooperate fully with investigators seeking to determine how the damage occurred.
label: OBJ

text: Officials are also reviewing both incidents to determine whether any voters were affected.
label: OBJ

text: “Our responsibility is to protect voters and ensure every eligible voter has the opportunity to cast a ballot,” said Dean Logan, Registrar-Recorder/County Clerk.
label: OBJ

text: “Any attempt to interfere with voting or election operations is taken seriously.
label: OBJ

text: We will continue working closely with law enforcement and other partners to safeguard the voting process and ensure voters can participate with confidence.”
label: OBJ

text: County officials said voters whose ballots may have been burned will be contacted directly and informed of their options, including obtaining replacement ballots if necessary.
label: OBJ

text: The incidents prompted a broader response from county leaders, who emphasized that attacks on election facilities, voting equipment or ballots will not be tolerated.
label: OBJ

text: “Voting is a fundamental right, and Los Angeles County remains committed to ensuring every eligible voter can cast a ballot safely and confidently,” said Los Angeles County Board Chair and First District Supervisor Hilda L. Solis.
label: OBJ

text: “Any attempt to vandalize election facilities, damage voting materials, or interfere with the voting process is unacceptable.
label: SUBJ

text: “We take these incidents seriously and will continue working with election officials and law enforcement partners to protect voters and uphold the integrity of our elections.”
label: OBJ

text: The county also reminded residents that state and federal laws impose criminal penalties on individuals who intentionally tamper with ballots, election materials, voting equipment or election facilities.
label: OBJ

text: Officials urged the public to remain vigilant and report any suspicious activity involving election operations, ballot drop boxes, voting locations or election materials as investigators continue examining both incidents.
label: OBJ

text: They encouraged voters to report any suspicious activity to the County Registrar-Recorder’s Office by calling (800) 815-2666.
label: OBJ

text: Maine Democratic Senate candidate Graham Platner is pointing fingers at incumbent Sen. Susan Collins’s (R-ME) stock assets as he seeks to shift voters’ eyes from another controversy plaguing his campaign this weekend.
label: SUBJ

text: News broke this weekend that Platner’s wife previously told his former political director she was aware of sexually explicit messages that Platner had sent to multiple women while they were married.
label: OBJ

text: But Platner is hoping to turn over a new leaf, shifting the focus to Collins with a new letter on her financial disclosures and her increase in wealth throughout her time in Congress.
label: OBJ

text: “Susan Collins is nearly 100 times wealthier than Graham Platner.
label: OBJ

text: The corporations writing her checks are the same ones raising costs for Maine families.
label: SUBJ

text: And the senator who arrived in Washington with debt in 1996 is leaving—when she loses in November—with a fortune,” Platner’s campaign wrote in the open letter shared with Politico.
label: OBJ

text: Platner’s hit against Collins on her stock portfolio is a safe play among the mainstream Democratic Party, which has been slamming Collins for her stock trading habits over the past year but has also had a hot-and-cold relationship with the controversy-laden progressive Platner campaign.

text: The populist oyster farmer was embraced by progressives such as Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-VT) in the campaign, but Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-NY) initially picked and backed Gov. Janet Mills (D-ME) before she dropped out of the primary.
label: OBJ

text: The extramarital texts are the latest controversy to hit Platner’s campaign to unseat Collins in Maine, following contention over a now-covered-up tattoo resembling Nazi imagery and deleted explicit social media posts.
label: OBJ

text: The controversies have caused mainstream Democrats such as Sen. Cory Booker (D-NJ) and Rep. Jake Auchincloss (D-MA) to voice concern over Platner’s campaign.
label: OBJ

text: Platner, turning away from the focus on these scandals, is painting himself as an everyday Mainer who “doesn’t have a stock portfolio” and “doesn’t take money from Super PACs or the oligarchs exploiting working Mainers” in the letter shared Monday.
label: OBJ

text: “What he does have is a small business, family and friends, and a local community where he goes to the grocery store, gets gas, and lives with the same rising costs and pressures that Maine families are facing every day.
label: OBJ

text: As for Susan Collins, she made more in passive investment income last year than most Maine households earn from a full year of work,” Platner’s campaign wrote.
label: OBJ

text: Collins’s campaign told Politico that Platner’s attempt to pin her as disconnected from everyday Maine residents is “as laughable as it is hypocritical.”
label: OBJ

text: Directly juxtaposing his own income to Collins’s, Platner listed his oyster farm as his greatest asset, while pinning Collins’s greatest asset as her stock portfolio.
label: OBJ

text: He also listed his own net worth at $100,000, while listing Collins’s as “up to $9.6 million.”
label: OBJ

text: Collins’s husband, Thomas Daffron, owns stock in several defense contracting companies while Collins chairs the Senate Appropriations Committee, a nugget that Democrats have long targeted Collins on.
label: OBJ

text: Daffron holds tens of thousands of dollars in corporate securities stock in War Department contractors Boeing and RTX Corporation, according to an Al Jazeera report.
label: OBJ

text: Platner and Collins, each without a primary challenge, are all but assured to face each other in the November general election.
label: OBJ

text: It’s a slogan that’s long encapsulated the unique ability of Hugo Chavez’s fiercely nationalistic revolution to stay in power in Venezuela for 27 years: “United, we will win!”
label: SUBJ

text: The young, the old, ruling party leaders and propagandists alike shout it at official events, street demonstrations and on state television, pumping their fists to show loyalty to the self-described socialist government — and its traditional antipathy toward the United States.
label: SUBJ

text: Even when confronted with overwhelming truths that defy such bravado, the diverse coalition of military, ideological and opportunistic hangers-on has acted in lockstep.
label: SUBJ

text: But cracks in that unity have emerged after the stunning U.S. military operation that captured then-President Nicolas Maduro in January.
label: SUBJ

text: Longtime loyalists are airing disagreements with the government of acting President Delcy Rodríguez and even discussing publicly rumors that an insider’s betrayal helped the U.S. depose Maduro.
label: OBJ

label: OBJ

text: Many criticize the warming relationship between Rodríguez’s government and the White House, whose occupant, regardless of party, Chavismo has historically seen as its main adversary.
label: OBJ

text: May’s deportation of a former minister to face criminal investigations in the U.S. and Rodríguez’s recent authorization for the U.S. military to conduct a training exercise in Venezuela’s capital laid bare internal divisions.

text: Silva questioned the legality of the deportation of Alex Saab, a close ally of Maduro’s, arguing that it violated a constitutional ban.

text: He contended that Rodríguez is not governing freely, as some decisions “are being made in the U.S. Embassy.”
label: OBJ

text: “The imperialists don’t negotiate.
label: OBJ

text: They conquer, test and probe — until our country shatters,” Silva said in a livestream.
label: OBJ

text: “Nobody is safe right now.
label: OBJ

text: And that is a concrete, terribly dangerous fact.”
label: SUBJ

text: On May 23, a few dozen people in Caracas protested the training that saw two Marine Corps Osprey aircraft land at the U.S. Embassy.

text: They held a Venezuelan flag with the message, “No to the Yankee drill” written over it.
label: OBJ

text: Participation was minimal, which stood out in a city used to frequent demonstrations with attendance in the thousands.
label: OBJ

text: Elías Jaua, who served as Chavez’s vice president and in Maduro’s Cabinet in his first years in office, repudiated the exercise on social media.
label: OBJ

text: He later told The Associated Press he was speaking up to raise awareness among Venezuelans of the “humiliating” situation facing the country.
label: OBJ

text: “At this stage, the most important thing is to prevent this occupation and this colonial administration to which a nation like Venezuela is being subjected from becoming normalized,” Jaua said.
label: OBJ

text: Chavez and Maduro — as well as Rodríguez, in her previous roles as vice president and communications and foreign affairs minister — had long prophesied that the U.S. would use force to take control of Venezuela’s oil industry, which has opened up to private capital after Maduro’s capture.
label: OBJ

text: The Trump administration oversees oil sales and administers revenues as part of its phased plan to turn the troubled South American country around.

text: Maduro’s ouster prompts power struggle

text: Sen. Chris Murphy (D-CT) examines the state of U.S. politics, culture, and life in his latest book, released Tuesday, becoming the latest possible 2028 presidential hopeful to unveil his vision for the country.
label: OBJ

text: Murphy’s book, Crisis of the Common Good, argues the common good is currently missing in the country because there is an emphasis on living for oneself while lacking strength in community.
label: OBJ

text: “I think for all the focus on Donald Trump and the news of the day, people in this country know there is a deeper story in this country, and that is we are a less connected culture today,” Murphy said on ABC’s Good Morning America.
label: OBJ

text: “We are a less happy nation than we’ve been in for a long time, and that is because we have lost, what I call in the book, a common good.”
label: OBJ

text: The book uses history and political philosophy to examine how six different “cults” contributed to the nation’s current problems.
label: OBJ

text: The six “cults” Murphy names are profit that punishes workers, globalism that weakens communities, technology that turns us against one another and poisons our young, consumption that undermines citizenship, credentialism that devalues those without degrees, and corruption that threatens democracy.
label: SUBJ

text: Based on these troubles, Murphy argues that Americans lost a sense of “daily purpose” and connection, leading them to become anxious and adrift.
label: OBJ

text: Murphy accuses President Donald Trump of “feeding off” this “emptiness” to get power.
label: OBJ

text: “The common good is about living in a world where you care about yourself but others as well,” he said.
label: OBJ

text: “Government has a lot to do with rebuilding that sense of the common good.”
label: OBJ

text: Murphy said Trump understood how the country was coming “unspooled,” offering scapegoats to blame for the problems people faced.
label: OBJ

text: When asked how Democrats should respond to this, Murphy said his political party should focus on building “happiness.”
label: OBJ

text: “Democrats have to focus on what actually builds happiness,” he said.
label: OBJ

text: “You need a job and an income to be happy, but really you need to feel like you live in a unique vibrant place.”
label: OBJ

text: As Murphy argues in the book, he said the government can help build happiness and communities.
label: OBJ

text: “Government can help set up the conditions so that we can have more connected, happier, more vibrant communities,” he said.
label: OBJ

text: “I think that that conversation brings the Right and Left together.
label: OBJ

text: Both Right and Left agree that our local communities are weaker.”

text: OPINION: Last week, Cal Thomas wrote an opinion piece for this newspaper titled “Americans whining over gas prices ignore Iran’s greater threat” (Web, May 26).
label: OBJ

text: In his piece, Mr. Thomas argues that our country is “plagued by complainers who whine about almost everything” and that “too many [of us] appear oblivious to the threat posed by Iran, preferring cheaper gas” to doing what is necessary to stop this evil regime from killing us.
label: OBJ

text: He contends that the “entitlement sentiment … has gripped so many” that when our president “asks Americans to endure higher gas prices for a short time in exchange for Iran not getting a nuclear weapon,” we respond that we would rather have cheap gas today.
label: OBJ

text: After rightly juxtaposing this myopia with the numerous sacrifices that our grandparents made during World War II — sacrifices that stopped Adolf Hitler from acquiring an atomic bomb before we did and thereby saved millions of people from obliteration — Mr. Thomas concludes with this simple admonition: “Gas prices have been high for only a few weeks.
label: SUBJ

text: The Great Depression and World War II spanned 16 years. Stop whining, America.”
label: SUBJ

text: Stop whining, America.” In short, Mr. Thomas argues that minor personal sacrifices, such as spending a little more for gas, are far less significant than what the Greatest Generation endured to protect and preserve the United States and its freedoms.
label: OBJ

text: I wholeheartedly agree with Mr. Thomas, and I am more than happy to spend a little extra to fill up the tank if that will help stop Iran from having the military wherewithal to impose its will on the world.
label: SUBJ

text: I reposted Mr. Thomas’ piece to my social media and encouraged all my “friends and followers” to read it.
label: OBJ

text: Then, you guessed it, I immediately received a contrary response: “Iran is a threat to Israel, not to the United States,” said one of my Facebook pals.
label: OBJ

text: Now, aside from my “friend’s” cluelessness that the survival of our only trustworthy ally in the Middle East matters, how in the world could anyone be so dumb?
label: SUBJ

text: “Iran is not a threat to the United States.” Are you serious?
label: SUBJ

text: Are you serious? A five-second Google search shows that for the past 47 years alone, this evil regime and its leaders have kidnapped, killed, maimed and beheaded Americans around the globe.
label: SUBJ

text: Here is just a short summary:
label: OBJ

text: • In 1979, Iranian militants seized the U.S. Embassy in Tehran and held 66 Americans hostage, keeping 52 of them for 444 days.
label: OBJ

text: • In April 1983, Iran backed the suicide bombing of the U.S. Embassy in Beirut, killing 17 Americans.
label: OBJ

text: • In October 1983, Iran’s leaders orchestrated the Beirut barracks bombing, which killed 241 U.S. military personnel, including 220 Marines.
label: OBJ

text: • In March 1984, CIA station chief William Buckley was kidnapped in Beirut and later killed.
label: OBJ

text: • In December 1984, Kuwait Airways Flight 221 was hijacked, and two American officials were killed.
label: OBJ

text: • In June 1985, TWA Flight 847 was hijacked, and a U.S. Navy diver was killed.
label: OBJ

text: • In July 1989, the Iranian-backed, -trained and -funded terrorist organization Hezbollah kidnapped, tortured and killed U.S. Marine Col. William Higgins.
label: SUBJ

text: • In June 1996, the Iranian militants bombed the Khobar Towers and killed 19 U.S. airmen and wounded nearly 500 other people.
label: OBJ

text: • In August 1998, the U.S. embassies in Kenya and Tanzania were bombed.
label: OBJ

text: Twelve Americans were among 224 total dead.
label: OBJ

text: • From 2003 to 2011, Iran-backed militias killed at least 603 U.S. troops in Iraq.
label: OBJ

text: • In January 2007, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps’ Quds Force and its operatives killed five U.S. soldiers in Karbala, Iraq.
label: OBJ

text: • In December 2019, an Iranian Kataib Hezbollah rocket killed a U.S. civilian contractor.
label: OBJ

text: • In January 2020, an Iranian ballistic missile strike on the Ain al-Asad Air Base in western Iraq injured 109 U.S. troops.
label: OBJ

text: • In March 2023, Iranian drone attacks killed an American contractor in Syria.
label: OBJ

text: • On Oct. 7, 2023, Iran supported and funded Hamas’ killing of 46 Americans and kidnapping of at least 12 others.
label: SUBJ

text: • In January 2024, Iran’s Kataib Hezbollah drone attack at Tower 22 in Jordan killed three U.S. service members.
label: OBJ

text: The list goes on and on.
label: SUBJ

text: In addition to their ubiquitous calls for “Death to America!”
label: SUBJ

text: Iran and its proxies have killed, kidnapped, tortured and raped well over 1,000 of our nation’s citizens in just the past 50 years.
label: OBJ

text: It is nothing short of astounding that someone would suggest that Iran is not a threat to the United States, and it is equally amazing that so many of us are unwilling to make a sacrifice as inconsequential as paying a bit more for gasoline if it will stop these mullahs from acquiring the means to do exactly what they have told us for decades they intend to do.
label: SUBJ

text: Cal Thomas is right.

text: Excavators recently uncovered a "mysterious and impressive" ancient tunnel in Jerusalem, not far from archaeological sites tied to the biblical Kingdom of Judah.
label: OBJ

text: The discovery was announced by the Israel Antiquities Authority (IAA) in a May 14 statement.
label: OBJ

text: The tunnel was found near Kibbutz Ramat Rachel, an archaeological site tied to the biblical-era Kingdom of Judah, according to previous IAA excavations.
label: OBJ

text: The rock-hewn tunnel is about 164 feet long and was found ahead of construction on a new residential neighborhood north of Ramat Rachel.
label: OBJ

text: The tunnel is around 16 feet tall and roughly 10 feet wide — and officials note that the rock-cutting "was executed meticulously."
label: OBJ

text: "The tunnel itself was discovered filled with layers of soil that had accumulated over hundreds, perhaps even thousands, of years," the IAA said.
label: OBJ

text: "It is clear that whoever carved this tunnel invested tremendous effort [and] careful planning, and possessed the capabilities and resources necessary to achieve this goal."
label: SUBJ

text: Excavation directors Sivan Mizrahi and Zinovi Matskevich told the IAA the discovery was unexpected.
label: OBJ

text: "We were excavating in relatively rocky and exposed terrain when suddenly we discovered a natural karstic cavity," the pair said in a joint statement.
label: OBJ

text: "To our amazement, as the excavation progressed, this cavity developed into a long tunnel.
label: OBJ

text: Parts of it are still collapsed, so the tunnel has not yet revealed all of its secrets."
label: SUBJ

text: "Usually we have explanations for the discoveries we uncover, but sometimes, as in this case, we stand astonished and amazed."
label: OBJ

text: In 2020, IAA excavations near Ramat Rachel revealed seal impressions and structures tied to the Kingdom of Judah, indicating that "governmental activity took place in the area," the organization said at the time.
label: OBJ

text: No evidence that's been uncovered so far, however, directly connects the newly discovered tunnel to the Kingdom of Judah or the biblical era — and the age of the tunnel is unknown.
label: OBJ

text: "The tunnel lies only a few hundred meters, as the crow flies, from two significant ancient sites — a public building from the Iron Age (First Temple period) in the Arnona neighborhood, and Tel Ramat Rachel, where settlement remains dating from the Iron Age through the Islamic period have been documented," Mizrahi and Matskevich said.
label: OBJ

text: Though the purpose of the tunnel remains unclear, researchers theorize it may have been carved to reach a chalk layer used for quarrying building stones or producing lime.
label: OBJ

text: "Possible evidence supporting this interpretation includes a shaft carved into the tunnel’s ceiling, which may have been used for ventilation, as well as quarrying debris discovered on the tunnel floor — although this interpretation, too, remains uncertain," the IAA said.
label: OBJ

text: Experts have ruled out the possibility that the tunnel was part of an ancient water system, and some posit the site may have been abandoned before construction was completed.
label: OBJ

text: "Alternatively, the findings may indicate that the quarrying and construction of the tunnel were never completed, and therefore its intended purpose and nature remain unknown," the IAA says.
label: OBJ

text: Amit Re'em, a Jerusalem district archaeologist at the IAA, said the find "joins many others being uncovered every day, hour by hour, throughout the city."
label: OBJ

text: "The archaeologists and researchers of the Israel Antiquities Authority are constantly at work, because this city never ceases to surprise," said Re'em.
label: OBJ

text: "Usually we have explanations for the discoveries we uncover, but sometimes, as in this case, we stand astonished and amazed."
label: OBJ

text: In American politics, the arc from City Hall to the Cabinet is one of the longest imaginable.
label: SUBJ

text: Most politicians spend entire careers trying to travel it and never arrive.
label: SUBJ

text: Dirk Kempthorne, the former Idaho governor, U.S. senator, and secretary of the interior who died April 24 in Boise at the age of 74, not only made that journey — he made it look, somehow, like the most natural thing in the world.
label: SUBJ

text: Dirk Arthur Kempthorne was born on Oct. 29, 1951, in San Diego and raised in San Bernardino, California — a fact that his adopted state of Idaho might have held against him, but never did.
label: SUBJ

text: After a stint at San Bernardino Valley College, he transferred north to the University of Idaho in Moscow, where he earned a degree in political science, served as student body president, and found the place he would always call home.
label: OBJ

text: He also found his future wife, Patricia, whom he married in 1977 in a sunrise ceremony atop Moscow Mountain, a setting that reflected his conviction that there was no more beautiful cathedral than the outdoors.
label: OBJ

text: After graduation, Kempthorne worked as executive assistant to the director of the Idaho Department of Lands and then as executive vice president of the Idaho Home Builders Association, gaining practical knowledge of how government actually worked.
label: SUBJ

text: His entry into electoral politics came in 1985, when, at 34, he ran for mayor of Boise — the youngest candidate in the race by a margin of 15 years.
label: OBJ

text: His successful platform was straightforward: Revitalize a downtown that two decades of failed urban renewal had left a patchwork of empty lots and an abandoned mall dream.
label: SUBJ

text: Seven years later, Boise had a new convention center, a regional shopping mall, a Grove Plaza, and a renovated cityscape to show for it.
label: OBJ

text: Kempthorne had also developed the instinctive, small-d democratic touch that would define his public persona — an almost supernatural ability to remember not just names but spouses’ names, children’s names, the last conversation he’d had with someone years before.
label: SUBJ

text: To those who experienced it, it felt less like a politician’s trick than a genuine expression of interest in other people.
label: SUBJ

text: In 1992, Kempthorne won the U.S. Senate seat vacated by Republican Steve Symms and arrived in Washington with an agenda shaped by his years running a city that had to live within its means while Congress dictated terms.
label: OBJ

text: His signature achievement became Senate Bill 1 of the 104th Congress: the Unfunded Mandates Reform Act of 1995, which barred the federal government from passing costs down to state and local governments without corresponding funding.
label: OBJ

text: Getting a bipartisan bill signed by a Democratic president as a freshman Republican senator was no small feat.
label: SUBJ

text: On the Senate floor, he argued that Congress had been “far too willing to merely pass the bill and then pass the buck to the states and localities, but the ultimate billpayer is the same weary American taxpayer.”
label: OBJ

text: President Bill Clinton signed it.
label: OBJ

text: Kempthorne also overhauled the Safe Drinking Water Act in 1996.
label: OBJ

text: Rather than seek Senate reelection in 1998, he ran for governor of Idaho and won with nearly 68% of the vote.
label: OBJ

text: As governor, he championed education reform, requiring Idaho children to read at grade level by the third grade, and pushed through a transportation financing program that broke Idaho’s long tradition of pay-as-you-go infrastructure.
label: OBJ

text: When the legislature balked, Kempthorne invited the TV cameras to his ceremonial office.
label: OBJ

text: On April Fools’ Day 2005, he grabbed a giant red veto stamp and slammed it down on eight House bills in quick succession.
label: SUBJ

text: The legislature came around.
label: OBJ

text: In 2006, President George W. Bush appointed Kempthorne the 49th secretary of the interior — the first person to hold the position who had previously served as governor, senator, and mayor.
label: OBJ

text: Kempthorne delivered at least one notable surprise: In 2008, he insisted that the polar bear be listed as a threatened species under the Endangered Species Act due to Arctic sea ice loss. Bush backed him.
label: OBJ

text: Bush backed him. During his tenure at Interior, Kempthorne lived on a houseboat moored in the Potomac, a fitting image for a landlocked Westerner making his way in Washington.
label: SUBJ

text: After leaving government, Kempthorne spent eight years as president and CEO of the American Council of Life Insurers and later chaired The Peregrine Fund, the raptor conservation organization.
label: OBJ

text: He remained engaged in retirement.
label: OBJ

text: In 2021, he helped organize the private evacuation of nearly 400 Americans and Afghan allies from Taliban-held Kabul, spending months raising money and navigating diplomatic channels to charter an Airbus A340 and get them out.
label: OBJ

text: As a University of Idaho student, Kempthorne had honed his public speaking by going out to the campus farmland to deliver speeches to the grazing cows.
label: OBJ

text: “I’d go out there where you’d find a number of cows, and they’d be grazing,” he once recalled, “And I’d go and I’d give them a speech.”
label: OBJ

text: The cows, apparently, were a tough crowd.
label: SUBJ

text: Idaho turned out to be a more appreciative audience, and Kempthorne never stopped giving them his best.
label: SUBJ

text: NEWARK, N.J. — The mayor of Newark imposed a curfew early Sunday around an immigration detention center in New Jersey after a series of intense clashes between protesters and police.
label: OBJ

text: The curfew around Delaney Hall will be in place between 9 p.m. and 6 a.m. until further notice, Mayor Ras Baraka said in a statement.

text: Congress returns facing looming deadlines, unfinished business and internal Republican divisions as the midterm elections draw closer.
label: OBJ

text: Lawmakers left Washington ahead of Memorial Day on the cusp of passing a massive immigration enforcement funding package.
label: OBJ

text: Doing so would have allowed Republicans to tackle other outstanding priorities.
label: SUBJ

text: Instead, the budget reconciliation process — which Republicans planned to use to funnel roughly $72 billion to Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and Border Patrol — stalled after a surprise move by the Trump administration exposed deep divisions within the GOP.
label: OBJ

text: Now, the setback in the reconciliation process threatens to further push back other key agenda items, including the fast-approaching deadline to reauthorize a critical spy law, a colossal defense policy bill and a potential affordability-focused legislative package.
label: SUBJ

text: The Senate is currently stuck on the reconciliation package after the Department of Justice unveiled its nearly $2 billion "anti-weaponization" fund geared to provide payouts to those who feel wronged by the government.
label: OBJ

text: Senate Republicans erupted over the fund during a closed-door meeting with acting Attorney General Todd Blanche, demanding answers on whether people convicted of assaulting police officers during the riots of Jan. 6, 2021, would be allowed to get access to the taxpayer-funded money.
label: OBJ

text: Little has changed between the Senate GOP and administration since then, with Republicans putting the onus of dealing with the issue onto the White House.
label: OBJ

text: "The administration appreciated last week’s conversation and feedback," a White House official told [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: "We look forward to additional conversations as needed."
label: OBJ

text: TRUMP DOUBLES DOWN ON $1.8 BILLION 'SLUSH FUND' THAT KILLED HIS AGENDA, SPURRED REPUBLICAN REBELLION
label: SUBJ

text: The House is expected to take up the budget reconciliation bill upon Senate passage.
label: OBJ

text: But with no clear path forward, other pressing issues are beginning to crowd the congressional calendar.
label: OBJ

text: Congress will soon have to address the looming June 12 deadline to reauthorize Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA).
label: OBJ

text: It’s a fight that lawmakers have punted on twice now over bipartisan demands for reforms to the program, despite the Trump administration pushing for a clean reauthorization.
label: SUBJ

text: The spy law fight is one of the few horseshoe issues in Congress that blends Democrats and conservatives in a push for stronger privacy protections.
label: OBJ

text: While Section 702 allows the government to spy on foreign nationals abroad, there’s nothing in the law to prevent it from collecting data on Americans if they’re ensnared in those communications.
label: SUBJ

text: House conservatives are seeking reforms that would require warrants to surveil Americans’ communications, close loopholes allowing the government to buy sensitive data from brokers without a warrant, and curb overly broad authorities permitting the incidental collection of information, according to a source familiar with the discussions. REPORTER’S NOTEBOOK:
label: OBJ

text: REPORTER’S NOTEBOOK: CONGRESS PASSES SHORT-TERM FISA 702 FIX, DELAYS LONG-TERM RENEWAL
label: OBJ

label: OBJ

text: Meanwhile, in the House, Democrats are likely to force a vote to rein in the president’s Iran war powers that could pass with modest GOP support.

text: Democrats are also expected to trigger a vote on legislation authorizing $1.3 billion in military aid to Ukraine and levy new sanctions on the Russian war effort.

text: The measure faces an uphill battle to become law due to expected opposition from Republican leadership and the Trump administration.
label: SUBJ

text: House Speaker Mike Johnson, R-La., is also facing pressure from a swath of House Republicans to get the ball rolling on a third budget reconciliation package ahead of the midterm elections.

text: Rep. August Pfluger, R-Texas, told [OUTLET] that GOP lawmakers are having "great meetings" and he expects the conference to pass another budget reconciliation package by the end of July.

text: Pfluger’s Republican Study Committee has consistently advocated for a package focused on affordability issues across the housing, energy and healthcare sectors.

text: Enthusiasm for a third reconciliation bill has been less pronounced in the Senate.

text: Congress is also beginning to make moves with the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA), the colossal package that would open up $1.15 trillion to fund the Pentagon.
label: SUBJ

text: It’s an annual must-pass piece of legislation that could be slowed by the more pressing fights on the Hill.

text: Lawmakers will be gone for their typical August recess, but will also have almost the entirety of October off to campaign.

text: That means that the next few weeks will be crucial, particularly for Republicans, who are trying to pass any outstanding parts of President Donald Trump’s agenda.
