text: Judge torches upstate NY dad who laughed in court about executing young son, girlfriend with shotgun
label: SUBJ

text: David Huff, 43, was sentenced Friday to 40 years to life behind bars after flashing a smug grin and chuckling while admitting he shotgunned his girlfriend, Yeraldith Tschudy, 23, and his son, Jeremiah Huff, inside his stepfather’s Syracuse home in March 2025.
label: OBJ

text: “Your actions are reprehensible, and you deserve to be incarcerated for the rest of your life,” Onondaga County Judge Ted Limpert raged in court, according to Syracuse.com.
label: OBJ

text: “Even a sentence of life is not long enough for you.”
label: SUBJ

text: Jeremiah’s mother, Samantha Gallup-Peltier, also tore into Huff while remembering her slain son.
label: OBJ

text: “You are destined for the seventh circle of hell,” she tearfully blasted in court.
label: SUBJ

text: “Children are supposed to trust their parents will protect them from harm, not become the source of it.”
label: SUBJ

text: Huff used a 12-gauge shotgun when he opened fire at his stepfather’s Roney Road residence around 9:30 p.m. on March 17, 2025, killing Tschudy and his son before also allegedly shooting his stepdad.
label: OBJ

text: Gallup-Peltier was the first to dial 911 after getting a disturbing call from her son just before the killings.
label: OBJ

text: Huff fled before police arrived and was arrested by state authorities the next morning while walking on West Seneca Turnpike, not far from the grisly scene.
label: OBJ

text: Limpert’s searing rebuke came over a month after the twisted dad tried to justify his disturbing courtroom antics at the April 28 appearance, insisting he had a “joke stuck” in his head as he pleaded guilty to two counts of second-degree murder.
label: SUBJ

text: “You find this funny?”
label: SUBJ

text: the judge asked the laughing killer, the outlet reported.
label: OBJ

text: Huff flippantly replied while still giggling, “No, it’s a joke stuck in my head… Go on,” before later telling the judge “I’m guilty of all that.
label: OBJ

text: Whatever you guys say, I’m guilty of.”
label: SUBJ

text: Defense attorney Shaun Chase claimed Friday that Huff was on the hallucinogenic drug ecstasy or “Molly” when he carried out the double killings.
label: OBJ

text: The last-ditch effort came after Chase conceded that multiple experts found any mental incapacity stemmed from Huff’s voluntary abuse of booze or drugs.
label: OBJ

text: The conviction followed months of delays while the defense waited for a medical expert to assess Huff’s mental health.
label: OBJ

text: It’s not clear which substances, if any, Huff was using or his level of intoxication on the night of the murders, prosecutors said.
label: OBJ

text: Huff was found competent to stand trial but opted to plead guilty.
label: OBJ

text: Had it been successful, the shooting at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner would have been a national rupture.
label: SUBJ

text: The events of the weekend are a true Sliding Doors moment, and the fate of our nation could have taken a different turn with just a few small changes in decision-making and outcomes.
label: SUBJ

text: According to reports, the suspect, Cole Allen, admitted he was targeting Trump administration officials inside one of the most visible, symbolically loaded gatherings in American public life.
label: OBJ

text: Had he succeeded — had the bullets found their targets in a crowded ballroom filled with senior officials, journalists, and their spouses — any outcome could have been foreseeable.
label: SUBJ

text: The fact that the attack was thwarted does not change what it was meant to be.
label: SUBJ

text: It only means we are spared, for now, from confronting the full weight of what nearly happened and where we’re headed as a nation.
label: SUBJ

text: A near-miss doesn’t elicit a national reckoning or soul-searching in the same way a tragedy does.
label: SUBJ

text: We treat them as lesser events, as interruptions rather than warnings, blips in the news cycle instead of earthquakes.
label: SUBJ

text: The WHCA dinner shooting attempt was not a random act of violence, nor was it an apolitical outburst detached from a broader worldview.
label: SUBJ

text: Had it succeeded, it would have been understood as a defining moment in modern American political life — a domestic analog, however imperfect, to the kind of shock that followed the Oct. 7, 2023, attacks in Israel, when a coordinated act of violence forced a global reckoning with the ideas and structures that produced it and celebrated it.
label: SUBJ

text: COLE ALLEN’S BACKGROUND ISN’T UNUSUAL FOR A TERRORIST
label: SUBJ

text: After Oct. 7, much of the conversation in certain Western activist spaces shifted in a way that surprised even seasoned observers.
label: SUBJ

text: Rather than recoil from the brutality, some voices applauded it as an act of resistance.
label: OBJ

text: The slogan that circulated — “vibes? papers? essays?
label: OBJ

text: papers? essays? losers” — captured the sentiment.
label: SUBJ

text: That mindset did not emerge overnight, nor does it remain confined to a single geography.
label: SUBJ

text: It is cultivated, repeated, and normalized in the environments where young, politically engaged people are forming their understanding of the world.
label: OBJ

text: The uncomfortable reality in the aftermath of the WHCA dinner shooting is that the suspect does not appear to have been an outlier in the way we often hope these cases will be.
label: SUBJ

text: Early descriptions of Allen emphasize how unremarkable he seemed and how thoroughly he blended into the academic and social environments that shape the next generation of professionals, educators, and leaders.
label: SUBJ

text: That ordinariness is precisely what makes the situation more difficult to dismiss.
label: SUBJ

text: If he were easily categorized as fringe or disconnected, or mentally unwell, we could safely move on as a society.
label: SUBJ

text: Instead, his background suggests his ideas are not confined to the margins, but rather, circulate widely in elite and online spaces.
label: SUBJ

text: In those spaces, the line between rhetoric and endorsement can become blurred in ways that would have been unthinkable even a decade ago.
label: SUBJ

text: Public figures such as Hasan Piker have built enormous platforms while engaging in commentary that frequently trivializes or reframes political violence, often cloaked in humor or irony but nonetheless conveying a permissive attitude toward it.
label: SUBJ

text: Polling data reinforce the impression that this shift, while still limited, is not imaginary.
label: OBJ

text: A clear majority of people across the political spectrum continue to reject political violence outright, which is both reassuring and important.
label: SUBJ

text: At the same time, younger people and those who identify with more progressive ideologies are statistically more likely to say that violence can be justified under certain circumstances.
label: OBJ

text: That does not make them violent, but it does indicate a softening of the moral boundary that has historically defined American political life.
label: SUBJ

text: When that boundary becomes more permeable, the distance between rhetoric and action narrows in ways that are difficult to predict but easy to observe in hindsight.
label: OBJ

text: Hamas did not appear fully formed on Oct. 7.
label: SUBJ

text: It was built over years, through schools, media, religious institutions, and cultural reinforcement that elevated grievance, sanctified violence, and trained young people to see their enemies not as fellow human beings but as legitimate targets.
label: SUBJ

text: What we are seeing in the United States is not that level of organization or sophistication, yet, but it is increasingly difficult to ignore that some of the same ideological instincts are taking root.
label: SUBJ

text: They are further along.
label: OBJ

text: We are earlier in the process.
label: OBJ

text: The WHCA dinner shooting attempt should be understood through that lens.
label: SUBJ

text: If it had succeeded — if the ballroom had become a mass-casualty scene with senior officials gunned down in front of the press — the country would not be talking about a “disturbed individual.”
label: SUBJ

text: We would ask how someone came to believe this was justified, which ecosystem validated that belief, and why the warning signs were ignored.
label: SUBJ

text: That is what Oct. 7 forced on Israel and, briefly, on the rest of the world: a recognition that ideas have real-world consequences and that the distance between rhetoric and violence is not as wide as we’d like to believe.
label: SUBJ

text: The only reason we are not having that conversation now is that the outcome was different, but not the shooter’s intent.
label: SUBJ

text: BETHANY MANDEL: THE FERTILITY CRISIS ISN’T AN ECONOMIC PROBLEM.
label: OBJ

text: IT’S A CULTURAL ONE
label: SUBJ

text: That is why this moment matters more than it is being treated.
label: SUBJ

text: The same cultural currents that produce chants about “resistance,” that celebrate disruption over debate, and that train students to see politics as a struggle rather than a process do not remain safely contained in classrooms, campuses, or online platforms.
label: SUBJ

text: They shape how people understand what is permissible.
label: OBJ

text: They lower the barrier to escalation.
label: OBJ

text: And eventually, for a small number of people, the unthinkable becomes thinkable.
label: SUBJ

text: We have been given a warning without the body count.
label: OBJ

text: The question is whether we are willing to take it seriously or wait for a version of this story that would be impossible to ignore.
label: SUBJ

text: EXCLUSIVE: NEW YORK CITY —
label: OBJ

text: A Brooklyn landlord says he has been trapped in a nearly decade-long legal nightmare that has cost him hundreds of thousands of dollars in unpaid rent and legal fees, while New York courts repeatedly delay resolution as his tenant continues living in the apartment without making direct rent payments to the landlord.
label: OBJ

text: Thomas Diana, who owns a small eight-unit building in Park Slope, told [OUTLET] he has spent the last nine years trying to remove a woman who originally moved into one of his apartments as a live-in companion for an elderly, disabled tenant.
label: OBJ

text: Court records show the woman moved into the apartment in 2014 after responding to a Craigslist advertisement seeking a live-in companion for the tenant, who later died in 2016.
label: OBJ

text: What followed was nearly a decade of litigation spanning multiple courts and proceedings.
label: OBJ

text: After the elderly tenant's death, disputes arose over the woman's tenancy status, rent obligations and whether the apartment remained subject to New York rent-stabilization laws as Diana sought unpaid rent and possession of the apartment.
label: OBJ

text: "This has gone on for nine years.
label: OBJ

text: Nothing about this is justice," Diana told [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: "Every time the case gets close to resolution, there’s another delay, another lawyer change, another new story."
label: SUBJ

text: Diana says the tenant has changed lawyers at least eight times in the ongoing legal saga, which Diana refers to as a "9-year squatter situation," although the case technically centers around a dispute over rent stabilization laws with the two sides disputing nearly every aspect of the case.
label: OBJ

text: "It drained my daughter’s college fund," Diana told [OUTLET] inside his home while wearing a now-outdated T-shirt that says, "Stuck with 8-year-squatter."
label: OBJ

text: "Now we’re borrowing money to pay for college while this just keeps dragging on.
label: SUBJ

text: It gets pretty stressful.
label: OBJ

text: People think eviction cases are like TV where it takes two weeks.
label: OBJ

text: In New York it can take years, and this one has turned into almost a decade."
label: SUBJ

text: IS MAMDANI’S SOCIALIST PUSH FOR RENT CONTROLS ABOUT TO WRECK THE NEW YORK CITY HOUSING MARKET?
label: SUBJ

text: Attorneys for the tenant strongly dispute Diana’s characterization of the case, and the tenant at one point sued Diana, claiming the apartment had been improperly removed from rent stabilization protections.
label: OBJ

text: "Mr. Diana’s distortion of the facts in this case is a sad attempt to harass our client out of her rent-stabilized apartment, and he will not be successful," Casey Gilfoil, an attorney with Brooklyn Legal Services, told [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: Gilfoil said a judge has already ruled Diana improperly removed the apartment from rent stabilization and said the remaining issue before the court is determining the legal rent and any potential damages.
label: OBJ

text: Brooklyn Legal Services also says the tenant has money set aside in escrow pending the court’s final ruling.
label: OBJ

text: Diana pushed back, saying the court did not find that he committed fraud and that he followed the guidance he says he received from New York State Division of Housing and Community Renewal when the apartment was deregulated years before the tenant sued.
label: OBJ

text: "The judge ruled there was no fraud," Diana told [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: "She said I incorrectly destabilized the apartment.
label: OBJ

text: I did it as they told me to."
label: OBJ

text: Diana also disputed Brooklyn Legal Services’ claim that the tenant has years of rent saved in escrow, saying the numbers do not add up and that, based on court communications regarding her employment history, it is unlikely she has accumulated "anywhere near" $300,000.
label: OBJ

text: Diana says the occupant’s lawsuit relied on what he describes as a series of shifting and contradictory claims, including allegations that the original elderly tenant was not disabled, that the occupant had been on the lease and that the apartment was illegally deregulated.
label: OBJ

text: During depositions, Diana said his attorney challenged those claims with emails, photographs, rent records and testimony.
label: OBJ

text: He contends the allegations did not withstand scrutiny during questioning.
label: OBJ

text: "She got destroyed on all 18 claims," Diana said.
label: OBJ

text: "And once those fell apart, they just made up new ones."
label: SUBJ

text: Court stipulations required the occupant to make monthly use-and-occupancy payments, similar to interim rent payments, of roughly $835 per month at one point, but Diana says those payments stopped years ago.
label: OBJ

text: He estimates total unpaid rent now ranges between $275,000 and $325,000.
label: OBJ

text: In her deposition, the occupant testified she has not worked full time in years and has limited income, a factor
label: OBJ

text: Diana says the courts have effectively allowed to justify continued nonpayment.
label: OBJ

text: Diana, who started a GoFundMe page to help with his financial struggles, says the prolonged case has left him struggling to maintain his building and cover basic expenses, including tuition for his children.
label: OBJ

text: "One apartment out of eight not paying rent wipes out any profit," Diana said.
label: OBJ

text: "Judges talk in terms of months.
label: OBJ

text: They don’t talk about what $300,000 actually does to a family."
label: SUBJ

text: He also pointed to an overall problem with the system and described repeated housing court inspections that he says resulted in excessive and duplicative violations, which further delayed proceedings and increased costs.
label: OBJ

text: "They’ll cite you for a paint drip from 20 years ago and call you a slumlord," Diana said.
label: OBJ

text: "Meanwhile, the tenant hasn’t paid rent in nearly a decade."
label: OBJ

text: Diana says his case highlights what he views as a systemic imbalance in New York’s housing courts that allows bad-faith actors to exploit tenant protections indefinitely.
label: OBJ

text: "They tell you to sell your building.
label: OBJ

text: They tell you to accept a buyout, to pay the person who owes you hundreds of thousands of dollars," he said. "That’s not justice. That’s legalized theft."
label: OBJ

text: "That’s not justice. That’s legalized theft." In April, the case was adjourned again until this summer, essentially guaranteeing that the saga will extend into its 10th year.
label: SUBJ

text: "This court case has become a Twilight Zone Marathon," Diana said.

text: CELINA, Texas — A few years ago, the city of Celina was a quiet ranching town surrounded by open fields an hour north of Dallas.
label: OBJ

text: Now, it’s the fastest-growing city in America, where bulldozers outnumber cattle and sprawling new neighborhoods are rising almost overnight.
label: SUBJ

text: The boom transforming this once-rural community reflects the explosive growth reshaping North Texas, as families and businesses push beyond city hubs in search of more affordable housing, better schools and more space.
label: SUBJ

text: But the rapid expansion is also testing Celina’s infrastructure, water supply and ability to preserve the small-town identity that drew many residents there in the first place.
label: OBJ

text: "If you drive around Celina, you can definitely tell that we're the fastest growing city," Mayor Ryan Tubbs told [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: "We have a lot of road construction going on, a lot of new sewer and water lines, just all different infrastructure projects getting ready to service our residents of the future."
label: OBJ

text: THE RED STATES RACING AHEAD IN AMERICA’S POWERFUL WEALTH BOOM — AND THE STATES FALLING BEHIND
label: SUBJ

text: According to new U.S. Census Bureau estimates, Celina grew by 24.6% between 2024 and 2025 — meaning roughly one in four people in the city today did not live there a year prior.
label: OBJ

text: Despite this, residents say Celina has managed to hold onto the close-knit community feel that prompted many families to put down roots there.
label: OBJ

text: "Yes, we can see and feel the growth, but we haven't lost our sense of community here in Celina," resident Carolyn Harvey told [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: The city’s rapid expansion mirrors the broader population boom unfolding across North Texas, where communities on the outer edges of the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex are growing at some of the fastest rates compared to other cities in the country.
label: OBJ

text: THE RED-STATE WINNERS IN THE CLIMB TO BECOME AMERICA’S NEXT ECONOMIC POWERHOUSE
label: SUBJ

text: The growth is reshaping not only the region’s economy, but also the political and cultural landscape of suburban Texas.
label: OBJ

text: These once-small, now-booming communities are gaining greater influence over schools, infrastructure and statewide priorities the more people relocate there.
label: OBJ

text: But the surge has also brought mounting pressure on roads, utilities and public services as cities like Celina race to keep up with demand.
label: OBJ

text: Tubbs said local leaders are trying to balance that growth by expanding infrastructure while continuing to attract more businesses and employers.
label: OBJ

text: "It definitely brings jobs, but it also brings a lot of challenges from an infrastructure standpoint, from water resources [and] road resources," Tubbs said.
label: OBJ

text: "But it brings a lot of opportunities."
label: OBJ

text: Much of the city’s workforce still commutes to nearby economic hubs like Frisco and McKinney, though officials hope new development will eventually allow more residents to work closer to home.
label: OBJ

text: Tubbs says that roughly 30% of residents work remotely, while the others commute to other North Texas cities.
label: OBJ

text: He also noted that the top employer in Celina is the school district with nearly 1,000 employees.
label: OBJ

text: Tubbs said the city hopes to preserve the sense of community that has long defined Celina, even as new residents continue pouring in.
label: OBJ

text: "If I was giving a recommendation for anybody else that's moving to Texas, it's just get involved," Tubbs said.
label: OBJ

text: "Give back and see how much you get back from the community."
label: SUBJ

text: This is part five of a series examining the challenges confronting the NATO alliance.
label: OBJ

text: For more than three decades, the U.S. carried the largest share of NATO's military burden while many European allies spent far less on defense than Washington wanted.
label: OBJ

text: The imbalance survived the Cold War, multiple U.S. administrations and repeated debates over burden sharing.
label: OBJ

text: Only in recent years — following Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine and renewed pressure from President Donald Trump — have many NATO members begun significantly increasing defense spending.
label: OBJ

text: So why did the gap persist for so long?
label: SUBJ

text: Defense analysts say the answer lies in a combination of post-Cold War optimism, domestic political priorities and an American defense umbrella that convinced much of Europe it could safely spend less on defense without sacrificing its security.
label: OBJ

text: "For much of the post–Cold War period, it is fair to say that Europeans underinvested in defense, partly because threats were low, and partly because a series of U.S. presidents did everything they could to convince Europeans that we would stay there forever," Barry Posen, a professor of political science at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, told [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: The collapse of the Soviet Union reinforced that mindset.
label: OBJ

text: With the primary threat NATO had been created to deter suddenly gone, governments across Europe moved to collect a so-called "peace dividend," redirecting resources toward domestic priorities and away from their militaries.
label: SUBJ

text: Between 1992 and 1999, defense spending among European NATO members fell 22%, helping establish a pattern of underinvestment that would persist for decades even as the United States maintained troops in Europe and continued serving as NATO's ultimate security backstop.
label: SUBJ

text: As defense spending declined, many European governments expanded or maintained social welfare systems that consumed a growing share of public budgets.
label: OBJ

text: Programs such as healthcare, pensions and higher education became deeply embedded in domestic politics, often making them harder to cut than military spending.
label: OBJ

text: With the U.S. continuing to provide the bulk of NATO's military power, many governments faced little immediate pressure to reverse course.
label: OBJ

text: Critics of the alliance's spending imbalance argued that American taxpayers were effectively subsidizing Europe's security, allowing allies to devote a larger share of public resources to domestic priorities.
label: OBJ

text: The result was what some defense analysts describe as a "moral hazard" problem: because the U.S. commitment to NATO was viewed as ironclad, allies could spend less on their own militaries without facing the full consequences of those decisions.
label: OBJ

text: NATO CHIEF WARNS EUROPE CAN’T DEFEND ITSELF WITHOUT US AS TENSIONS RISE OVER GREENLAND
label: OBJ

text: Over time, that dynamic became self-reinforcing.
label: OBJ

text: As European militaries shrank, many allies grew increasingly dependent on American capabilities ranging from logistics and intelligence to missile defense, strategic airlift and nuclear deterrence.
label: OBJ

text: "We are still having a strong, conventional U.S. presence in Europe," NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte said earlier in 2026, "and, of course, the nuclear umbrella as our ultimate guarantor."
label: OBJ

text: American frustration over burden sharing is nearly as old as NATO itself.
label: OBJ

text: In 1953, President Dwight D. Eisenhower warned European allies that "the American well can run dry" and pressed them to assume a larger share of the alliance's defense burden.
label: OBJ

text: The issue resurfaced repeatedly over the following decades as successive administrations sought greater European contributions to collective defense.
label: OBJ

text: The concern persisted long after the Cold War.
label: OBJ

text: In a blunt 2011 farewell speech in Brussels, then-War Secretary Robert Gates warned of a "dim if not dismal future" for NATO if European governments continued underinvesting in their militaries.
label: OBJ

text: Gates cautioned that there would be "dwindling appetite and patience" among American lawmakers and taxpayers to bear a disproportionate share of the alliance's defense costs.
label: OBJ

text: Yet despite decades of warnings, the underlying incentives changed little.
label: SUBJ

text: Washington repeatedly reaffirmed its commitment to NATO and maintained a large military presence on the continent, reducing pressure on allies to rapidly increase defense spending.
label: OBJ

text: "Every administration has been pushing allies to spend more money on their own defense," former Deputy Assistant Secretary of Defense for Europe and NATO Jim Townsend told [OUTLET].
label: OBJ

text: The issue gained renewed urgency after Russia's annexation of Crimea in 2014, when NATO established a benchmark for members to spend at least 2% of GDP on defense.
label: OBJ

text: While spending gradually increased, progress remained uneven across the alliance.
label: OBJ

text: "Nations slowly began going to that.
label: OBJ

text: But it's been slow," Townsend said.
label: OBJ

text: For years, burden-sharing disputes followed a familiar pattern: American officials urged allies to spend more, European leaders promised improvements and NATO continued to rely heavily on American military power.
label: OBJ

text: What finally broke that cycle, Townsend said, was the combination of Russia's growing aggression and Trump's willingness to challenge assumptions that had shaped the alliance for decades.
label: OBJ

text: "What really woke everyone up were two things," Townsend said.
label: OBJ

text: "One was the 2022 invasion by Putin the second time.
label: OBJ

text: And then the second was Trump."
label: OBJ

text: Unlike previous presidents, Trump openly questioned whether the United States should defend allies that failed to meet defense spending commitments.
label: OBJ

text: During his first term and again during his return to office, Trump argued that NATO members were taking advantage of American taxpayers and suggested U.S. protection should not be unconditional.
label: OBJ

text: Whether European leaders viewed Trump's approach as pressure, a warning or a negotiating tactic, it altered assumptions that had shaped the alliance since the end of the Cold War and accelerated a debate that had simmered for decades.
label: OBJ

text: The shift culminated at NATO's summit in The Hague, where allies agreed to a new goal of spending 5% of GDP on defense and defense-related investments by 2035.
label: OBJ

text: The agreement marked a dramatic leap from NATO's long-standing 2% benchmark and reflected a growing consensus that the alliance faced a far more dangerous security environment than the one that emerged after the Soviet Union's collapse.
label: SUBJ

text: The agreement also signaled that many allies had come to the same conclusion American presidents had voiced for decades: the post-Cold War era of reduced military spending was over.
label: OBJ

text: TRUMP PUSHED NATO TO SPEND BIG — NOW COMES THE HARDER QUESTION: CAN EUROPE ACTUALLY FIGHT?
label: SUBJ

text: But analysts caution that rebuilding military power is far more complicated than increasing budgets.
label: OBJ

text: Europe remains dependent on the U.S. for capabilities ranging from air defense and logistics to intelligence and defense industrial capacity, Townsend said.
label: OBJ

text: Even as governments commit more money to defense, translating those investments into military readiness will take years.
label: OBJ

text: John Byrne of Concerned Veterans for America said the challenge extends beyond equipment and spending levels.
label: OBJ

text: "They don’t have the experience," Byrne told [OUTLET], referring to the decades in which large multinational military commands were overwhelmingly led by American officers.
label: OBJ

text: Running large coalition military operations requires years of institutional knowledge and leadership experience, he said — something that cannot be rebuilt overnight.
label: OBJ

text: "You can buy equipment," Byrne said.
label: OBJ

text: "You can’t instantly buy command experience."
label: OBJ

text: The doctor recommended a low-dose aspirin, as Mr. Trump has admitted that he takes more than recommended.
label: OBJ

text: Photos of bruising on his hand have circulated since he came back to the White House for a second term.
label: OBJ

text: They show makeup covering the bruise.
label: OBJ

text: Mr. Trump’s slight lower leg swelling shows improvement from last year, Capt. Barbabella said.
label: OBJ

text: The president was diagnosed with chronic venous insufficiency last summer after being spotted with swollen ankles and legs.
label: OBJ

text: The doctor also touched on the president’s weight, noting preventive counseling was provided, including guidance on diet, increased physical activity and continued weight loss.
label: OBJ

text: At 6 feet 3 inches and 238 pounds, Mr. Trump’s Body Mass Index is 29.7.
label: OBJ

text: This technically places him in the “overweight” category, sitting right below the threshold for clinical obesity, which begins at a BMI of 30.0.
label: OBJ

text: He gained 14 pounds since his medical exam in April 2025.
label: OBJ

text: He is also on two medications for cholesterol control, Capt. Barbabella noted.
label: OBJ

text: Mr. Trump was “within normal limits” on the Montreal Cognitive Assessment, a screening tool to detect mild cognitive impairment and early signs of dementia, scoring a 30 out of 30.
label: OBJ

text: His cardiac age was estimated to be 14 years younger than his chronological age.
label: OBJ

text: This is his third checkup since returning to the White House last year.
label: OBJ

text: Mr. Trump, the oldest president ever inaugurated, will turn 80 on June 14.
label: OBJ

text: An Arizona school board member is facing calls to resign after appearing to make a Nazi salute during a contentious public meeting before later comparing the board president to a dictator and saying, "All I could think of tonight was Hitler."
label: OBJ

text: Video from a May 26 Deer Valley Unified School District Governing Board meeting appears to show board member Kimberly Fisher raising her right arm and saying, "Heil, heil" during a dispute with board President Paul Carver Jr.
label: OBJ

text: The exchange occurred near the end of the meeting during a disagreement over scheduling a community study session related to district boundary discussions.
label: OBJ

text: According to video of the meeting, Fisher objected to holding the session during the afternoon, arguing that community members would have difficulty attending.
label: OBJ

text: "The whole point of having a study session with our community is that we can get their input and they can hear our discussions," Fisher said during the meeting.
label: OBJ

text: Carver later said he moved to adjourn the meeting because the discussion involved an item that was not on the posted agenda and could have raised concerns under Arizona's Open Meeting Law.
label: OBJ

text: "The reason for calling for the adjournment was simply that, as the question turned into discussion concerning an item that was not on the agenda, the board was moving into an area that could have been considered a violation of Arizona's Open Meeting Law," Carver said in a Facebook video posted after the meeting.
label: OBJ

text: Carver said Fisher made the gesture and comment after the motion to adjourn.
label: OBJ

text: "The point behind this post is that there's a lot of noise being made that she may have been justified in making that statement because she felt like I was being a dictator," Carver said.
label: OBJ

text: "I was simply following the rules of the state of Arizona."
label: OBJ

text: He added that "it is never okay to make those gestures and make that statement with those gestures in any environment."
label: OBJ

text: The incident prompted condemnation from district officials, who said Fisher was acting independently and did not represent the views of the district.
label: OBJ

text: "The District does not condone, support, or endorse gestures or language associated with hate, discrimination, intimidation or violence in any form," Deer Valley Unified School District said in a statement.
label: OBJ

text: "Such actions do not reflect the mission or vision of Deer Valley Unified School District."
label: OBJ

text: The district added that Fisher's "views and actions do not reflect and should not be attributed to other board members, staff, other members of the school community or the District."
label: OBJ

text: TENNESSEE SCHOOL BOARD MEMBER CENSURED AFTER CALLING STUDENT ‘HOT’ AT MEETING
label: OBJ

text: The Deer Valley Educators Association also condemned Fisher's conduct and called for her resignation.
label: OBJ

text: "DVEA was horrified and disgusted to see DVUSD Governing Board Member Kimberly Fisher deliver a Nazi salute during the Tuesday, May 26, 2026, board meeting," association president Kelley Fisher said in a statement.
label: OBJ

text: "Any leader who uses a Nazi salute during a School Board meeting is unfit for public service.
label: SUBJ

text: There is no justification for this behavior.
label: SUBJ

text: Kimberly Fisher should resign before she does more harm to our students and the community at large."
label: SUBJ

text: Hours after the meeting, Fisher posted a Facebook livestream in which she doubled down on her criticism of Carver, repeatedly describing his leadership as dictatorial.
label: OBJ

text: "We have been living or operating under virtually a dictatorship for a long time," Fisher said.
label: OBJ

text: She also accused Carver of acting like "a dictator" and urged voters not to support him in future elections.
label: OBJ

text: Near the end of the livestream, Fisher appeared to connect her thinking during the meeting to historical dictators. "What was it?
label: SUBJ

text: "What was it? Pol Pot, you know, was the most egregious dictator I've heard of," Fisher said.
label: OBJ

text: "All I could think of tonight was Hitler."
label: OBJ

text: Fisher did not directly address the gesture or comment from the meeting during the livestream.
label: OBJ

text: In a separate video posted to social media, Carver said community members had asked why the board was not taking action against Fisher.
label: OBJ

text: He argued Arizona law limits the ability of school boards to discipline elected members.
label: OBJ

text: "I need the community to understand that in the state of Arizona, the school district and the board do not have the ability to discipline board members," Carver said.
label: OBJ

text: Carver called Fisher's behavior "rampant and repetitive" and said her actions were "totally unacceptable and unprofessional."
label: OBJ

text: Deer Valley Unified School District serves more than 33,000 students across northern Maricopa County, including communities in north Phoenix, Glendale, Peoria, Cave Creek and New River, according to the district.
label: OBJ

text: [OUTLET] reached out to Fisher for comment.
label: OBJ

text: ATLANTA — The U.S. Department of Justice is asking a judge to recuse herself in a fight over Georgia election records, arguing that she attended an event honoring Fulton County District Attorney Fani Willis, who prosecuted President Donald Trump, raising questions about the judge’s ability to be impartial.
label: OBJ

text: A federal judge in 11th Judicial Circuit received a “private reprimand” after a court investigation found that the judge had sex in the courthouse with a high-ranking uniformed police officer within earshot of staff, attended a partisan event and then initially lied to deny the allegations.
label: OBJ

text: The court’s investigation did not publicly identify the judge or the court location within the 11th Circuit’s jurisdiction, which includes Alabama, Florida and Georgia.
label: OBJ

text: The Justice Department is relying on media reports that identify U.S. District Judge Eleanor Ross in Atlanta as the judge in question.
label: OBJ

text: The Associated Press has not independently confirmed the judge’s identity.
label: OBJ

text: A person who answered the phone in Ross’ chambers Friday said the judge was unavailable and referred questions about the allegations to the court’s media office which said, “Judge Ross has no comment right now.”
label: OBJ

text: The media office did not immediately respond Saturday to a second email seeking comment about the Justice Department motion seeking Ross’ recusal.
label: OBJ

text: Federal judges are appointed for life but can be subject to disciplinary action, including censure, public or private reprimands and temporary withholding of cases.
label: OBJ

text: They can only be removed through impeachment by Congress.
label: OBJ

text: Ross was nominated in January 2014 by then-President Barack Obama, a Democrat, and she was confirmed by the Senate in November of that year.
label: OBJ

text: She had previously served as a state court judge in DeKalb County, which includes a small part of the city of Atlanta, since 2011.
label: OBJ

text: Prior to taking the bench, she had worked as a state and federal prosecutor, mostly in Atlanta, for more than a decade.
label: OBJ

text: Ross is overseeing the election records case filed by the Justice Department against Georgia Secretary of State Brad Raffensperger.
label: OBJ

text: The Justice Department has sued multiple states seeking statewide voter lists.
label: OBJ

text: Raffensperger has said that Georgia law prohibits the release of voters’ confidential personal information unless certain qualifications are met and that the federal government hadn’t met those conditions.
label: OBJ

text: He has said that he sent the public part of the voter roll to the Justice Department in December.
label: OBJ

text: Ross has scheduled a hearing in the case for Wednesday, though the Justice Department has asked to delay that hearing because of its request for the judge to recuse herself.
label: OBJ

text: In the disciplinary case against the unnamed federal judge, the Judicial Council of the 11th Circuit chose in a February order to impose a private reprimand that kept the judge’s name secret.
label: OBJ

text: The Committee on Judicial Conduct and Disability of the Judicial Conference of the United States on May 22 affirmed that order.
label: OBJ

text: An investigation report attached to the order says the judge went to an event hosted by a district attorney’s campaign.
label: OBJ

text: The judge acknowledged having gone to the event to visit with former colleagues in the district attorney’s office at a private mixer that was held in the same place but was separated from the prosecutor’s victory party.
label: OBJ

text: Ross previously worked in the Fulton County district attorney’s office and overlapped with Willis there before Willis was district attorney.
label: OBJ

text: Willis began investigating Trump and others for possible interference in the 2020 election in Fulton County soon after becoming district attorney in January 2021.
label: OBJ

text: Among the things she looked at was a January 2021 phone call in which Trump urged Raffensperger to help “find” the votes needed to overturn Democrat Joe Biden’s win in Georgia in the 2020 presidential election.
label: OBJ

text: Willis in August 2023 obtained an indictment against Trump and 18 others, accusing them of participating in a wide-ranging scheme to overturn Georgia’s 2020 election results.
label: OBJ

text: That case was ultimately dismissed in November after an appeals court found an “appearance of impropriety” created by a romantic relationship Willis had with the outside lawyer she had hired to lead the prosecution.
label: OBJ

text: The Justice Department’s arguments
label: OBJ

text: “A judge who attended a party celebrating the election of a Democrat best known for prosecuting a Republican President for alleged election interference cannot then preside over a case concerning that President’s efforts to ensure election integrity,” Justice Department lawyers wrote in their filing Friday.
label: OBJ

text: The Justice Department argued that any “objective reasonable observer” would see Ross’ presence at Willis’ election night party as an endorsement of her election and her actions in office.
label: OBJ

text: “If Judge Ross is indeed the Subject Judge, that conduct gives rise to an appearance of bias, which requires Judge Ross to recuse herself from this election-related case,” the Justice Department filing says.
label: OBJ

text: The Justice Department filing makes passing mention of the allegations of improper sexual activity with a police officer in the judge’s chambers and the subsequent false statements the judge made to deny those allegations, but says “those are not the subject of this Motion.”
label: OBJ
